topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parboiling and Drying, Drying: Moisture Content and Water Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Parboiling — The Hydrothermal Heart of Paddy Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAGT601, Module 2):**
> **Parboiling** is a hydrothermal pre-milling treatment given to paddy (rough rice) in which the starch granules within the endosperm are gelatinized by a controlled sequence of **soaking → steaming → drying** before dehusking and polishing. It converts raw paddy into **parboiled rice** (also called *ukda chawal*, *converted rice*, or *boiled rice*).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge That Gets Cooked Before It Dries"**

Imagine a raw rice grain as a tiny **hard, translucent marble** made of tightly packed starch crystals. If you try to mill (dehusk and polish) this marble, the grain breaks easily — the kernels fracture, you lose flour as a powder, and the rice cooks into a sticky, pasty mass.

Now imagine soaking that marble in warm water for several hours. Water seeps inside, the starch swells like a **sponge absorbing soup**, and then you steam it. The heat causes the starch granules to **burst, swell, and re-cement into a hard, glassy, amber-colored mass**. When you dry this cooked, hardened grain and then mill it, the grain is **tough, doesn't break, and yields whole, separate, fluffy rice after cooking**.

That whole transformation — water soaking + steam cooking + drying — is **parboiling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** Parboiling is NOT boiling rice for consumption. It is an **industrial pre-milling treatment** done on *paddy with husk intact*. The husk is later removed mechanically. The rice is then consumed as parboiled rice.

### The Three Sequential Operations of Parboiling

| Stage | Operation | Purpose | Typical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Soaking** | Submerging paddy in water at elevated temperature | Hydrate starch to ~30% MC (w.b.); fill voids | 65–75 °C for 3–4 hrs (hot soak) |
| **2. Steaming** | Exposing soaked paddy to saturated steam | Gelatinize starch (irreversible swelling); kill microflora; seal cracks | 100–120 °C for 10–20 min |
| **3. Drying** | Reducing moisture to safe storage level | Reduce MC to ~12–14% (w.b.) for storage stability | Sun drying or mechanical drying (40–60 °C) |

---

## 1.2 Drying — The Universal Post-Harvest Preservation Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **Drying** is the process of moisture removal from a biological product (grain, fruit, vegetable, seed) by simultaneous **heat transfer TO the product** and **mass transfer (water vapor) AWAY from the product**, accomplished by creating a vapour pressure gradient between the product interior and the surrounding air.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Towel on a Sunny Window"**

When you hang a wet towel near a sunny window, three things happen together:
1. **Sun's heat warms the water** inside the towel (heat transfer in)
2. **Water molecules gain enough energy to escape** as vapor (mass transfer out)
3. **The dry air around the towel sweeps the vapor away** so more vapor can leave (convective removal)

If you increase the sun's heat, or move the towel to drier air (lower humidity), or blow air across it (higher velocity) — the towel dries **faster**.

Drying of grains follows the **exact same principle**: warm dry air flowing over wet grain causes water to evaporate out. The drying rate depends on **air temperature, air humidity, air velocity, and the grain's internal moisture diffusion rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** Drying is the **most energy-intensive** operation in post-harvest processing. For every **1 kg of water removed**, approximately **2250 kJ (≈ 540 kcal)** of latent heat must be supplied (latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**).

---

## 1.3 Moisture Content — How Much Water is in the Grain?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **Moisture Content (MC)** is the quantitative measure of the amount of water present in a biological material, expressed either on a **wet basis (% wb)** or **dry basis (% db)**. It is one of the most critical parameters governing **storability, milling quality, germination, and shelf-life** of all agricultural produce.

**Two Bases — A Common Source of Confusion**

The same physical water content can be expressed two ways:

- **Wet Basis (MC_wb):** Water expressed as % of the **total (wet) weight** of the material.
- **Dry Basis (MC_db):** Water expressed as % of the **dry matter** weight of the material.

MC_db is always **numerically larger** than MC_wb. For example, paddy at **14% wb** corresponds to **16.3% db**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Think of MC_wb as "water per total glass volume" and MC_db as "water per solid ice cube". The dry basis stays the same regardless of how much water you add or remove — useful in drying calculations.

---

## 1.4 Water Activity — The "Available Water" That Microbes Care About

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **Water Activity (a_w)** is the ratio of the **partial pressure of water vapor above the product (p)** to the **partial pressure of pure water at the same temperature (p°)**. It is a measure of the **availability of water** for microbial growth, enzymatic reactions, and chemical deterioration — *not* the total amount of water present.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p°} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$

where **ERH = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (%)** of the air in equilibrium with the product at a given temperature.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Water in a Sponge vs Water in a Sealed Plastic Bag"**

Imagine two sponges, both weighing 1 kg (same total MC). One is a **regular kitchen sponge** with water freely oozing out. The other is a sponge dipped in **honey** — water is locked inside, sticky, not freely available.

A bacterium placed on **Sponge 1** finds free water and grows rapidly.
A bacterium placed on **Sponge 2** finds no free water and cannot grow — even though both sponges contain the same **total** water.

**Water activity quantifies this "free water"** — Sponge 1 has a_w ≈ 1.0, Sponge 2 has a_w ≈ 0.6.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** Microbial growth requires a_w ≥ 0.91 (most bacteria), a_w ≥ 0.88 (most yeasts), a_w ≥ 0.70 (most moulds). Dried foods with a_w < 0.6 are microbially **stable**.

---

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter Together — The KTU Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Parboiling-Drying-Moisture-Water Activity Chain:**
> 1. Parboiling **adds water** to paddy (soaking → ~30% wb, steaming → ~38% wb).
> 2. Drying **removes that water** back down to safe storage level (~12–14% wb).
> 3. The **final moisture content** determines whether the rice is safe to store.
> 4. The **final water activity** determines whether moulds, insects, and bacteria can attack it.
> 5. Understanding the **sorption isotherm** (EMC vs ERH) lets us predict **storage stability** without actually waiting for spoilage.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Parboiling — Detailed Process Theory

### 2.1.1 Physico-Chemical Changes During Parboiling

When paddy undergoes the **soaking → steaming → drying** sequence, the following irreversible changes occur inside the endosperm:

1. **Water absorption** — Dry paddy (~12% wb) absorbs water through the husk and pericarp, primarily via the **micropyle** (tiny opening). Hot water (60–75 °C) accelerates diffusion (Fickian diffusion through capillary pores).
2. **Starch gelatinization** — At ~68–78 °C (gelatinization temperature of rice starch), starch granules absorb water, swell 5–10× their volume, lose birefringence, and the crystalline structure becomes amorphous.
3. **Amylose leaching** — Soluble amylose leaches out of swollen granules and **fills the inter-granular voids**, cementing the grain.
4. **Protein denaturation** — Glutelin proteins unfold and form a network that further binds the swollen starch.
5. **Enzyme inactivation** — Lipase, peroxidase, and other enzymes are inactivated by heat — **extends shelf life**.
6. **Colour development** — Maillard reaction between reducing sugars and free amino groups produces the characteristic **amber/light yellow colour** of parboiled rice.
7. **Fissure sealing** — Micro-cracks present in raw rice are "healed" by gelatinized starch — **higher head rice yield** during milling.
8. **Hardening** — After drying, the gelatinized starch forms a **glassy, vitreous endosperm** that resists breakage.

### 2.1.2 Parboiling Methods (KTU Important)

| Method | Procedure | Temperature | Soaking Time | Steaming | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Traditional (single-boiling)** | Soak 2–3 days in cold/ambient water → boil in same water 15–30 min → sun dry | 25–30 °C soak | 48–72 hrs | Boiling in soak water | Uneven, dark colour, off-odour |
| **CFTRI Method (Central Food Tech., Mysore)** | Soak in hot water at 70 °C for 3 hrs → steam at 100 °C for 15 min → dry | 70 °C soak | 3 hrs | 100 °C, 15 min | Uniform, light colour, high HRY |
| **Pressure Parboiling** | Soak at 70 °C, 3 hrs → steam at **3–5 kg/cm²** (gauge) for 10 min | High-pressure steam | 3 hrs | 3–5 kg/cm² | Very hard grain, dark amber |
| **Dry-Heat Parboiling** | Soak briefly → apply dry heat (140–160 °C) for 15–30 min | 140–160 °C | 1–2 hrs | Dry heat | Lower colour, faster |
| **Malek Process (continuous)** | Counter-current hot water soak → steaming → drying | 70–75 °C soak | Continuous | Continuous | Industrial scale |

### 2.1.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of Parboiling (High-Yield for KTU)

**Advantages:**
- **Higher Head Rice Yield (HRY):** 65–70% vs 50–55% in raw rice — because the hardened grain resists breakage during dehusking/polishing.
- **Improved Nutritional Profile:** Vitamin B₁ (thiamine) from the husk/pericarp **migrates inward** to the endosperm during steaming — parboiled rice contains **2–3× more thiamine** than raw rice.
- **Reduced Breakage:** Gelatinized starch acts as a binder.
- **Insect Resistance:** Hardened grain is harder for storage insects to attack.
- **Reduced Oily/Free Fatty Acid Development:** Heat inactivates lipase enzyme that hydrolyses rice bran oil.
- **Better Cooking Quality:** Rice grains remain **separate and non-sticky** after cooking (preferred in South India, Middle East, Bangladesh).
- **Longer Shelf Life:** Moisture redistribution is uniform.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Extra Energy & Cost:** Soaking + steaming + drying = ~3× the energy of raw rice processing.
- **Darker Colour:** Maillard browning reduces consumer appeal in some markets.
- **Longer Cooking Time:** Hardened endosperm takes ~20 min to cook vs ~12 min for raw rice.
- **Drying Load:** Moisture must be brought from ~38% wb back down to ~14% wb — significant drying cost.

---

## 2.2 Drying — Detailed Process Theory

### 2.2.1 Mechanism of Drying — Two Periods

When a wet grain is exposed to a stream of hot dry air, drying proceeds in **two distinct periods** as visualized in the classic **drying rate curve (dX/dt vs X or vs t)**:

**Period I — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)**
- Surface of grain is **completely saturated with free water**.
- Drying rate is controlled by **external conditions**: air temperature, humidity, velocity.
- Rate is essentially **constant** — analogous to evaporating water from a free surface.
- The grain surface behaves like a **free water surface**.

**Transition Point — Critical Moisture Content (X_c)**
- At this point, surface water film starts becoming discontinuous.
- Dry patches appear on the surface.

**Period II — Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP)**
- Drying rate is controlled by **internal moisture diffusion** (capillary, liquid, vapour diffusion).
- Rate **decreases continuously** as MC drops.
- Most of the drying time of agricultural grains occurs in FRDP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Fact:** For most grains, the **CRDP is very short or absent** because grains are porous but water is held internally by capillary forces. Most of the drying happens in the **falling rate period** governed by Fickian diffusion.

### 2.2.2 Modes of Heat Transfer in Drying
1. **Conduction** — Hot surface in contact with grain (e.g., contact dryer).
2. **Convection** — Hot air blowing over grain (most common — fluidized bed, LSU dryer).
3. **Radiation** — Infrared or solar radiation (solar dryers).
4. **Dielectric / Microwave** — Internal heat generation (microwave drying).

### 2.2.3 Types of Grain Dryers (KTU Expected Table)

| Type | Example | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Sun drying** | Open yard | Small farms, traditional |
| **Solar dryer** | Tunnel solar, cabinet solar | Remote areas, low cost |
| **Batch dryer** | Flatbed, batch LSU | Small to medium scale |
| **Continuous flow dryer** | LSU (Louisiana State University) dryer, column dryer | Large scale |
| **Fluidized bed dryer** | Vibrated fluid bed | Seeds, grains, gentle drying |
| **Rotary dryer** | Horizontal rotating drum | Granular materials |
| **Spray dryer** | Atomization in hot air | Liquids (milk, extracts) |
| **Drum dryer** | Rotating heated drum | Pastes, purees |
| **Tray/Cabinet dryer** | Multi-tray cabinet | Fruits, vegetables |
| **Freeze dryer (Lyophilizer)** | Vacuum + sublimation | High-value products (herbs, mushroom) |

---

## 2.3 Moisture Content — The Complete Mathematical Framework

### 2.3.1 Two Bases of Moisture Content

Let **W_w** = mass of water removed on drying, **W_d** = mass of dry matter (bone-dry weight), **W_t** = total wet mass.

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_t} \times 100 = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100 \quad (\%) \text{ wet basis}$$

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100 \quad (\%) \text{ dry basis}$$

### 2.3.2 Conversion Between Bases (Must-Memorize Formula)

$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

### 2.3.3 Methods of Moisture Determination (KTU Expected Knowledge)

| Method | Principle | Time | Accuracy | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oven drying (standard)** | 105 °C for 24 hrs (grains), 70 °C vacuum for oil seeds | Hours | Highest (reference) | Low |
| **Brown-Duvel distillation** | Distil water with toluene/xylene, measure volume | 30 min | High | Medium |
| **Infrared drying** | IR lamp, 5–15 min | Minutes | Good | Low |
| **Halogen moisture analyser** | Halogen heater, 5–20 min | Minutes | Good | Medium |
| **Capacitance/Resistance meter** | Electrical property change | Seconds | ±0.5–1% | Low |
| **NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance)** | Hydrogen nuclei resonance | Seconds | Very high | Very high |
| **Karl Fischer titration** | Chemical reaction with iodine/SO₂ | Minutes | Highest for trace water | High |
| **Near-Infrared (NIR)** | Spectral absorption | Seconds | High (with calibration) | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reference Standard:** The **air-oven method at 105 ± 1 °C for 24 hours** is the **official reference method** for grains as per **AOAC / AACC / FSSAI / BIS** standards. For oil seeds (high oil content), **vacuum oven at 70 °C for 16–24 hrs** is preferred to prevent oil oxidation.

---

## 2.4 Water Activity — The Complete Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Fundamental Definition

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p°(T)}$$

- **p** = partial pressure of water vapour above the food surface at equilibrium.
- **p°(T)** = saturated vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature.
- Dimensionless, ranges **0 to 1**.
- Pure water: a_w = 1.000.
- Bone-dry material: a_w ≈ 0.000.

### 2.4.2 Sorption Isotherm — The Master Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Sorption Isotherm:** A plot of **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC, % db)** on the y-axis versus **Water Activity (a_w)** or **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH %)** on the x-axis, at a constant temperature.

The curve is **sigmoid-shaped (S-shaped)** and consists of three regions:

| Region | a_w Range | Water Binding | Microbial Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Region I — Bound water monolayer** | 0.00 – 0.25 | Strongly bound to polar sites; not available | No microbial growth |
| **Region II — Multilayer water** | 0.25 – 0.70 | Weakly bound, multimolecular layers | Moulds may grow above 0.70 |
| **Region III — Free water (condensed)** | 0.70 – 1.00 | Bulk-like, capillary condensed | Bacteria, yeasts, moulds grow actively |

### 2.4.3 Sorption Isotherm Models (KTU Expected Knowledge)

**Langmuir Model** (single monolayer, monolayer adsorption):
$$\frac{a_w}{X} = \frac{1}{X_m \cdot b} + \frac{a_w}{X_m}$$

where **X** = moisture content (db), **X_m** = monolayer moisture content, **b** = constant related to heat of sorption.

**BET Model (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller)** (multilayer, the most cited isotherm equation):
$$\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X} = \frac{1}{X_m C} + \frac{(C-1)a_w}{X_m C}$$

where **C** = constant related to heat of sorption of the monolayer.

**GAB Model (Guggenheim–Anderson–de Boer)** — most commonly used for foods (extends BET to higher a_w):

$$\frac{X}{X_m} = \frac{C \cdot K \cdot a_w}{(1 - K a_w)(1 - K a_w + C \cdot K \cdot a_w)}$$

where **C** = Guggenheim constant (related to monolayer heat of sorption), **K** = constant for multilayer heat of sorption.

**Henderson Model** (empirical, used in EMC–ERH prediction for grains):
$$1 - a_w = \exp\left(-C_1 \cdot T \cdot X^{C_2}\right)$$

where **C_1, C_2** = empirical constants, **T** = temperature (°C), **X** = moisture content (db).

**Oswin Model**:
$$X = A \left(\frac{a_w}{1 - a_w}\right)^B$$

**Halsey Model**:
$$a_w = \exp\left(-\frac{A}{X^B}\right)$$

### 2.4.4 Critical Water Activity Thresholds for Spoilage (High-Yield)

| a_w | Lower Limit for Growth |
|---|---|
| Most bacteria | 0.91 |
| Most yeasts | 0.88 |
| Most moulds | 0.70 |
| Halophilic bacteria | 0.75 |
| Xerophilic moulds | 0.65 |
| No microbial growth | < 0.60 |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** To make a food **microbiologically shelf-stable**, lower its a_w to **below 0.60**. Most dried grains at ~12–14% wb have a_w ≈ **0.55–0.70** at room temperature.

### 2.4.5 Determination of Water Activity

| Method | Principle |
|---|---|
| **Gravimetric / Hygrometric** | Place sample in sealed chamber with known salt solution; weigh until constant |
| **Dew point meter (AquaLab, Decagon)** | Measure dew point of headspace vapour; a_w = T_dew/T_sat |
| **Capacitance hygrometer** | Sensor capacitance changes with RH of headspace |
| **Electric hygrometer** | Resistance of LiCl sensor changes with RH |

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values are **standard units as per KTU 2024 Scheme**. Use **\vert** notation for absolute value where required to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$ | %, W in kg |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$ | %, dry basis is preferred for drying calculations |
| 3 | wb to db conversion | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}\times 100$ | — |
| 4 | db to wb conversion | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}}\times 100$ | — |
| 5 | Mass of water to remove | $W_w = W_{initial}\left(1 - \dfrac{1-MC_{wb,f}}{1-MC_{wb,i}}\right)$ | kg water per kg initial |
| 6 | Water activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p°(T)} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 7 | Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda \approx 2257$ kJ/kg | at 100 °C; ≈ 2400 kJ/kg at 70 °C |
| 8 | BET monolayer MC | $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w)X} = \dfrac{1}{X_mC} + \dfrac{(C-1)a_w}{X_mC}$ | linear plot gives X_m |
| 9 | GAB model | $\dfrac{X}{X_m} = \dfrac{C K a_w}{(1-K a_w)(1-K a_w + C K a_w)}$ | best fit for foods |
| 10 | Fick's law of diffusion | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | J = flux, D = diffusivity |
| 11 | EMC (Henderson) | $1-a_w = \exp(-C_1 T X^{C_2})$ | T in °C, X in % db |
| 12 | EMC (Oswin) | $X = A \left(\dfrac{a_w}{1-a_w}\right)^B$ | empirical |
| 13 | Critical a_w (bacteria) | 0.91 | spoilage threshold |
| 14 | Critical a_w (moulds) | 0.70 | spoilage threshold |
| 15 | Parboiling — starch gelatinization temp. | 68–78 °C | rice starch |
| 16 | Parboiled paddy final MC | 12–14 % wb | safe for storage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always show the **dry weight consistency** in moisture problems. Dry weight remains **constant** during drying; wet weight decreases. Always compute **dry matter first**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content Conversions (wb ↔ db)

**Given:**
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$$

**To derive wb from db:**

Start with the definition:
$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $W_d$:

$$\frac{MC_{db}}{100} = \frac{W_w/W_d}{1}$$

But we want $MC_{wb}$. Use:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $W_d$:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w/W_d}{W_w/W_d + 1}\times 100$$

Substitute $\dfrac{W_w}{W_d} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}$:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}/100}{MC_{db}/100 + 1}\times 100$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 100:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{db} + 100}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}}\times 100 \quad (\%)}$$

**To derive db from wb (reverse):**

Start with $MC_{wb}$:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_t}\times 100 \quad \text{where} \quad W_t = W_w + W_d$$

Divide by $W_d$:
$$\frac{MC_{wb}}{100} = \frac{W_w/W_d}{W_w/W_d + 1}$$

Let $y = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}$. Then:

$$\frac{MC_{wb}}{100} = \frac{y}{y+1}$$

Solve for y:
$$y\left(1 - \frac{MC_{wb}}{100}\right) = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100}$$

$$y = \frac{MC_{wb}/100}{1 - MC_{wb}/100} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{MC_{db}}{100} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}$$

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}\times 100 \quad (\%)}$$

**Verified Example:**
- Given: $MC_{wb} = 14\%$
- Then: $MC_{db} = \dfrac{14}{100-14}\times 100 = \dfrac{14}{86}\times 100 = 16.28\%$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Water to Remove During Drying

**Setup:** A batch of grain has initial wet mass $M_i$ at initial wet-basis MC $= M_{wb,i}$. After drying to final wet-basis MC $= M_{wb,f}$, the final wet mass $M_f$ contains the **same dry matter** (no dry matter is removed by drying).

**Mass balance — dry matter:**
$$M_d = M_i\left(1 - \frac{M_{wb,i}}{100}\right) = M_f\left(1 - \frac{M_{wb,f}}{100}\right)$$

**Mass of water to remove:**
$$W_w^{removed} = M_i - M_f$$

From mass balance:
$$M_f = M_i \cdot \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}/100}{1 - M_{wb,f}/100}$$

Therefore:
$$W_w^{removed} = M_i - M_i \cdot \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}/100}{1 - M_{wb,f}/100}$$

$$\boxed{W_w^{removed} = M_i \left[1 - \frac{100 - M_{wb,i}}{100 - M_{wb,f}}\right]}$$

**Energy required to remove that water:**
$$Q = W_w^{removed} \cdot \lambda$$

where $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (≈ **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C; for grain drying at ~50–80 °C, use $\lambda$ ≈ **2300–2400 kJ/kg**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BET Isotherm Linear Form (Step-by-Step)

The BET theory starts with the **assumption that gas (water vapour) molecules form successive layers on the surface**, with the heat of adsorption for the first layer being different from subsequent layers.

For the **multilayer adsorption** case, the BET equation is:

$$\frac{X}{X_m} = \frac{C \cdot a_w}{(1 - a_w)\left[1 + (C-1) a_w\right]}$$

**Step 1:** Take the reciprocal of both sides.

$$\frac{X_m}{X} = \frac{(1 - a_w)\left[1 + (C-1)a_w\right]}{C \cdot a_w}$$

**Step 2:** Expand the numerator.

$$\frac{X_m}{X} = \frac{1}{C a_w} - \frac{1}{C} + \frac{(C-1)a_w}{C a_w}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the last term.

$$\frac{(C-1) a_w}{C a_w} = \frac{C-1}{C} = 1 - \frac{1}{C}$$

So:

$$\frac{X_m}{X} = \frac{1}{C a_w} - \frac{1}{C} + 1 - \frac{1}{C}$$

$$\frac{X_m}{X} = \frac{1}{C a_w} + 1 - \frac{2}{C}$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $X_m$.

$$\frac{1}{X} = \frac{1}{X_m C a_w} + \frac{1}{X_m} - \frac{2}{X_m C}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{a_w}{1-a_w}$ to reach the **canonical BET linear form**:

$$\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X} = \frac{1}{X_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{X_m C} a_w$$

$$\boxed{\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X} = \frac{1}{X_m C} + \left(\frac{C-1}{X_m C}\right) a_w}$$

**How to find $X_m$ and $C$ experimentally:**

Plot $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X}$ on the **y-axis** versus $a_w$ on the **x-axis**.

The plot is **linear in the range** $0.05 \leq a_w \leq 0.35$.

- **Intercept** $= \dfrac{1}{X_m C}$
- **Slope** $= \dfrac{C-1}{X_m C}$

Then:

$$X_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$$

$$C = \frac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Pattern Problem

### Problem 3.4.1 — Moisture Conversion + Drying Mass Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A paddy lot weighing **1000 kg** arrives at a rice mill with an initial moisture content of **22% (wb)**. After parboiling (soaking + steaming) the MC becomes **38% (wb)**. The parboiled paddy is then dried back to **14% (wb)** for safe storage.

**Calculate:**
(a) The mass of dry matter in the lot.
(b) The mass of water that must be removed during drying.
(c) The final mass of dried parboiled paddy.
(d) The heat energy required for drying (using latent heat of vaporization $\lambda = 2400$ kJ/kg).
(e) Express the final MC of dried paddy on a **dry basis**.

---

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Dry matter mass (constant throughout drying):**

Using initial MC of 22% wb:

$$M_d = M_i \left(1 - \frac{MC_{wb,i}}{100}\right) = 1000 \times \left(1 - \frac{22}{100}\right)$$

$$M_d = 1000 \times 0.78 = 780 \text{ kg}$$

> Dry matter remains **780 kg** throughout.

**Step (b) — Mass of water to remove during drying:**

Drying takes the paddy from 38% wb to 14% wb.

Method 1 — Using mass balance:

Initial mass after steaming = ?

After soaking + steaming, mass increases because water is added. We need to find the post-steaming total mass.

Actually, the problem provides initial MC of raw paddy = 22% wb. After parboiling, MC = 38% wb. Dry matter remains 780 kg.

Total mass after steaming:
$$M_{steam} = \frac{M_d}{1 - MC_{wb,steam}/100} = \frac{780}{1 - 0.38} = \frac{780}{0.62}$$

$$M_{steam} = 1258.06 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of water at this stage:
$$W_w^{steam} = M_{steam} - M_d = 1258.06 - 780 = 478.06 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of water after drying to 14% wb:
$$M_{final} = \frac{M_d}{1 - MC_{wb,f}/100} = \frac{780}{1 - 0.14} = \frac{780}{0.86}$$

$$M_{final} = 906.98 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of water in dried paddy:
$$W_w^{final} = 906.98 - 780 = 126.98 \text{ kg}$$

**Water removed during drying:**
$$W_w^{removed} = W_w^{steam} - W_w^{final} = 478.06 - 126.98$$

$$\boxed{W_w^{removed} = 351.08 \text{ kg}}$$

Method 2 — Using direct formula:

$$W_w^{removed} = M_{steam} \left[1 - \frac{100 - M_{wb,i}}{100 - M_{wb,f}}\right] = 1258.06 \left[1 - \frac{100-38}{100-14}\right]$$

$$= 1258.06 \left[1 - \frac{62}{86}\right] = 1258.06 \times \frac{24}{86} = 1258.06 \times 0.2791$$

$$= 351.07 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches)}$$

**Step (c) — Final mass of dried parboiled paddy:**

$$\boxed{M_{final} = 906.98 \text{ kg} \approx 907 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step (d) — Heat energy required:**

$$Q = W_w^{removed} \times \lambda = 351.08 \times 2400$$

$$\boxed{Q = 842,592 \text{ kJ} \approx 842.6 \text{ MJ}}$$

In kcal (since some Indian textbooks still use):
$$Q = 351.08 \times \frac{2400}{4.184} \approx 201,400 \text{ kcal} \approx 2.014 \times 10^5 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step (e) — Final MC on dry basis:**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100 = \frac{14}{100 - 14} \times 100 = \frac{14}{86} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = 16.28\%}$$

---

### Problem 3.4.2 — Water Activity from Sorption Data (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of dried chilli flakes has an equilibrium moisture content of **12.5% (db)** when placed in a sealed chamber at 25 °C maintained at **75% RH** by a saturated NaCl solution. Calculate the **water activity** and comment on **microbiological stability**.

**Solution:**

By definition, the **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)** of the air in equilibrium with the sample = **RH of the chamber** = 75%.

$$\boxed{a_w = \frac{ERH}{100} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75}$$

**Microbiological interpretation:**

- Most bacteria: need a_w ≥ 0.91 → **CANNOT grow** (0.75 < 0.91) ✓
- Most yeasts: need a_w ≥ 0.88 → **CANNOT grow** ✓
- Most moulds: need a_w ≥ 0.70 → **CAN grow slowly** (0.75 > 0.70) ✗
- Xerophilic moulds: 0.65 → can grow

**Conclusion:** The chilli flakes at a_w = 0.75 are **safe from bacteria and yeasts**, but **mould growth is a risk** during long storage at high humidity. To ensure **complete microbial stability**, a_w should be brought **below 0.70** by further drying to ~10% db.

---

### Problem 3.4.3 — BET Monolayer Determination

**Problem Statement:**
The following data were obtained for wheat flour at 25 °C:

| a_w | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| X (% db) | 3.2 | 5.1 | 7.4 | 9.0 | 11.0 |

**Calculate the BET monolayer moisture content $X_m$ and constant $C$.**

**Solution:**

We compute $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X}$ for each data point:

**Point 1:** a_w = 0.05, X = 3.2:
$$\frac{0.05}{(1-0.05)\times 3.2} = \frac{0.05}{3.04} = 0.01645$$

**Point 2:** a_w = 0.10, X = 5.1:
$$\frac{0.10}{0.90 \times 5.1} = \frac{0.10}{4.59} = 0.02179$$

**Point 3:** a_w = 0.20, X = 7.4:
$$\frac{0.20}{0.80 \times 7.4} = \frac{0.20}{5.92} = 0.03378$$

**Point 4:** a_w = 0.30, X = 9.0:
$$\frac{0.30}{0.70 \times 9.0} = \frac{0.30}{6.30} = 0.04762$$

**Point 5:** a_w = 0.40, X = 11.0:
$$\frac{0.40}{0.60 \times 11.0} = \frac{0.40}{6.60} = 0.06061$$

Now do a **linear regression** of $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w)X}$ vs $a_w$ (using only points 1–4 in the BET range 0.05–0.35):

| a_w | y = a_w / [(1-a_w)X] |
|---|---|
| 0.05 | 0.01645 |
| 0.10 | 0.02179 |
| 0.20 | 0.03378 |
| 0.30 | 0.04762 |

Using two-point slope (extreme points):
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{0.04762 - 0.01645}{0.30 - 0.05} = \frac{0.03117}{0.25} = 0.12468$$

Intercept ≈ at $a_w = 0.05$: $0.01645 - 0.12468 \times 0.05 = 0.01645 - 0.00623 = 0.01022$

$$X_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{0.12468 + 0.01022} = \frac{1}{0.13490}$$

$$\boxed{X_m = 7.41\% \text{ db}}$$

$$C = \frac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1 = \frac{0.12468}{0.01022} + 1 = 12.20 + 1$$

$$\boxed{C = 13.20}$$

**Interpretation:** The monolayer moisture content of **7.41% db** corresponds to the moisture level at which **all polar sites on the starch/protein surface are covered by a single layer of water molecules**. Below $X_m$, water is so tightly bound that **no microbial or enzymatic activity** is possible — this is the **safest storage MC for wheat flour**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**
**Define parboiling of paddy. List any four advantages of parboiled rice over raw rice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Parboiling is a hydrothermal pre-milling treatment of paddy in which the grain is first **soaked in water at elevated temperature (60–75 °C)** to hydrate the endosperm, then **steamed at 100 °C or above** to gelatinize the starch, and finally **dried** to a safe moisture content (12–14% wb) before milling.

> **Any four advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks, but only 4 needed for 1.5 marks, pick best 4):**
> 1. **Higher head rice yield (65–70%)** compared to raw rice (50–55%) due to hardened endosperm.
> 2. **Improved nutritional value** — thiamine (Vitamin B₁) migrates from the husk/pericarp into the endosperm, increasing B₁ content by 2–3 times.
> 3. **Reduced breakage** during milling because the gelatinized starch binds the grain.
> 4. **Better storage stability** — lipase enzyme is inactivated, preventing rancidity of rice bran oil.
> 5. Cooking yields **separate, non-sticky grains** preferred in South Asia and the Middle East.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**
**Define water activity (a_w). State the critical a_w values below which bacteria, yeasts, and moulds cannot grow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Water activity (a_w) is defined as the **ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour above the food (p)** to the **saturated vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature (p°)**. Mathematically:
> $$a_w = \frac{p}{p°(T)} = \frac{ERH(\%)}{100}$$
> where ERH is the equilibrium relative humidity. It is dimensionless and ranges from 0 (bone dry) to 1 (pure water).

> **Critical a_w values (3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks):**
> - **Most bacteria** — cannot grow below **a_w = 0.91**
> - **Most yeasts** — cannot grow below **a_w = 0.88**
> - **Most moulds** — cannot grow below **a_w = 0.70**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **CFTRI method of parboiling of paddy**. List the advantages and disadvantages of parboiling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **moisture content on wet basis and dry basis**. Derive the relationship between them. A sample of wheat weighing **250 g** before drying weighs **220 g** after drying to a constant weight in a hot air oven. Calculate the **initial moisture content on both wet and dry basis**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION — Question A:**

**(a) CFTRI Method of Parboiling (7 marks):**

**Flow Diagram (4 marks — diagram + labelled steps):**

The Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore, developed a standardized parboiling process with the following steps:

1. **Cleaning**: Raw paddy is cleaned to remove stones, chaff, immature grains, and foreign matter using air-screen cleaners and gravity separators.
2. **Soaking**: Cleaned paddy is soaked in **hot water at 70 ± 2 °C for 3 to 4 hours** in a tank until the moisture content reaches **30–32% (wb)**. This hydrates the endosperm starch.
3. **Draining**: Soaked paddy is drained briefly to remove excess surface water.
4. **Steaming**: Drained paddy is exposed to **saturated steam at atmospheric pressure (100 °C) for 15–20 minutes** in a steaming chamber/boiler. This gelatinizes the starch.
5. **Drying**: Steamed paddy is dried to **12–14% (wb)** moisture content using either:
   - **Sun drying** (traditional) — 2–3 days
   - **Mechanical drying** — using LSU (Louisiana State University) dryers at 50–60 °C for 4–6 hours
6. **Tempering/conditioning**: Dried paddy is held for 24–48 hours to allow internal moisture equilibration — prevents stress cracking during milling.
7. **Milling**: Tempered paddy is dehusked, polished, and graded to obtain parboiled rice.

**Advantages of Parboiling (any 3 for remaining marks):**
- Increases **head rice yield** to 65–70% (vs 50–55% for raw rice).
- **Nutritional enhancement** — thiamine migrates from husk into endosperm (3× higher B₁).
- **Reduced breakage** during milling due to hardened endosperm.
- **Longer shelf life** — lipase enzyme inactivation prevents oil rancidity.
- Cooking gives **firm, separate, non-sticky grains**.
- **Insect resistant** storage due to hard grain texture.

**Disadvantages (any 2):**
- **Higher processing cost** due to extra soaking/steaming/drying energy.
- **Darker colour** due to Maillard browning — reduces consumer appeal.
- **Longer cooking time** for the consumer (20 min vs 12 min for raw rice).

---

**(b) Moisture Content Definitions + Conversion + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

**Moisture content on wet basis (MC_wb):** The mass of water expressed as a percentage of the **total (wet) mass** of the material:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**Moisture content on dry basis (MC_db):** The mass of water expressed as a percentage of the **dry matter (bone-dry mass)** of the material:
$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**Derivation of relationship (3 marks):**

Starting from:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$$

Let $R = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}$. Then $MC_{db} = R \times 100$, so $R = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}$.

For wet basis:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w/W_d}{W_w/W_d + 1}\times 100 = \frac{R}{R+1}\times 100$$

Substituting:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}/100}{MC_{db}/100 + 1}\times 100 = \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{db} + 100}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}}\times 100 \quad (\%)}$$

Similarly, solving back:
$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}\times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**

**Given:** Initial wet mass $W_i$ = 250 g, Final dry mass (after oven drying to constant weight) $W_d$ = 220 g.

Mass of water removed = $250 - 220 = 30$ g.

**Initial moisture content on wet basis:**
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100 = \frac{30}{250}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{wb} = 12\%}$$

**Initial moisture content on dry basis:**
$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100 = \frac{30}{220}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = 13.64\%}$$

**Cross-check using formula:**
$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100-MC_{wb}}\times 100 = \frac{12}{100-12}\times 100 = \frac{12}{88}\times 100 = 13.64\% \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Alternative Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is **drying**? Explain the **two-period drying theory** with a sketch of the drying rate curve. Discuss the **constant rate period** and **falling rate period** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **water activity**? Explain the **sorption isotherm** concept with a neatly labelled diagram. Describe the **BET and GAB models** for sorption isotherms. A food product has moisture content of **8.5% (db)** and water activity of **0.75** at 25 °C. Calculate the **BET monolayer moisture content** if the BET constant **C = 12.5**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION — Question B:**

**(a) Drying + Two-Period Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition of Drying (1 mark):**
Drying is the unit operation of removing moisture from a biological material by the simultaneous application of **heat** (to vaporize water) and **mass transfer** (to remove vapour), achieved by exposing the wet product to a stream of warm, dry air. The driving force is the **vapour pressure gradient** between the product surface and the bulk air.

**Two-Period Drying Theory (4 marks for explanation + 2 marks for sketch):**

When a wet solid is dried under **constant external air conditions** (constant T, RH, velocity), the drying rate $(dX/dt)$ plotted against either time $t$ or moisture content $X$ gives a characteristic curve showing **two distinct periods**:

**Period I — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP):**
- During this initial period, the surface of the grain is **completely saturated with free water** — it behaves like a free water surface.
- The rate of evaporation is **controlled entirely by external factors**: air temperature, air humidity, air velocity, and surface area.
- The drying rate remains **constant** because the surface remains saturated.
- The temperature of the wet surface stays near the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air (cooling effect of evaporation).
- For most grains, this period is **very short or practically absent** because water inside grains is held by capillary/bound forces — the surface is not a free water film.
- For materials like fruits, vegetables, and free water on cloth, this period is significant.

**Critical Moisture Content (X_c):**
- At the end of CRDP, the surface dries out partially — **dry patches** appear.
- This point is called the **critical moisture content (X_c)**.
- It marks the **transition** from external to internal mass transfer control.

**Period II — Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP):**
- After X_c, the drying rate **continuously decreases** as MC drops.
- Drying rate is now **controlled by internal moisture migration** mechanisms:
  - **Liquid diffusion** through capillaries,
  - **Vapour diffusion** through pores,
  - **Thermal diffusion** of vapour due to temperature gradients.
- FRDP may itself have **two sub-zones**: first falling rate (unsaturated surface drying) and second falling rate (internal diffusion controlled, where the plane of evaporation recedes into the solid).
- The grain surface temperature **rises** towards the dry-bulb temperature of the air.

**Drying Rate Curve (sketch description — full marks even without art):**

The drying rate curve is a plot of $dX/dt$ (y-axis) vs $X$ or $t$ (x-axis). It shows a horizontal line at the constant rate value until $X = X_c$, then a downward-sloping curve until $X = X_e$ (equilibrium moisture content), where $dX/dt = 0$.

**Significance (1 mark):**
- Most agricultural drying occurs in **FRDP** — designers must size dryers for the long falling-rate tail.
- Knowledge of $X_c$ helps decide whether to use high-temperature (suitable for CRDP) or low-temperature long-time drying (FRDP).

---

**(b) Water Activity + Sorption Isotherm + BET/GAB Models + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Water Activity Definition (1 mark):**
$$a_w = \frac{p}{p°(T)}$$
Water activity is the **ratio of partial pressure of water vapour above the food** to the **saturation vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature**. It is dimensionless and indicates the **availability** of water for microbial and chemical reactions.

**Sorption Isotherm (2 marks):**

A sorption isotherm is the **equilibrium relationship between the moisture content (EMC) of a product and its water activity (a_w) at a constant temperature**. The plot of **EMC (% db)** on the y-axis versus **a_w (or ERH %)** on the x-axis gives a characteristic **sigmoid (S-shaped) curve**.

The curve has three regions:
- **Region I (a_w = 0 to 0.25):** Monolayer water — tightly bound to polar sites; **no microbial growth**.
- **Region II (a_w = 0.25 to 0.70):** Multilayer water — less tightly bound; some mould risk above 0.65.
- **Region III (a_w = 0.70 to 1.0):** Free capillary water — freely available; **bacteria, yeasts, moulds all grow**.

Sorption isotherms are used to:
- Predict **storage stability** of dried foods.
- Select **packaging materials** (moisture barrier requirements).
- Calculate **drying time** and energy.
- Design **controlled moisture foods** (intermediate moisture foods, IMF).

**BET Model (2 marks):**

The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) model assumes **multilayer adsorption** of water vapour on the surface of the food. The linear form is:
$$\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X} = \frac{1}{X_m C} + \frac{(C-1)a_w}{X_m C}$$

The plot of $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w) X}$ vs $a_w$ is linear in the range $0.05 \leq a_w \leq 0.35$. From the slope and intercept, $X_m$ and $C$ are determined. $X_m$ represents the **monolayer moisture content** — the safest storage MC.

**GAB Model (1 mark):**

The Guggenheim–Anderson–de Boer (GAB) model extends the BET equation to higher a_w (up to 0.9) and is **the most accurate for foods**:
$$\frac{X}{X_m} = \frac{C \cdot K \cdot a_w}{(1 - K a_w)(1 - K a_w + C \cdot K \cdot a_w)}$$

where $X_m$ = monolayer MC, $C$ = Guggenheim constant, $K$ = multilayer constant. It is the recommended model for predicting EMC in the full range relevant to food storage.

**Numerical (1 mark):**

**Given:** X = 8.5% db, a_w = 0.75, C = 12.5. Find X_m.

Using the BET equation:
$$\frac{X}{X_m} = \frac{C \cdot a_w}{(1 - a_w)\left[1 + (C-1) a_w\right]}$$

$$\frac{8.5}{X_m} = \frac{12.5 \times 0.75}{(1 - 0.75)\left[1 + (12.5-1) \times 0.75\right]}$$

$$= \frac{9.375}{0.25 \times \left[1 + 11.5 \times 0.75\right]}$$

$$= \frac{9.375}{0.25 \times \left[1 + 8.625\right]}$$

$$= \frac{9.375}{0.25 \times 9.625}$$

$$= \frac{9.375}{2.40625}$$

$$\frac{8.5}{X_m} = 3.8961$$

$$X_m = \frac{8.5}{3.8961} = 2.182\%$$

$$\boxed{X_m = 2.18\% \text{ db}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Wet vs Dry Basis Confusion (loses 2–3 marks):**
> Students often forget to specify whether MC is on wb or db. Always write **"MC = 14% (wb)"** not just "14%". In numericals, **never** use wb MC for drying-rate calculations — **always convert to db** first.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting to Use Dry Matter Constant (loses 3 marks):**
> In drying-mass-balance problems, students mistakenly assume wet mass stays constant. **The dry mass is the constant.** Total wet mass decreases as water evaporates. Compute dry mass first using: $M_d = M_i(1 - MC_{wb,i}/100)$.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Confusing Water Content with Water Activity (loses 2 marks):**
> A dried food can have low MC but high a_w if water is loosely bound (e.g., high-sugar dried fruit). Conversely, a wet food can have low a_w (e.g., honey, salt fish). **a_w ≠ MC** — they are related through the sorption isotherm.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Using Wrong BET Range (loses 1–2 marks):**
> The BET equation is valid only in the range **0.05 ≤ a_w ≤ 0.35**. Applying it to high a_w data gives wrong monolayer values. Use **GAB** for higher range.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Latent Heat Value (loses 1 mark):**
> At **100 °C**, $\lambda$ = **2257 kJ/kg**. At typical grain drying temperatures (50–70 °C), use **≈ 2300–2400 kJ/kg**. Examiner expects you to **state the temperature** for which your $\lambda$ value applies.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Missing Units in Final Answer (loses 0.5–1 mark per subpart):**
> Always specify units: kg, kJ, % wb, % db, kJ/kg.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Exam!**

### Parboiling — Quick Recall
- ☐ Parboiling = **Soaking + Steaming + Drying** of paddy before milling.
- ☐ Three operations occur in **sequence**, not simultaneously.
- ☐ Hot soaking temperature: **65–75 °C** for **3–4 hours**.
- ☐ Steaming: **100 °C for 15–20 min** (atmospheric) or **3–5 kg/cm² for 10 min** (pressure).
- ☐ Starch gelatinization temperature for rice: **68–78 °C**.
- ☐ Final MC after drying: **12–14% wb**.
- ☐ Key advantage: **Higher head rice yield (65–70%)** vs raw rice (50–55%).
- ☐ Thiamine (Vitamin B₁) increases **2–3×** in parboiled rice.
- ☐ CFTRI method = standardized Indian industrial method.

### Drying — Quick Recall
- ☐ Drying = simultaneous **heat transfer in + mass transfer out**.
- ☐ Driving force = **vapour pressure gradient** between product and air.
- ☐ Two periods: **Constant Rate Period (CRDP)** and **Falling Rate Period (FRDP)**.
- ☐ Critical moisture content (X_c) = transition point between CRDP and FRDP.
- ☐ For grains, **FRDP dominates** the drying time.
- ☐ Reference drying method: **Hot air oven at 105 °C for 24 hrs**.
- ☐ For oil seeds: **Vacuum oven at 70 °C for 16–24 hrs**.
- ☐ Latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C**.
- ☐ Main dryer types: **Sun, solar, LSU, fluidized bed, rotary, spray, drum, tray, freeze**.

### Moisture Content — Quick Recall
- ☐ $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$ — for reporting, storage specs.
- ☐ $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$ — for drying calculations.
- ☐ Conversion: $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100-MC_{wb}}\times 100$.
- ☐ Always compute **dry matter first** in numericals.
- ☐ MC values: Paddy safe storage = **12–14% wb**; Wheat = **12–13% wb**; Groundnut = **7–8% wb**.

### Water Activity — Quick Recall
- ☐ $a_w = p/p° = ERH/100$ — dimensionless, 0 to 1.
- ☐ Critical a_w thresholds:
  - Bacteria: **0.91**, Yeasts: **0.88**, Moulds: **0.70**, Xerophilic moulds: **0.65**.
- ☐ Safe storage target: **a_w < 0.60** for microbial stability.
- ☐ **Sorption isotherm** = EMC vs a_w (sigmoid curve, 3 regions).
- ☐ **BET model** valid in **0.05 ≤ a_w ≤ 0.35** — gives monolayer MC (X_m).
- ☐ **GAB model** valid up to **a_w ≈ 0.9** — most accurate for foods.
- ☐ Henderson model — empirical, used in grain EMC prediction.
- ☐ Methods: **Dew point meter**, **capacitance hygrometer**, **gravimetric with saturated salt solutions**.

### Formulae to Memorize (Card Format)
1. $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$
2. $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$
3. $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}\times 100$
4. $a_w = \dfrac{ERH(\%)}{100}$
5. $W_w^{removed} = M_i\left[1 - \dfrac{100 - M_{wb,i}}{100 - M_{wb,f}}\right]$
6. BET: $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w)X} = \dfrac{1}{X_mC} + \dfrac{(C-1)a_w}{X_mC}$
7. GAB: $\dfrac{X}{X_m} = \dfrac{C K a_w}{(1-K a_w)(1-K a_w + C K a_w)}$
8. Henderson EMC: $1 - a_w = \exp(-C_1 T X^{C_2})$

### Exam-Boost Mnemonics
- **"PAR"** for Parboiling → **P**re-soak → **A**pply steam → **R**educe moisture.
- **"B-M-Y-M"** for water activity critical thresholds → **B**acteria 0.91, **M**oulds 0.70, **Y**easts 0.88 → add **M**onolayer (~0.1–0.25) for safe BET region.
- **"DB-WB-Dry Matter First"** — three rules of moisture numericals: use DB for drying, WB for reporting, **compute dry matter FIRST**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always carry 2–3 marks by **labelling diagrams clearly**, **stating units**, **mentioning operating conditions (T, time, MC)** in process descriptions, and **quoting standard values** (e.g., "AOAC oven method 105 °C, 24 hrs"; "BET valid for 0.05 ≤ a_w ≤ 0.35"; "latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C = 2257 kJ/kg"). Examiners reward **specificity and precision**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Free, Bound and Equilibrium Moisture Content, Isotherm, Hysteresis Effect, EMC Determination, Psychrometric Chart and Its Use in Drying, Drying Principles and Theory, Different Methods of Drying, Conventional, Modern and Integrated Rice Milling Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Moisture Content Fundamentals

### Free Moisture Content (FMC)
- **Formal Definition:** Free moisture content is the amount of water present in a biological material that is in excess of the **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** at the prevailing drying air conditions. It represents the water that can be readily removed by evaporation under given temperature and relative humidity conditions.
- **Mathematical Expression:** $\text{FMC} = M_t - M_e$, where $M_t$ is the instantaneous moisture content (% dry basis) and $M_e$ is the EMC.
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a wet sponge sitting on a sunny table. The water dripping off the sponge is like *free moisture* — it's loosely held, easy to remove, and will evaporate quickly. It's the "extra" water beyond what the sponge naturally wants to retain in that environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Free moisture is the *removable* moisture in any drying operation. A dryer can only theoretically reduce moisture up to the EMC of the product under the given air conditions — never below it.

### Bound Moisture Content (BMC)
- **Formal Definition:** Bound moisture is the water held within the cellular and capillary structure of a biological material that exerts a vapor pressure less than that of pure water at the same temperature. It includes water of constitution (chemically bound) and water of adhesion (physically bound within cells).
- **Key Characteristic:** Bound moisture can only be removed by supplying additional energy beyond what is required to vaporize free water. It is essentially the water that remains even after the material has reached equilibrium with air of 100% relative humidity.
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the same sponge, but now wrung out completely and still slightly damp. That remaining dampness inside the cellular walls is *bound moisture* — chemically and physically attached to the fiber structure, requiring extra heat/energy to remove.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Bound moisture is expressed as a percentage of dry weight (% db) and is characteristic of the material itself, not the drying conditions. The boundary between bound and free moisture is defined by a **critical moisture content** corresponding to a Relative Humidity of 100%.

### Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)
- **Formal Definition:** The EMC is the moisture content at which a hygroscopic biological material is in *thermodynamic equilibrium* with the surrounding air — i.e., the material neither gains nor loses moisture when exposed to air of a given temperature and relative humidity.
- **Physical Significance:** EMC represents the *lowest possible moisture content* attainable by drying under a specific set of air conditions. Beyond the EMC, the material will either continue to lose water (if wetter) or absorb moisture (if drier).
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a closed glass jar containing a slice of bread and a small cup of salt solution. Eventually the bread neither gets drier nor more soggy — it has reached EMC with the air inside the jar. Similarly, grain in a silo stabilizes at the EMC determined by the silo air's temperature and humidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** EMC is **NOT a fixed value** — it changes with both air temperature and relative humidity. Always specify EMC as $M_e(T, RH)$ in numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Sorption Isotherm

- **Formal Definition:** A sorption isotherm is a graphical plot (or mathematical relationship) showing the **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** of a hygroscopic material as a function of **Relative Humidity (RH)** of the surrounding air, at a **constant temperature**.
- **Mathematical Form:** $M_e = f(\text{RH})_{T = \text{constant}}$
- **Types of Isotherms:**
  - **Desorption Isotherm:** Plotted when a *wet* material is dried (loses moisture) to equilibrium at increasing RH values.
  - **Adsorption Isotherm:** Plotted when a *dry* material absorbs moisture at increasing RH values.
- **Shape Characteristic:** The isotherm is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** for most biological materials, divided into three regions:
  - **Region I (Bound Water):** RH < ~20–25% — water strongly bound, monolayer formation.
  - **Region II (Multilayer):** RH ~25–75% — additional water layers form.
  - **Region III (Capillary Condensation):** RH > 75% — water condenses in capillaries, free water behavior emerges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Sigmoidal Models used in KTU context:**
> - **BET Equation** (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) — for monolayer moisture
> - **GAB Equation** (Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer) — most widely used for foods & grains
> - **Henderson Equation** — empirical, popular for agricultural products
> - **Oswin Equation** — also commonly used for grains

---

## 1.3 Hysteresis Effect

- **Formal Definition:** Hysteresis is the phenomenon in which the **sorption isotherm for a material during drying (desorption) does not coincide with the isotherm during wetting (adsorption)**, producing a loop. At any given RH, the EMC during desorption is **higher** than during adsorption.
- **Physical Reasons:**
  1. Capillary condensation differs in pores of different shapes (ink-bottle effect).
  2. Trapping of air/vapor bubbles within the cellular matrix.
  3. Non-equilibrium shrinkage and swelling of the material.
  4. Chemical and structural changes during initial desorption (irreversible).
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a stretched rubber band. When you slowly release it (desorption), it takes a different path back than when you stretch it again (adsorption). The "memory" of the path creates a loop. Grain behaves similarly — once dried, it doesn't easily reabsorb the same amount of moisture at the same RH.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often assume EMC has a single value for a given RH. In reality, **two EMC values exist** — one for adsorption and one for desorption. Always state the *type* of isotherm when referencing EMC values in problems.

---

## 1.4 EMC Determination Methods

| Method | Principle | Application |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Static Gravimetric** | Sample held over saturated salt solutions of known RH until constant weight | Laboratory standard |
| **Dynamic Method** | Air of controlled T and RH passed through sample | Faster, more accurate |
| **Infrared Moisture Balance** | Mass loss under IR lamp correlated to moisture | Quick industrial check |
| **Capacitance / Resistance** | Electrical property change with MC | Online sensors |
| **NMR / Microwave** | Resonance of hydrogen nuclei | Non-destructive, research-grade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Saturated Salt Solutions used in KTU lab curriculum:**
> - Lithium Chloride → ~11% RH
> - Potassium Acetate → ~22% RH
> - Magnesium Chloride → ~33% RH
> - Sodium Chloride → ~75% RH
> - Potassium Nitrate → ~93% RH
> - Distilled Water → 100% RH

---

## 1.5 Psychrometric Chart — Definition

- **Formal Definition:** A psychrometric chart is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air, with **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** on the abscissa (X-axis) and either **Humidity Ratio (W)** or **Absolute Humidity** on the ordinate (Y-axis), incorporating lines of constant **relative humidity, wet bulb temperature, specific volume, and enthalpy**.
- **Key Lines on the Chart:**
  - Constant **DBT** lines (vertical)
  - Constant **W (humidity ratio)** lines (horizontal)
  - Constant **RH (φ)** lines (curved, sweeping upward)
  - Constant **WBT (Wet Bulb Temperature)** lines
  - Constant **enthalpy (h)** lines
  - Constant **specific volume (v)** lines
  - **Saturation curve (φ = 100%)** — upper boundary

---

## 1.6 Drying — Principles & Theory

- **Formal Definition:** Drying is the process of **simultaneous heat and mass transfer** in which moisture is removed from a solid material by evaporation into a gaseous medium (usually heated air), causing the material's moisture content to decrease from an initial value to a final acceptable level.
- **Two Concurrent Processes:**
  1. **Heat Transfer:** From hot drying air → to the product surface and interior (drives evaporation).
  2. **Mass Transfer:** Water vapor migrates from inside the product → to the surface → into the bulk air stream.
- **Conceptual Analogy:** Drying a wet cloth on a clothesline is the simplest drying system. The sun provides heat (energy input), wind carries away the evaporated water (mass removal), and the cloth dries progressively.

### Drying Theory Stages (Thin Layer Drying Curve)
A classical thin-layer drying curve is divided into distinct phases:

**Phase A — Initial Adjustment Period:**
- Brief transient where the wet material adjusts to drying air conditions.
- Surface temperature rises from initial to wet-bulb temperature.

**Phase B — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP):**
- Surface remains saturated with free water.
- Drying rate is governed entirely by *external conditions* (air velocity, T, RH).
- Surface temperature ≈ Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) of drying air.
- Rate depends on: heat transfer coefficient, mass transfer coefficient.

**Phase C — First Falling Rate Period (FFRP):**
- Surface begins to dry; wetted area decreases.
- Rate decreases linearly as the wetted surface fraction declines.
- Internal moisture movement (by capillary action) still supplies water to the surface.

**Phase D — Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP):**
- Surface is completely dry; the *evaporation plane* recedes inward.
- Diffusion of moisture through the solid matrix controls the rate.
- Temperature of material rises above WBT and approaches DBT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Most agricultural products skip the constant rate period and begin drying in the falling rate period from the start (since they are hygroscopic). This is a frequent exam point.

---

## 1.7 Rice Milling Operations

### Conventional Rice Milling
A traditional multi-step process:
1. **Pre-cleaning** → removal of foreign matter, stones, weed seeds.
2. **De-stoning** → density-based separation.
3. **Dehusking / Shelling** → removal of outer husk (hull) using rubber roll sheller or disc sheller.
4. **Paddy Separation** → separation of brown rice from unshelled paddy using paddy separator (lengta table / indented cylinder).
5. **Whitening / Polishing** → removal of bran layer using friction or abrasive polishers.
6. **Grading** → separation of broken from head rice using indented cylinder / length graders.
7. **Color Sorting** → optical removal of discolored grains.
8. **Weighing & Packaging.**

### Modern Rice Milling
- Integration of **pre-cleaners, destoners, rubber roll shellers, paddy separators, multi-stage polishers, mist polishers, color sorters, and online moisture meters** in a continuous automated line.
- Higher head rice yield, lower broken percentage, better whiteness control.

### Integrated Rice Milling
- A *fully automated* milling complex that combines **drying → storage → milling → value addition** (parboiling, flaking, fortification, packaging) under one roof.
- Includes **rice parboiling units, modern Satake/Bühler milling lines, rice bran oil extraction, and quality control labs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Conventional milling is like a roadside rice mill doing 6 steps manually. Modern milling is a well-equipped factory. Integrated milling is a complete *rice processing city* — from paddy procurement to packaged rice bags ready for the supermarket.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Moisture Content Representations

### 2.1.1 Wet Basis Moisture Content ($M_w$)
$$M_w = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100 \;\; (\% \; \text{wb})$$
- $W_w$ = mass of water (kg)
- $W_d$ = mass of dry matter (kg)

### 2.1.2 Dry Basis Moisture Content ($M_d$)
$$M_d = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100 \;\; (\% \; \text{db})$$

### 2.1.3 Conversion Formula
$$M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w/100}$$

$$M_w = \frac{M_d}{1 + M_d/100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two bases?** Wet basis is intuitive for farmers ("the grain has 14% moisture"). Dry basis is preferred in engineering calculations because the dry matter mass is invariant during drying.

---

## 2.2 EMC Models — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Henderson Equation (1952)
- **Empirical, two-parameter model**, widely used for grains.
$$\text{RH} = 1 - \exp\left[-A \cdot (T + C) \cdot M_e^{\,B}\right]$$
- Rearranged for EMC:
$$M_e = \left[\frac{-\ln(1 - \text{RH})}{A(T + C)}\right]^{1/B}$$
- $A$, $B$, $C$ are material-specific constants.
- **Use:** Most cereal grains (corn, wheat, rice).

### 2.2.2 Chung-Pfost Equation
$$M_e = -A - B \ln[-(T + C)\ln(\text{RH})]$$
- Semi-theoretical, derived from thermodynamic considerations.
- **Use:** Starchy grains and oilseeds.

### 2.2.3 Halsey Equation
$$M_e = \left(\frac{-A}{\ln(\text{RH})}\right)^{1/B}$$
- Models multilayer adsorption.
- **Use:** Starch-rich products.

### 2.2.4 Oswin Equation
$$M_e = A \left(\frac{\text{RH}}{1 - \text{RH}}\right)^{B}$$
- Best fit for many food products over a wide RH range.
- **Use:** Cereal grains, flours.

### 2.2.5 BET Equation (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller)
$$\frac{\text{RH}}{(1-\text{RH}) M_e} = \frac{1}{M_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{M_m C}\,\text{RH}$$
- $M_m$ = monolayer moisture content
- $C$ = energy constant related to heat of sorption.
- **Valid only for RH < 50%.** Useful for determining monolayer moisture.

### 2.2.6 GAB Equation (Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer)
$$M_e = \frac{M_m \cdot C \cdot K \cdot \text{RH}}{(1 - K\cdot\text{RH})(1 - K\cdot\text{RH} + C \cdot K \cdot \text{RH})}$$
- Three-parameter model — most accurate for biological materials.
- Valid up to ~95% RH.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Gold:** Know the form of the **Henderson equation** and **GAB equation**. Most numerical problems use Henderson.

---

## 2.3 Drying Rate Theory

### 2.3.1 Definition of Drying Rate
$$N = -\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{dW}{A \cdot dt} \;\; \left(\frac{\text{kg water}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)
- Surface is saturated → mass transfer controls.
$$N_c = h_m (P_{s,T_s} - P_v) = \frac{h}{\lambda}(T_a - T_s)$$
- $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $h$ = heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $P_{s,T_s}$ = saturation vapor pressure at surface temperature
- $P_v$ = partial vapor pressure in bulk air
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)

### 2.3.3 Falling Rate Period (FRP)
- Moisture movement within the solid controls.
- **Lewis model (Newtonian):**
$$M(t) - M_e = (M_0 - M_e)\,\exp(-k\,t)$$
- $k$ = drying constant (1/s), material-specific.
- **Page model (empirical improvement):**
$$M(t) - M_e = (M_0 - M_e)\,\exp(-k\,t^{\,n})$$
- $n$ is an empirical constant.

### 2.3.4 Critical Moisture Content ($M_c$)
- The moisture content at which the constant rate period ends and falling rate begins.
- For biological materials, $M_c$ is often very high (sometimes equals initial MC), so **no CRDP exists** — the entire drying is in the falling rate period.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | MC, wet basis | $M_w = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$ | % wb |
| 2 | MC, dry basis | $M_d = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100$ | % db |
| 3 | wb to db | $M_d = \dfrac{M_w}{1 - M_w/100}$ | % |
| 4 | Free MC | $M_f = M_t - M_e$ | % db |
| 5 | Drying rate | $N = -\dfrac{dM}{dt}$ | kg/m²·s |
| 6 | Lewis model | $MR = e^{-kt}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Page model | $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | EMC (Henderson) | $M_e = \left[\dfrac{-\ln(1-\phi)}{A(T+C)}\right]^{1/B}$ | % db |
| 9 | EMC (Oswin) | $M_e = A\left(\dfrac{\phi}{1-\phi}\right)^{B}$ | % db |
| 10 | GAB isotherm | $M_e = \dfrac{M_m CK\phi}{(1-K\phi)(1-K\phi + CK\phi)}$ | % db |
| 11 | Saturation humidity ratio | $W_s = 0.622 \dfrac{P_s}{P - P_s}$ | kg water/kg dry air |
| 12 | Humidity ratio (actual) | $W = W_s \cdot \phi$ | kg/kg dry air |
| 13 | Specific volume (moist air) | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v}$ | m³/kg dry air |
| 14 | Enthalpy (moist air) | $h = 1.005\,T + W(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | kJ/kg dry air |
| 15 | Wet bulb depression | $T_w = T_a - \dfrac{\lambda (W_s - W_a)}{1.82}$ | °C (approx) |
| 16 | Heat transfer (drying) | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_a - T_s)$ | W |
| 17 | Mass transfer (drying) | $\dot{m}_w = h_m \cdot A \cdot (P_{s,T_s} - P_v)$ | kg/s |
| 18 | Latent heat (water) | $\lambda = 2501 - 2.381\,T$ | kJ/kg |
| 19 | EMC (BET monolayer) | $\dfrac{\phi}{(1-\phi)M_e} = \dfrac{1}{M_mC} + \dfrac{(C-1)}{M_mC}\phi$ | – |
| 20 | Critical MC | $M_c$ = value at end of CRDP | % db |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** In the table above, vertical bars in $|\cdot|$ style have been replaced with $\vert$ or omitted to prevent markdown table breakage. All equation references use the rendered LaTeX block below.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of EMC / Drying Theory |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Grain Storage Silos** | Safe storage MC is set ~2% below the EMC to prevent mold growth. |
| **Seed Drying** | Temperature-sensitive drying to preserve germination viability. |
| **Rice Milling** | Proper paddy drying to ~14% MC ensures fewer broken grains during dehusking. |
| **Food Powder Production** | Spray drying theory uses falling rate period analysis for residence time. |
| **Pharmaceutical Drying** | Psychrometric chart analysis for batch fluidized bed dryer design. |
| **Oilseed Processing** | EMC helps decide whether artificial drying is needed post-harvest. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wet-Basis to Dry-Basis Moisture Content Conversion

### Problem Context
A maize sample has a wet-basis moisture content of $M_w = 20\%$. Convert it to dry-basis moisture content.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of wet-basis moisture content.

$$M_w = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}\times 100$$

**Step 2:** Assume a basis of $100\,\text{kg}$ of wet product. Then the mass of water in the product is $W_w = M_w$ kg and total mass is $100$ kg.

$$M_w = 20 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; W_w = 20\,\text{kg,}\;\; W_d = 80\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the dry-basis formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{20}{80} \times 100 \\
    &= 25\,\%\,\text{db}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verify with the direct conversion formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w/100} \\
    &= \frac{20}{1 - 0.20} \\
    &= \frac{20}{0.80} \\
    &= 25\,\%\,\text{db}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Reverse conversion check (going back to wb).

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= \frac{M_d}{1 + M_d/100} \\
    &= \frac{25}{1 + 0.25} \\
    &= \frac{25}{1.25} \\
    &= 20\,\%\,\text{wb} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of Water to be Removed in Drying

### Problem Context
$1000\,\text{kg}$ of paddy at $M_{w1} = 24\%$ wb must be dried to $M_{w2} = 14\%$ wb. Calculate the mass of water to be removed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the mass of dry matter (which does not change during drying).

$$\begin{aligned}
W_d &= W_{\text{total}} \times \left(1 - \frac{M_{w1}}{100}\right) \\
    &= 1000 \times (1 - 0.24) \\
    &= 1000 \times 0.76 \\
    &= 760\,\text{kg of dry matter}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the initial mass of water in the paddy.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{w1} &= W_d \times \frac{M_{w1}}{100 - M_{w1}} \\
       &= 760 \times \frac{24}{76} \\
       &= 760 \times 0.3158 \\
       &= 240\,\text{kg of water (initial)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the final mass of water remaining in the dried paddy (at the *same* dry matter mass).

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{w2} &= W_d \times \frac{M_{w2}}{100 - M_{w2}} \\
       &= 760 \times \frac{14}{86} \\
       &= 760 \times 0.1628 \\
       &= 123.7\,\text{kg of water (final)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Mass of water to be removed.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{removed}} &= W_{w1} - W_{w2} \\
                   &= 240 - 123.7 \\
                   &= 116.3\,\text{kg of water}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Verify with the universal drying formula (in terms of db MC).

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{d1} &= \frac{24}{76}\times 100 = 31.58\,\%\,\text{db} \\
M_{d2} &= \frac{14}{86}\times 100 = 16.28\,\%\,\text{db} \\
W_{\text{removed}} &= \frac{W_d (M_{d1} - M_{d2})}{100} \\
                   &= \frac{760 \times (31.58 - 16.28)}{100} \\
                   &= \frac{760 \times 15.30}{100} \\
                   &= 116.3\,\text{kg} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Formula (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> $$\boxed{W_{\text{water}} = W_1\left(\frac{M_{w1} - M_{w2}}{100 - M_{w2}}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Lewis Thin-Layer Drying Model

### Problem Context
A thin-layer drying experiment for wheat yields the following data:
- Initial MC: $M_0 = 30\%$ db
- EMC: $M_e = 10\%$ db
- Drying constant: $k = 0.012\,\text{min}^{-1}$

Calculate the time required to reduce MC to $M = 15\%$ db.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** State the Lewis (Newtonian) thin-layer drying equation.

$$\frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k\,t)$$

**Step 2:** Define Moisture Ratio (MR).

$$\text{MR} = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute known values.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MR} &= \frac{15 - 10}{30 - 10} \\
           &= \frac{5}{20} \\
           &= 0.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for time $t$ by taking the natural logarithm on both sides.

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(\text{MR}) &= -k\,t \\
\ln(0.25) &= -0.012 \cdot t \\
-1.3863 &= -0.012 \cdot t \\
t &= \frac{1.3863}{0.012} \\
t &= 115.5\,\text{min} \approx 1.93\,\text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Final Answer.

$$\boxed{t \approx 1.93\,\text{hours}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: EMC from Henderson Equation

### Problem Context
For maize, the Henderson equation constants are $A = 1.10 \times 10^{-5}$, $B = 1.85$, $C = 49.81$. Determine the EMC at $T = 30°C$ and RH = 70%.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the Henderson equation in its rearranged form.

$$M_e = \left[\frac{-\ln(1 - \phi)}{A\,(T + C)}\right]^{1/B}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\phi = 0.70$, $T = 30$, and the constants.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_e &= \left[\frac{-\ln(1 - 0.70)}{1.10\times 10^{-5}\,\cdot\,(30 + 49.81)}\right]^{1/1.85} \\
    &= \left[\frac{-\ln(0.30)}{1.10\times 10^{-5} \cdot 79.81}\right]^{0.5405} \\
    &= \left[\frac{1.20397}{8.7791 \times 10^{-4}}\right]^{0.5405} \\
    &= \left[1371.49\right]^{0.5405}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the power.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_e &= \exp\bigl(0.5405 \times \ln(1371.49)\bigr) \\
    &= \exp(0.5405 \times 7.2244) \\
    &= \exp(3.9048) \\
    &= 49.69 \;\; \%\,\text{db}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check. This is unusually high for maize EMC. The result indicates that the *temperature term* in the Henderson equation parameters must be used with the correct unit base, and the formula is a simplified form. The standard reference value of maize EMC at 30°C/70% RH is approximately **13–15% db**, so the constants $A$, $B$, $C$ above correspond to a different formulation. KTU problems always provide context-correct constants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When solving EMC problems, always use the *constants provided in the question*. Do not cross-mix parameter sets from different references.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Drying Methods

| Drying Method | Operating Principle | Best Suited For | Product Quality | Energy Use |
|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Sun Drying** | Solar radiation + ambient air | Cereals, pulses, oilseeds (low-cost) | Variable, contamination-prone | Very low |
| **Batch Tray Dryer** | Hot air through stationary trays | Fruits, vegetables, small lots | Good color retention | Moderate |
| **Continuous Belt Dryer** | Product on perforated belt, hot air through | Vegetables, pasta, snacks | Uniform drying | High throughput |
| **Fluidized Bed Dryer** | Hot air suspends particles | Grains, seeds, granular powders | Excellent, fast | High |
| **Spray Dryer** | Atomized liquid in hot air | Milk, coffee, dyes, enzymes | Powdered, high quality | Very high |
| **Drum Dryer** | Rotating heated drum | Pastes, purees, slurries | Heat-sensitive products OK | Moderate |
| **Rotary Dryer** | Tumbling in inclined cylinder | Granular bulk products | Uniform | High |
| **Infrared Dryer** | IR radiation directly heats surface | Thin layers, surfaces | Fast surface drying | Moderate |
| **Microwave Dryer** | Volumetric heating by MW | Specialty, high-value products | Internal moisture removal | Very high |
| **Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer)** | Sublimation under vacuum | Heat-sensitive (pharma, herbs) | Premium quality | Very high |
| **Solar Tunnel Dryer** | Enclosed greenhouse + chimney draft | Fruits, vegetables | Hygienic, consistent | Low |
| **LSU (Low Temperature Drying)** | Drying with natural/slightly heated air | In-bin drying of grains | Excellent germination retention | Very low |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Rice Milling Stages

| Stage | Conventional Method | Modern Method | Output Quality |
|-------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Cleaning | Manual winnowing, hand sieves | Vibratory screen, aspirator pre-cleaner | Removes 70–80% impurities vs 95%+ |
| De-stoning | Manual density separation | Destoner with vibration + air | Better purity, less loss |
| Husking | Stone/disc sheller, hand pounding | Rubber roll sheller (Satake) | Less breakage, higher head rice |
| Paddy separation | Manual winnowing | Lenga table, indented cylinder separator | High precision |
| Whitening | Mortar & pestle (chakki) | Friction whitener, abrasive polisher | Controlled bran removal |
| Polishing | Manual brushing | Mist polisher, water polisher | Glossy white rice |
| Grading | Hand sorting | Indented cylinder, length grader | Accurate broken % |
| Color sorting | Visual inspection | Optical color sorter (CCD) | High-end quality |
| Weighing & packing | Manual balance | Automatic weighers, bagging | Consistent pack weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** In commercial rice milling, the *head rice yield* (HRY) determines economic viability. Modern rubber roll shellers achieve HRY of 60–68%, while disc shellers achieve only 50–55%.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sorption Isotherm and Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Dry Material\nM very low"] --> nodeB["Humid Air\nRH increasing\n0% to 100%"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Adsorption Isotherm\nEMC increases with RH\nLower curve path"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Wet Material\nAt RH = 100%\nSaturated state"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Drying Air\nRH decreasing\n100% to 0%"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Desorption Isotherm\nEMC decreases with RH\nUpper curve path"]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeA -.-> nodeG["Hysteresis Loop\nM_ads < M_des at same RH"]
    nodeF -.-> nodeG
    nodeC -.-> nodeG
    nodeD -.-> nodeG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at bottom-left (dry material), follow the rightward path (adsorption) to the top-right (saturated).
- Then follow the leftward path (desorption) back to the dry state.
- The closed loop encloses the **hysteresis region** — for any given RH, desorption EMC is higher than adsorption EMC.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Thin-Layer Drying Curve (Moisture vs Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Initial MC\nM_0 db"] --> nodeB["Phase A\nInitial Adjustment\nBrief transient"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Phase B\nConstant Rate Period\nDrying rate N_c is constant\nSurface saturated with water"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Critical Point\nM_c = Critical MC\nPhase transition"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Phase C\nFirst Falling Rate\nWetted area decreases\nLinear decrease in rate"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Phase D\nSecond Falling Rate\nInternal diffusion controls\nRate drops sharply"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Equilibrium MC\nM_e\nDrying stops"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Psychrometric Chart — Use in Drying Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Air at Inlet\nDBT_1, RH_1\nHumidity ratio W_1\nEnthalpy h_1"] --> nodeB["Dryer Chamber\nHeating Coil adds heat\nOr no heat addition"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Air at Outlet\nDBT_2, RH_2\nHumidity ratio W_2\nEnthalpy h_2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Process Path on Chart\nVertical: Sensible heating\nSloped: Combined H and M transfer\nHorizontal: Pure humidification"]
    nodeA -.-> nodeE["Chart Region 1\nLeft lower corner\nCool dry air"]
    nodeC -.-> nodeF["Chart Region 2\nRight upper area\nWarm humid air"]
    nodeE -.-> nodeG["Direction of Process\nFrom region 1 to region 2\nHeating and humidification"]
    nodeF -.-> nodeG
```

**Process Directions on the Psychrometric Chart:**

| Process | Direction on Chart |
|---------|---------------------|
| Sensible Heating | Horizontal rightward (W constant, T rises) |
| Sensible Cooling | Horizontal leftward |
| Humidification (Adiabatic) | Along constant WBT line (upward-left) |
| Dehumidification | Downward vertical |
| Combined H & M transfer | Sloped line — typical dryer path |

---

```mermaid

    nodeA["Raw Paddy\nField harvest\nMC = 22 to 24 percent wb"] --> nodeB["Pre-Cleaner\nRemoval of straws, stones, weeds"]
```
