> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection, Installation and Their Performance, BIS Standards for Various Processed Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Post Harvest Engineering**, the phrase *"Selection, Installation and Their Performance"* refers to the **systematic engineering methodology** used to:

1. **Select** the most appropriate processing equipment/machinery for cereals (rice, wheat, maize), oil seeds (mustard, groundnut, soybean, sunflower), and horticultural crops (fruits, vegetables, spices) based on physico-chemical properties of the raw material, throughput capacity, and product quality requirements.
2. **Install** the selected machinery following manufacturer specifications, ergonomic layout planning, utility (power, water, steam) requirements, and safety codes.
3. **Evaluate Performance** using quantitative engineering metrics — **Capacity (kg/h)**, **Efficiency (%)**, **Recovery (%)**, **Broken percentage**, **Specific Energy Consumption (kWh/tonne)**, **Quality Retention Index**, and **Plant Uptime (% MTBF/MTTR)**.

The companion concept of **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** defines the **mandatory and voluntary quality thresholds** that processed products must satisfy to be legally marketable in India. **BIS** is the **National Standards Body of India**, established under the **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016** (replacing the earlier 1986 Act).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Selection is **NOT** a matter of buying the largest machine — it is a *decision matrix* problem where feed characteristics, product specification, economics, and BIS compliance must converge. Installation is the *physical realization*, and Performance is the *measurement of success*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of setting up a **post-harvest processing plant** like planning a **professional kitchen in a 5-star restaurant**:

- **Selection** is like choosing the right oven — a wood-fired pizza oven is perfect for Italian cuisine but useless for baking pastries. Similarly, a **rubber roll sheller** is ideal for paddy but a **stone grinder (chakki)** is what you need for wheat atta.
- **Installation** is like the kitchen *layout* — placing the deep-fryer next to the walk-in freezer is dangerous and inefficient; similarly, placing a noisy **destoner** next to a vibration-sensitive **color sorter** is engineering malpractice.
- **Performance** is like tasting the food — you measure doneness, oil absorption, customer satisfaction. In engineering terms: throughput, broken %, oil yield, energy per tonne.
- **BIS standards** are like the **FSSAI hygiene rules** — they don't tell you *how* to cook, but they tell you the *minimum safety and quality* the customer is entitled to.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Plant Capacity (Q)** measured in **kg/h** or **tonnes/day (TPD)**
> - **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** typically **70 – 90 %** for most post-harvest machines
> - **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** target industry benchmark **≥ 85 %**
> - **Standard reference:** **1 hectare ≈ 2.47 acres**; **1 tonne = 1000 kg**

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

The KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based curriculum maps this topic to:
- **CO3 (Module 3):** *Apply engineering principles to select, install, and evaluate post-harvest processing equipment for cereals, oilseeds, and horticultural crops complying with BIS standards.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze / Evaluate (L3–L5).

This is **high-yield** for university exams because:
- It is **directly numerical** (Part B questions carry 14 marks).
- It is **industry-relevant** — every food processing PSU recruitment (FCI, CWC, NAFED) and private sector interview (ITC, Cargill, Bühler) tests this.
- It bridges **Mechanical Engineering** (machine design), **Agricultural Engineering** (crop science), and **Food Technology** (standards).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Engineering Framework

### Phase 1 — SELECTION (Decision Engineering)

Selection is governed by **four interacting parameters**:

1. **Raw Material Characteristics**
   - Moisture content (% w.b.), kernel size, bulk density (kg/m³), angle of repose, hardness, oil content.
   - *Example:* Paddy moisture for shelling must be **12–14 % w.b.**; soybean for dehulling **9–10 % w.b.**

2. **Product Specification**
   - Final product quality (e.g., head rice ≥ 75 %, oil recovery ≥ 38 % for groundnut, juice clarity ≤ 2 NTU).

3. **Throughput / Capacity**
   - Must match **raw material arrival rate** (peaked during harvest season).

4. **Economic & Compliance Factors**
   - Capital cost, payback period (typically 3–5 yrs for food plants), **BIS compliance**, **FSSAI license**, **EIA clearance** (if applicable).

### Phase 2 — INSTALLATION (Plant Layout Engineering)

The classic **4-M Rule** of installation:
- **Man** (operator skill, ergonomics)
- **Machine** (foundation, vibration isolation, alignment)
- **Material** (gravity flow vs. conveyor-fed)
- **Method** (CIP cleaning, batch vs. continuous)

Two dominant plant layouts:

| Layout Type | Description | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Layout (Line)** | Equipment arranged in sequence of operations | High-volume single product (e.g., rice mill) |
| **Process Layout (Functional)** | Similar machines grouped together | Multi-product, low-volume (e.g., spice processing) |

### Phase 3 — PERFORMANCE EVALUATION

Performance is measured across **three engineering dimensions**:

| Dimension | Quantitative Metric | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical / Operational | Capacity, Throughput, Efficiency | kg/h, % |
| Quality | Broken %, Head yield, Oil recovery, Color (L*a*b*) | %, NTU, Lab |
| Economic / Reliability | OEE, MTBF, Specific Energy Consumption | %, hours, kWh/t |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures; **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.
> **Availability = MTBF ÷ (MTBF + MTTR)**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Numerical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | $P_{out}$ = useful power output (kW); $P_{in}$ = input power (kW) | 70 – 90 % |
| 2 | $\%\,Broken = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\,paddy}} \times 100$ | $M_{broken}$ = mass of broken grains (kg) | ≤ 5 % (BIS for rice) |
| 3 | $\%\,Recovery = \dfrac{M_{final\,product}}{M_{raw\,material}} \times 100$ | Mass-based yield | 65 – 72 % for rice |
| 4 | $SEC = \dfrac{E\,(kWh)}{M\,(tonnes)}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | 25 – 50 kWh/t (rice) |
| 5 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A = Availability; P = Performance; Q = Quality | World-class ≥ 85 % |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability ratio | ≥ 90 % desired |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{M}{\Delta t}$ | Capacity (kg/h); M = mass processed; $\Delta t$ = time | Rated machine specific |
| 8 | $\%\,Oil\,Recovery = \dfrac{M_{oil\,extracted}}{M_{oil\,seed}} \times 100$ | Solvent/mech. extraction efficiency | 35 – 42 % groundnut |
| 9 | $Moisture\,Conversion: M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | w.b. → d.b. moisture | Universal |
| 10 | $Payback = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{Annual\,Net\,Cash\,Flow}$ | Years | 3 – 5 yrs typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** In equation 9, if $M_{wb} = 0.14$ (14 %), then $M_{db} = 0.14 / 0.86 \approx 0.163$ or **16.3 % d.b.** Engineers ALWAYS quote d.b. moisture for storage calculations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice Industry (India):** Selection of rubber roll sheller vs. disc sheller is decided by paddy variety (long-grain Indica needs rubber rolls; short-grain Japonica prefers disc).
- **Edible Oil Industry:** Solvent extraction plants are selected when seed oil content < 20 % (e.g., rice bran), while mechanical expellers suffice for > 35 % oil seeds (e.g., coconut, groundnut).
- **Horticulture (Fruits):** Selection of pulper finisher mesh size (0.5 mm vs. 1.0 mm) directly determines juice clarity — a critical BIS parameter for **Fruit Beverages IS 14543**.
- **Spices:** Installation of **plate magnet** before hammer mill is mandatory per BIS **IS 1797** for turmeric/coriander to prevent metal contamination.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Performance Efficiency of a Multi-Stage Processing Line

Consider a **rice milling line** with three sequential stages: Cleaning → Shelling → Polishing. The total plant efficiency is **NOT** the simple arithmetic mean — it is the **product** of stage efficiencies.

### Step 1 — Define Stage Efficiencies

Let:
- $\eta_1$ = efficiency of cleaner (mass of cleaned paddy ÷ mass of dirty paddy fed)
- $\eta_2$ = shelling efficiency (mass of brown rice ÷ mass of cleaned paddy)
- $\eta_3$ = polishing efficiency (mass of white rice ÷ mass of brown rice)

### Step 2 — Write Mass Balance at Each Stage

Let $M_0$ = mass of raw paddy entering the cleaner.

$$
M_1 = \eta_1 \cdot M_0
$$

Here, $M_1$ is the mass of cleaned paddy exiting Stage 1 and entering Stage 2.

$$
M_2 = \eta_2 \cdot M_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot M_0
$$

Here, $M_2$ is the mass of brown rice entering the polisher.

$$
M_3 = \eta_3 \cdot M_2 = \eta_3 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot M_0
$$

Here, $M_3$ is the mass of final white rice.

### Step 3 — Define Overall Plant Recovery

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{M_3}{M_0} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The overall plant recovery is the **multiplicative product** of individual stage efficiencies. This is why a small drop in shelling efficiency has a **disproportionately large** effect on final yield.

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration

Let $\eta_1 = 0.97$, $\eta_2 = 0.92$, $\eta_3 = 0.90$.

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.90
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.8925 \times 0.90 = 0.80325
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} \approx 80.3\,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every **1000 kg** of raw paddy, **803 kg** of white rice is produced. The remaining 197 kg consists of husk, bran, broken grains, and process losses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) of a Dryer

Let a dryer process $M$ kg of grain from moisture $M_{wb,i}$ (initial) to $M_{wb,f}$ (final). The water to be evaporated is:

$$
M_{water} = M \cdot \frac{M_{wb,i} - M_{wb,f}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}
$$

Reasoning: Starting with $M$ kg of wet grain, the dry matter is $M \cdot (1 - M_{wb,i})$. The final dry matter equals initial dry matter (conservation of solids), so:

$$
M_f \cdot (1 - M_{wb,f}) = M \cdot (1 - M_{wb,i})
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_f = M \cdot \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}
$$

And water evaporated is $M - M_f$:

$$
M_{water} = M - M \cdot \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}}{1 - M_{wb,f}} = M \cdot \left[1 - \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}\right]
$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$
1 - \frac{1 - M_{wb,i}}{1 - M_{wb,f}} = \frac{(1 - M_{wb,f}) - (1 - M_{wb,i})}{1 - M_{wb,f}} = \frac{M_{wb,i} - M_{wb,f}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{M_{water} = M \cdot \frac{M_{wb,i} - M_{wb,f}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Specific Energy Consumption

If total energy consumed is $E$ kWh:

$$
SEC = \frac{E\,(kWh)}{M_{water\,evaporated}\,(kg)} = \frac{E}{M \cdot \dfrac{M_{wb,i} - M_{wb,f}}{1 - M_{wb,f}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria by Crop

| Crop | Key Machine | Critical Selection Parameter | BIS Standard (Finished Product) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paddy → Rice | Rubber roll sheller | Grain length, moisture (12–14 % w.b.) | **IS 14830** (Rice — Specification) |
| Wheat → Atta | Stone chakki / Roller mill | Gluten content, hardness | **IS 1155** (Wheat Atta) |
| Groundnut → Oil | Expeller / Solvent | Oil content (45–50 %), kernel size | **IS 544** (Groundnut Oil) |
| Mustard → Oil | Expeller + Filter | Glucosinolate level | **IS 546** (Mustard Oil) |
| Soybean → Oil | Solvent extractor | Oil (18–22 %), protein (38–42 %) | **IS 9822** (Soybean Oil) |
| Mango → Pulp | Pulper-finisher | Pulp-to-peel ratio, Brix (≥ 14°) | **IS 3883** (Mango Pulp) |
| Tomato → Ketchup | Pulper + Evaporator | Acidity, total solids (≥ 25 %) | **IS 3882** (Tomato Ketchup) |
| Turmeric → Powder | Hammer mill + Pulverizer | Curcumin content (≥ 2 %) | **IS 3576** (Turmeric Powder) |

---

## 3.4 Installation: Foundation & Power Calculation Example

**Problem:** A 5 HP (3.73 kW) paddy cleaner is to be installed. Determine the cement foundation block size if foundation density is 2200 kg/m³ and allowable ground pressure is 50 kPa.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Total dynamic load = motor + machine + foundation weight.
Assume machine + motor weight = 200 kg. Required foundation mass (rule of thumb: foundation mass ≥ 5× equipment mass):

$$
m_{foundation} = 5 \times 200 = 1000\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Foundation volume:

$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{1000}{2200} = 0.4545\,m^3
$$

**Step 3:** Choose square block of side $L$. Then $V = L^3$ (cubic block) or $V = L^2 \cdot h$ for rectangular.

Assume cubic block: $L = V^{1/3} = 0.4545^{1/3} \approx 0.769\,m \approx 77\,cm$.

**Step 4:** Check ground pressure:

$$
\text{Total weight} = (1000 + 200) \times 9.81 = 11{,}772\,N
$$

$$
\text{Area} = 0.769^2 = 0.591\,m^2
$$

$$
P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{11{,}772}{0.591} \approx 19{,}920\,Pa \approx 19.92\,kPa
$$

Since $19.92 < 50\,kPa$, the foundation is **safe**.

---

## 3.5 BIS Standards for Major Processed Products (Comprehensive)

| Product Category | IS Number | Title | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cereals** | IS 14830 | Rice | Broken ≤ 5 %, foreign matter ≤ 1 % |
| | IS 1155 | Wheat Atta | Moisture ≤ 14 %, ash ≤ 2.0 % |
| | IS 1156 | Maize Flour | Moisture ≤ 12.5 % |
| | IS 2813 | Suji / Rava | Particle size ≤ 0.5 mm |
| **Oilseeds** | IS 544 | Groundnut Oil (Refined) | FFA ≤ 3.0 %, moisture ≤ 0.1 % |
| | IS 546 | Mustard Oil | Allyl isothiocyanate, FFA ≤ 2.5 % |
| | IS 9822 | Soybean Oil | Iodine value 120–141 |
| | IS 427 | Coconut Oil | Moisture ≤ 0.1 % |
| **Horticultural** | IS 3883 | Mango Pulp / Concentrate | TSS ≥ 28 °Brix, acidity 0.5–1.0 % |
| | IS 3882 | Tomato Ketchup | Total solids ≥ 25 % |
| | IS 3576 | Turmeric Powder | Curcumin ≥ 2 %, lead ≤ 10 ppm |
| | IS 1797 | Chili Powder | Capsaicin, color value |
| | IS 14543 | Fruit Beverages | TSS, CO₂ pressure for carbonated |
| | IS 13862 | Dehydrated Potato | Moisture ≤ 8 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS Certification Levels:**
> - **ISI Mark** (voluntary) — manufacturer self-certifies with BIS testing.
> - **Mandatory BIS** (since 2012, expanded in 2020) — for items like **pressure cookers, BIS marked steel, packaged drinking water** — non-compliance attracts penalties under the BIS Act 2016.

---

## 3.6 Performance Evaluation: Worked Numerical

**Problem:** A rice mill processes **5 tonnes** of paddy in **8 hours**. The output is **3.4 tonnes** of head rice, **0.25 tonnes** of broken rice, and the rest is husk + bran + loss. Calculate:
(a) Capacity, (b) Overall recovery, (c) Head rice yield, (d) SEC if total electrical energy consumed is 110 kWh.

### Solution

**(a) Capacity:**

$$
Q = \frac{M}{\Delta t} = \frac{5000\,kg}{8\,h} = 625\,kg/h
$$

**(b) Overall Recovery (Head + Broken, BIS considers both as edible):**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{3.4 + 0.25}{5.0} = \frac{3.65}{5.0} = 0.73 = 73\,\%
$$

**(c) Head Rice Yield:**

$$
Y_{head} = \frac{3.4}{5.0} \times 100 = 68\,\%
$$

**(d) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$
SEC = \frac{110\,kWh}{5\,tonnes} = 22\,kWh/t
$$

This is **well within** industry benchmark of 25–50 kWh/t for modern rice mills.

---

## 3.7 Installation Checklist (KTU Examiner's Standard)

| # | Item | Specification / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Site selection | Elevated, flood-free, near market |
| 2 | Floor space | 1.5× machine footprint for operator movement |
| 3 | Foundation | 5× machine weight (rule of thumb) |
| 4 | Power supply | 3-phase, ±10 % voltage variation; with earthing |
| 5 | Ventilation | 10–15 air changes/hour |
| 6 | Lighting | ≥ 300 lux at work points |
| 7 | Noise | < 85 dB(A) (Factories Act) |
| 8 | Fire safety | Extinguisher every 10 m (Class A for grain dust) |
| 9 | Drainage | Sloped floor 1:60 toward floor drain |
| 10 | BIS/FSSAI | Display licenses prominently |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
List **any six BIS standards** applicable to processed cereal and oilseed products in India.

**Model Answer (6 points):**

| S.No. | IS Number | Product |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | IS 14830 | Rice |
| 2 | IS 1155 | Wheat Atta |
| 3 | IS 1156 | Maize Flour |
| 4 | IS 544 | Refined Groundnut Oil |
| 5 | IS 546 | Mustard Oil |
| 6 | IS 9822 | Soybean Oil |

**Mark distribution:** ½ mark each × 6 = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** and state the **world-class benchmark** value.

**Model Answer:**
OEE is a composite performance metric that measures the effectiveness of a machine by combining three factors:

$$
OEE = Availability \times Performance \times Quality
$$

Where:
- Availability = $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- Performance = $\dfrac{Actual\,Throughput}{Rated\,Throughput}$
- Quality = $\dfrac{Good\,Units\,Produced}{Total\,Units\,Produced}$

**World-class benchmark** for OEE in food processing industries is **≥ 85 %**. (3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for each component / 1 for benchmark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **selection criteria** for post-harvest processing equipment with reference to **cereals, oil seeds, and horticultural crops**. Use specific examples. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the **installation procedure** for a typical post-harvest processing plant, including foundation design, utility connections, and safety considerations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Selection Criteria Framework:**

The selection of post-harvest equipment is a **multi-criteria decision** involving raw material properties, product quality targets, throughput, and economic feasibility.

**1. Raw Material-Based Selection:**
- *Cereals (Paddy):* For Indica long-grain paddy, a **rubber roll sheller** (roll gap 0.5–0.8 mm) is preferred over disc sheller because of low grain breakage (< 5 %).
- *Oil seeds (Groundnut):* With 45–50 % oil content, a **mechanical screw expeller** at 30–40 kg/h capacity per HP is selected over solvent extraction.
- *Horticulture (Mango):* For Brix 14° fruit, a **pulper-finisher** with 0.5 mm screen is selected to achieve BIS pulp specification (IS 3883).

**2. Product-Based Selection:**
Final product dictates machine selection. For **head rice ≥ 75 %** per **IS 14830**, a multi-stage **Paddy Separator + Polisher** is mandatory.

**3. Capacity-Based Selection:**
Capacity is matched to **harvest season peak arrival** — typically 2× annual average. Formula:

$$
Q_{design} = \frac{M_{annual}}{\Delta t_{operating\,hrs\,per\,year}}
$$

**4. Economic Selection:**
Capital cost vs. operating cost trade-off — BIS compliance adds ~3–5 % to capital cost but reduces rejection/rework cost.

*(7 marks: 1 for framework, 2 for cereals, 2 for oil seeds, 1 for horticultural, 1 for economic rationale)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Installation Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Site Preparation:**
- Soil bearing capacity test (≥ 100 kPa for small plants, ≥ 200 kPa for heavy expellers).
- Floor plan: 1.5× machine footprint reserved for operator movement.

**Step 2 — Foundation Design (5× rule):**
Foundation mass ≥ 5 × equipment dynamic mass. For a 500 kg cleaner:

$$
m_{foundation} \geq 2500\,kg
$$

**Step 3 — Utility Connections:**
- **Power:** 3-phase, 415 V ± 10 %, separate earthing (≤ 1 Ω).
- **Water:** Potable supply at 1.5–2 bar for cleaning.
- **Steam:** 4–6 bar for processing lines.
- **Drainage:** Floor slope 1:60 toward floor drain.

**Step 4 — Machine Erection and Alignment:**
- Belt alignment using laser or straight-edge.
- Shaft alignment: angular < 0.05 mm/m, parallel < 0.1 mm/m.

**Step 5 — Safety:**
- Emergency stop within 1.5 m of every operator station.
- Fire extinguishers (Class A for grain dust, Class B for oil).
- PPE compliance per IS 8518.

**Step 6 — Commissioning:**
- No-load trial for 4 hours.
- Load trial for 8 hours.
- BIS-grade product sample drawn and lab-tested.

*(7 marks: 1 site prep + 2 foundation + 2 utilities + 1 safety + 1 commissioning)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Define **BIS** and explain the **mandatory and voluntary certification schemes** under the **BIS Act, 2016**. Give **five examples** of processed products and their IS numbers. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A rice mill processes **8 tonnes of paddy** per shift (8 hours). The output is **5.6 tonnes** of head rice and **0.4 tonnes** of broken rice. Total electrical energy consumed is **240 kWh**.
Calculate: **(i)** Plant capacity, **(ii)** Overall recovery, **(iii)** Head rice yield, **(iv)** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), and **(v)** OEE if MTBF = 180 h and MTTR = 20 h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**BIS Definition:**
The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is the **National Standards Body of India**, established under the **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016**. It formulates and enforces standards for products, processes, and services to ensure consumer safety and quality.

**Certification Schemes:**

| Scheme | Nature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **ISI Mark (Voluntary)** | Manufacturer self-certifies with BIS testing | Rice, Atta, Spices |
| **Mandatory BIS Certification** | Compulsory by Govt. of India notification | Packaged drinking water, Steel, Pressure cookers |
| **Hallmarking** | For precious metals | Gold/Silver |
| **Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS)** | For electronics (now under MeitY) | Mobile phones, laptops |

**Five Examples with IS Numbers:**

| Product | IS Number |
|---|---|
| Mango Pulp | IS 3883 |
| Tomato Ketchup | IS 3882 |
| Turmeric Powder | IS 3576 |
| Refined Groundnut Oil | IS 544 |
| Packaged Drinking Water | IS 14543 |

*(7 marks: 2 definition + 3 schemes + 2 examples)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
$M_{paddy} = 8\,t = 8000\,kg$
$M_{head} = 5.6\,t$
$M_{broken} = 0.4\,t$
$E = 240\,kWh$
$MTBF = 180\,h$, $MTTR = 20\,h$
Shift time $\Delta t = 8\,h$

**(i) Plant Capacity:**

$$
Q = \frac{M}{\Delta t} = \frac{8000}{8} = 1000\,kg/h = 1\,tonne/h
$$

**(ii) Overall Recovery:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{5.6 + 0.4}{8.0} \times 100 = \frac{6.0}{8.0} \times 100 = 75\,\%
$$

**(iii) Head Rice Yield:**

$$
Y_{head} = \frac{5.6}{8.0} \times 100 = 70\,\%
$$

**(iv) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$
SEC = \frac{240\,kWh}{8\,tonnes} = 30\,kWh/t
$$

**(v) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):**
Only availability component is computable from given data; assume Performance = 1.0 and Quality = 1.0 (no defects mentioned):

$$
A = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\,\%
$$

$$
OEE = 0.90 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.90 = 90\,\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** OEE of **90 %** is **above world-class benchmark (85 %)** — indicates excellent plant.

*(7 marks: 1 each for sub-parts i, ii, iii, iv, plus 3 for v + interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing wet basis (w.b.) and dry basis (d.b.) moisture. ALWAYS convert and explicitly state the basis.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Writing "BIS" without explaining it stands for **Bureau of Indian Standards** — costs 0.5 mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** In OEE problems, students forget that **Quality ratio = Good units ÷ Total units**, not total raw material.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Conflating **payback period** with **return on investment (ROI)** — they are different formulas.
> - **Common mistake 5:** Not labeling units in final numerical answers — even if the answer is correct, examiner deducts ½ mark.
> - **Common mistake 6:** In installation questions, ignoring **earthing resistance** (must be ≤ 1 Ω) and **noise limits (< 85 dB(A)**) — both are KTU favorites.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Selection** is governed by **raw material properties**, **product specification**, **capacity**, and **economics**.
- [ ] **Installation** follows the **5× foundation rule**: foundation mass ≥ 5 × equipment mass.
- [ ] **Plant capacity** is computed as $Q = M / \Delta t$, always in **kg/h** or **TPD**.
- [ ] **Overall recovery** in multi-stage lines is the **multiplicative product** of stage efficiencies, NOT the arithmetic mean.
- [ ] **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**; **world-class ≥ 85 %**.
- [ ] **Availability** $= MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$; **target ≥ 90 %**.
- [ ] **SEC** for rice milling industry benchmark is **25–50 kWh/t**.
- [ ] **BIS** = **Bureau of Indian Standards**; legal authority = **BIS Act, 2016**.
- [ ] **Head rice ≥ 75 %** and **broken ≤ 5 %** are **IS 14830** quality markers.
- [ ] **Moisture conversion**: $M_{db} = M_{wb} / (1 - M_{wb})$.
- [ ] **Earthing resistance** ≤ 1 Ω; **noise limit** < 85 dB(A) — both mandatory.
- [ ] **Product layout** = line arrangement (single product, high volume).
- [ ] **Process layout** = functional grouping (multi-product, low volume).
- [ ] **BIS standards to memorize**: IS 14830 (Rice), IS 1155 (Wheat Atta), IS 544 (Groundnut Oil), IS 546 (Mustard Oil), IS 3883 (Mango Pulp), IS 3882 (Tomato Ketchup), IS 3576 (Turmeric), IS 9822 (Soybean Oil).
- [ ] **Cement foundation** must satisfy both **mass rule** AND **ground pressure rule** (≤ allowable soil bearing capacity).
- [ ] **Payback period** = Capital Cost / Annual Net Cash Flow; typical food plant = **3–5 years**.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Topic: Selection, Installation and Their Performance, BIS Standards for Various Processed Products**
> **Course:** PCAGT601 | **KTU 2024 Scheme** | **Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Processing of Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Importance of Processing of Fruits and Vegetables ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard Terminology):** > **Post-Harvest Processing of Fruits and Vegetables** refers to the series of value-addition, preservation, packaging, and transformation operations applied to perishable horticultural produce *immediately after harvest* (and before primary consumption), with the explicit objectives of (i) minimizing quantitative and qualitative losses, (ii) extending the **shelf-life** of seasonal produce, (iii) enhancing marketability, (iv) ensuring food safety, and (v) converting low-value raw farm produce into high-value finished products such as juices, pulps, jams, jellies, pickles, dried/dehydrated products, canned products, frozen products, and minimally processed ready-to-eat (RTE) items. In simpler terms, **post-harvest processing of fruits and vegetables (F&V)** is the **bridge between the farmer's field and the consumer's plate** that prevents the produce from rotting, improves its taste/appearance/nutrition, and turns it into convenient, longer-lasting products. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strawberry Jam" Intuition Imagine a farmer in Wayanad (Kerala) harvests **1,000 kg of strawberries** on a Monday morning. - **Without processing**: He must sell all 1,000 kg *within 2–3 days* at the local market price of say ₹40/kg → Total revenue ≈ **₹40,000**. Any unsold berries simply **rot** and become worthless. - **With processing**: He converts the same 1,000 kg into **strawberry jam (≈ 600 jars × 250 g)**, selling each jar at ₹120 → Total revenue ≈ **₹72,000**, with a shelf-life of **12–18 months**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Processing does not just *preserve* food — it **multiplies economic value, time-value, and geographic reach** of the produce. This is the single most important "why" answer you can write for any 14-mark question on this topic. ### 1.2 Standard Metrics Used in F&V Processing (Board-Examiner Expectation) - **Post-Harvest Loss (PHL)** in India for F&V: **5–16 %** in developed cold-chain systems; **up to 30–40 %** in traditional Indian supply chains (per ICAR-CIPHET 2022 estimates). - **Shelf-life extension factor**: Fresh tomato = 7–14 days → Processed tomato ketchup = **12–24 months**. - **Recovery yield** of pulp from fruits: typically **60–75 %** for mango, guava; **80–85 %** for tomato. - **Standard preservation pH** for safe acidic products: **pH < 4.5** (below this, *Clostridium botulinum* cannot germinate). - **Standard water activity (a_w) threshold** for microbial safety: **a_w < 0.60** for shelf-stable dried products. - **FSSAI standards** govern all commercial F&V processed products in India. > [!TIP] > Whenever you cite a numerical fact in a KTU 14-mark answer, **bold it** and **attribute the source** (e.g., *ICAR-CIPHET, FSSAI, FAO*) — this gives the examiner a strong impression of "beyond-syllabus depth" and earns full marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of "Importance" — Board-Examiner's Mental Checklist For any question like *"Discuss the importance of processing of fruits and vegetables"* (a very common **Part B 14-mark** question), examiners expect a structured answer covering the following **five pillars**: #### Pillar 1 — Reduction of Post-Harvest Losses (Quantitative Importance) - F&V are **highly perishable** due to: - **High moisture content** (typically **75–95 %** w.b.) → favorable for microbial growth. - **Active respiration** (climacteric fruits like mango, banana, tomato show a *respiratory peak*). - **Soft tissue structure** → prone to mechanical damage during handling. - **High enzymatic activity** (polyphenol oxidase → browning; pectin methyl esterase → softening). - **Processing interventions** that reduce loss: - Cooling / pre-cooling, refrigerated transport. - Canning, pasteurization, sterilization. - Dehydration / osmotic dehydration. - Freezing / IQF (Individual Quick Freezing). - Chemical preservation (sugar, salt, vinegar, KMS). - Controlled / Modified Atmosphere Packaging (CAP / MAP). #### Pillar 2 — Shelf-Life Extension & Seasonal Availability - Most F&V are **seasonal** but demand is **year-round**. - Processing bridges this temporal gap: e.g., **mango pulp in May → used for beverages in December**. - Quantified shelf-life: | Product Form | Typical Shelf-Life | |---|---| | Fresh fruit (ambient) | 2 – 14 days | | Refrigerated fresh (4 °C) | 7 – 30 days | | Frozen (–18 °C) | 6 – 12 months | | Hot-fill pasteurized juice | 6 – 12 months | | Retort-sterilized canned | 12 – 24 months | | Dried / dehydrated | 6 – 18 months | | Jam / Jelly (high-sugar) | 12 – 24 months | #### Pillar 3 — Value Addition & Economic Importance - **Value addition** = the difference between the **processed product price** and the **raw material price** (minus processing cost). - This directly increases **farmer income**, **rural employment**, and **foreign exchange earnings** through horticultural exports. - India is the **2nd largest producer of fruits and vegetables globally** (~ 330 million tonnes, MoAFW 2022-23), yet processes only about **2–3 %** (vs 60–80 % in USA / Brazil) — huge untapped potential. #### Pillar 4 — Nutritional Preservation & Food Security - Processing, when done correctly, **retains** vitamins, minerals, fiber, and phytochemicals. - It also **eliminates anti-nutritional factors** and **microbial pathogens**. - Examples: - Blanching + dehydration of green leafy vegetables preserves **iron, folate**. - Tomato processing **increases bioavailable lycopene**. - Fortified fruit juices deliver **Vitamin C, D, calcium** to mass consumers. #### Pillar 5 — Convenience, Diversification & By-Product Utilization - Modern consumer wants **RTE / RTC** (Ready-To-Eat / Ready-To-Cook) products. - Processing enables product diversification: juice, nectar, squash, jam, jelly, marmalade, pickle, sauce, ketchup, candy, powder, leather, wine, vinegar, essential oils, pectin. - **By-products** (peel, seed, pomace) are sources of **pectin, essential oils, antioxidants, dietary fiber, biofuel** — supporting the **zero-waste / circular bio-economy** model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for 14-mark Answers — "FIVE"**: **F** – Food security; **I** – Income / value-addition; **V** – Variety / diversification; **E** – Extended shelf-life; (and the unspoken 5th) **L** – Loss reduction. Use this structure to never miss a mark. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Although this topic is largely qualitative, KTU examiners frequently test **numerical/quantitative aspects** linked to losses, drying, and yield. Master these equations. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Post-Harvest Loss (%) | $$\text{PHL \%} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$$ | $W_i$ = initial mass (kg), $W_f$ = final marketable mass (kg) | | 2 | Moisture Content on **Wet Basis** (w.b.) | $$M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$ | $W_w$ = wet mass, $W_d$ = dry mass (g) | | 3 | Moisture Content on **Dry Basis** (d.b.) | $$M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$ | ratio of water to dry solid | | 4 | **Moisture Ratio** (drying kinetics) | $$MR = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$$ | $M_t$ = moisture at time *t*; $M_0$ = initial; $M_e$ = equilibrium | | 5 | Thin-Layer Drying (Page / Henderson-Pabis) | $$MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$$ | $k$ = drying constant (1/h), $n$ = dimensionless exponent | | 6 | Water Activity Relationship | $$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$$ | $p$ = vapour pressure of product, $p_0$ = that of pure water | | 7 | Process Yield | $$\eta_p = \dfrac{m_{product}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$$ | mass basis, % | | 8 | Value Addition Ratio (VAR) | $$\text{VAR} = \dfrac{P_{processed} - C_{raw}}{C_{raw}}$$ | $P_{processed}$ = selling price/kg, $C_{raw}$ = raw material cost/kg | | 9 | Pickle Brine Concentration (Salt % w/w) | $$\%S = \dfrac{m_{salt}}{m_{salt}+m_{water}} \times 100$$ | common target: 8–15 % for safe pickle | | 10 | Pasteurization Equivalent (Ball formula) | $$F = F_{ref} \cdot 10^{\dfrac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$$ | $F$ = lethality (min), $z$ ≈ 10 °C for most spoilage microbes | > [!WARNING] > **Penalty Alert:** Many students write $|x|$ inside a markdown table to denote "absolute value" — this **breaks** the table renderer. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in tables. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Supply chain design**: Enables cold-chain and ambient-chain decisions. - **Plant layout**: Drives sizing of processing lines (e.g., 5 TPH tomato ketchup plant). - **Instrumentation**: Dictates need for pH meters, refractometers (°Brix), colorimeters, texture analyzers. - **Regulatory compliance**: FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India), AGMARK grading, BIS standards, Codex Alimentarius international standards. - **Export engineering**: HACCP, ISO 22000, BRC Global Standards for processed F&V. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantitative Post-Harvest Loss (PHL) Estimation **Objective:** Show the working from raw harvested mass to the *effective economic loss* in a typical F&V supply chain. **Given:** A farmer harvests $W_i = 1{,}000$ kg of tomatoes in Kottayam. After sorting, grading, transport to Ernakulam wholesale market, and retail handling, the *salable* mass arriving to the consumer is $W_f = 720$ kg. Spoiled/unsold mass is discarded. **Step 1 — Define PHL on a mass basis** $$ \text{PHL \%} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values** $$ \text{PHL \%} = \dfrac{1{,}000 - 720}{1{,}000} \times 100 $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator** $$ \text{PHL \%} = \dfrac{280}{1{,}000} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 — Final result** $$ \text{PHL \%} = 28\% $$ **Interpretation:** India loses **~28 %** of tomatoes in this typical short-chain — equivalent to ₹ 280 × ₹30/kg ≈ **₹ 8,400 lost per tonne** before reaching the consumer. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of Wet-Basis Moisture to Dry-Basis Moisture **Objective:** Show conversion essential for drying calculations in F&V processing. **Given:** A sample of sliced mango has wet mass $W_w = 250$ g. After oven drying at 105 °C for 24 h, the constant dry mass is $W_d = 65$ g. **Step 1 — Compute wet-basis moisture** $$ M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \dfrac{250 - 65}{250} \times 100 $$ $$ M_{wb} = \dfrac{185}{250} \times 100 = 74\% $$ **Step 2 — Convert to dry-basis moisture** $$ M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \dfrac{185}{65} \times 100 $$ $$ M_{db} = 284.6\% $$ **Step 3 — Verify the algebraic conversion relation** $$ M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100 $$ $$ M_{db} = \dfrac{74}{100 - 74} \times 100 = \dfrac{74}{26} \times 100 $$ $$ M_{db} = 284.6\% $$ **Engineering takeaway:** For drying design (mass of water to be removed), engineers **always use $M_{db}$**, because $M_{wb}$ becomes misleading at very low moisture contents. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Value Addition Ratio (VAR) for Mango Pulp Processing **Given:** - Raw mango cost = ₹ 25 / kg. - 1,000 kg raw mango → 700 kg pulp (after peeling, deseeding, pulping, pasteurization). - Processed pulp selling price = ₹ 90 / kg. **Step 1 — Compute raw-material cost of 1 kg pulp** $$ C_{raw\_per\_kg\_pulp} = \dfrac{1{,}000 \times 25}{700} = 35.71 \text{ ₹/kg} $$ **Step 2 — Apply VAR formula** $$ \text{VAR} = \dfrac{P_{processed} - C_{raw}}{C_{raw}} \times 100 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ \text{VAR} = \dfrac{90 - 35.71}{35.71} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 — Final value-addition** $$ \text{VAR} = \dfrac{54.29}{35.71} \times 100 = 152\% $$ **Interpretation:** Every ₹ 100 worth of raw mango yields **₹ 252** worth of pulp — a **152 % value addition**, justifying capital investment in processing plants. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Processing Matters: Cause → Effect → Solution | # | Problem Faced | Scientific Cause | Processing Solution | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Quick spoilage of ripe mango | Microbial growth + high $a_w$ (> 0.95) | Hot-fill pasteurization + aseptic packaging → shelf-life 12 mo | | 2 | Enzymatic browning of apple slice | Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) + O₂ | Blanching + KMS (0.1 %) + MAP with N₂ | | 3 | Seasonal glut of tomato | Short harvest window | Conversion to ketchup / paste / powder | | 4 | Transportation damage in banana | Mechanical bruising + ethylene | Corrugated fibre boxes + BHT/CaC₂ treatment | | 5 | Toxic glycoalkaloids in potato | Solanine / chaconine | Peeling + blanching before drying | | 6 | Aflatoxin in dried chili | *Aspergillus flavus* in high moisture drying | Solar tunnel dryer → $a_w$ < 0.6 | | 7 | Vit-C loss in citrus juice | Oxidation by O₂ + ascorbic acid oxidase | Flash pasteurization + nitrogen blanketing | | 8 | Whey + peel waste in juice industry | By-product accumulation | Pectin extraction, biogas, essential oils | ### 3.5 Comparative Numerical Matrix — Fresh vs Processed Forms | Parameter | Fresh F&V | Processed F&V (canned / dried / frozen) | |---|---|---| | Shelf-life | 2 – 14 days | 6 – 24 months | | Storage temperature | 0 – 8 °C | –18 to +25 °C (depending on process) | | Microbial load | Variable | Controlled / sterilized | | Vitamin retention | High initially | Depends on process (freeze > can > dry) | | Logistics cost per kg-km | High (cold chain) | Lower (ambient) | | Consumer convenience | Low (peeling, cutting) | High (RTE/RTC) | | Price per kg (consumer) | Baseline | 1.5 – 4 × baseline | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — F&V Processing Importance Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Field Harvest
Raw F and V Produce] --> B[Sorting and Grading] B --> C[Primary Processing
Washing Peeling Slicing] C --> D{Quality Decision Node} D -->|Grade A Fresh| E[Fresh Market Channel
Cold Chain Distribution] D -->|Grade B C Surplus| F[Secondary Processing Plant] F --> G1[Thermal Processing
Pasteurization Canning] F --> G2[Dehydration Technology
Drying Freeze Drying] F --> G3[Minimal Processing
IQF MAP CAP] F --> G4[Fermentation Pickle
Vinegar Sugar Salt] F --> G5[Extraction Technology
Juice Pulp Oil Pectin] G1 --> H[Packaging and Labeling
FSSAI AGMARK BIS] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H G5 --> H E --> I[End Consumer Market
Domestic and Export] H --> I F --> J[By Product Utilization
Peel Seed Pomace] J --> K1[Pectin Extraction] J --> K2[Essential Oil Recovery] J --> K3[Animal Feed] J --> K4[Biogas Composting] H --> L[Five Fold Importance Output] L --> L1[Loss Reduction] L --> L2[Shelf Life Extension] L --> L3[Value Addition] L --> L4[Nutrition and Food Safety] L --> L5[Employment and Export Earnings] ``` ### 4.2 Sequential Decision Flow — Why a Farmer Should Process F&V ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1
Harvest F and V] --> S2[Step 2
Estimate Post Harvest Loss Risk] S2 --> S3[Step 3
Identify Suitable Processing Method] S3 --> S4[Step 4
Apply Preservation Treatment] S4 --> S5[Step 5
Package and Label per FSSAI] S5 --> S6[Step 6
Distribute to Domestic or Export Market] S6 --> S7[Step 7
Realize Value Added Income] ``` ### 4.3 Cause-Effect Fishbone (Ishikawa) — Drivers of Processing Importance ```mermaid flowchart TD Main[Central Theme
Importance of F and V Processing] Main --> Br1[Economic Branch] Main --> Br2[Technical Branch] Main --> Br3[Nutritional Branch] Main --> Br4[Social Branch] Main --> Br5[Environmental Branch] Br1 --> Br1a[Farmer Income] Br1 --> Br1b[Export Earnings] Br1 --> Br1c[MSME Growth] Br2 --> Br2a[Shelf Life Extension] Br2 --> Br2b[Year Round Availability] Br2 --> Br2c[Product Diversification] Br3 --> Br3a[Vitamin Retention] Br3 --> Br3b[Pathogen Elimination] Br3 --> Br3c[Bioactive Enrichment] Br4 --> Br4a[Rural Employment] Br4 --> Br4b[Women Empowerment] Br4 --> Br4c[Food Security] Br5 --> Br5a[Zero Waste Goal] Br5 --> Br5b[By Product Valorization] Br5 --> Br5c[Reduced Cold Chain Footprint] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `Br1a`, `S7`) and *never* reuse reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `style`). All node labels use plain uppercase text — no embedded bold/markdown — so the diagram renders cleanly in GitHub, VS Code, and Moodle previews. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > **Q1. Define post-harvest processing of fruits and vegetables. List any four processed products obtained from mango.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Post-harvest processing of fruits and vegetables is the series of value-addition, preservation, and transformation operations applied to harvested horticultural produce to reduce losses, extend shelf-life, and enhance market value (1 mark). > Four processed mango products: > 1. Mango pulp / concentrate (0.5 mark) > 2. Mango juice / nectar (0.5 mark) > 3. Mango jam / jelly (0.5 mark) > 4. Mango pickle / chutney / dried mango slices (0.5 mark) --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]** > **Q2. State any three reasons why processing of fruits and vegetables is important.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **Reduction of post-harvest losses** — India loses 30–40 % of F&V due to perishability (1 mark). > 2. **Value addition** — Processed F&V fetch 1.5–4× higher price than raw form (1 mark). > 3. **Year-round availability** of seasonal produce via canning, drying, freezing (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]** > **Q(A). Discuss in detail the importance of processing of fruits and vegetables. Explain the various processing methods adopted for their preservation.** #### Part (a) — Importance of Processing (7 marks) Fruits and vegetables constitute nearly **30 %** of India's total horticultural produce (~ 330 MT, MoAFW 2022-23), yet only **2–3 %** is processed — leaving an enormous gap that processing must fill. **1. Reduction of Post-Harvest Losses (2 marks):** F&V have high moisture (75–95 % w.b.), active respiration, and soft tissue. Losses in the Indian supply chain reach **30–40 %** (ICAR-CIPHET). Processing interventions like cold storage, canning, dehydration, MAP, and chemical preservation bring this below 10 %. **2. Value Addition & Economic Gains (2 marks):** Processing multiplies economic value. Worked example: 1,000 kg raw mango → 700 kg pulp → VAR = **152 %**. This increases farmer income, generates rural employment, and earns foreign exchange through export of pulp, juice, dried products. **3. Shelf-Life Extension & Nutritional Retention (2 marks):** Thermal processing, freezing, and drying extend shelf-life from days to 6–24 months while preserving vitamins (especially vitamin C, A, lycopene). FSSAI-mandated pasteurization ensures microbial safety. **4. Product Diversification & Convenience (1 mark):** Juices, jams, jellies, pickles, sauces, dried powders, frozen cubes, minimally processed RTE products — all add consumer convenience and open new domestic & export markets. #### Part (b) — Processing Methods for Preservation (7 marks) | Method | Principle | Example | |---|---|---| | Thermal — Pasteurization | Heat 70–90 °C, destroy pathogens, retain flavor | Fruit juices, pulps | | Thermal — Sterilization / Canning | Heat 121 °C in sealed container, commercial sterility | Canned pineapple, peas | | Cold — Refrigeration / Freezing / IQF | Slow microbial action, ice-crystal control | Frozen peas, mango cubes | | Dehydration — Solar / Tray / Fluidized bed | Reduce $a_w$ < 0.6 | Raisins, dried chili, banana chips | | Osmotic Dehydration | Partial water removal by sugar/salt osmosis | Candied fruits | | Freeze Drying (Lyophilization) | Sublimation under vacuum | Premium coffee, mushroom powder | | Chemical Preservation | KMS, Na-benzoate, sorbate, salt, sugar, vinegar | Pickles, squashes | | Fermentation | Lactic acid, alcohol | Wine, vinegar, sauerkraut | | MAP / CAP | Modified O₂/CO₂/N₂ atmosphere | Fresh-cut salads | | Irradiation | Ionizing radiation for sprout/pathogen control | Onion, potato, spices | > Each method is chosen based on the **target shelf-life, sensory quality desired, capital available, and FSSAI regulatory framework** (1 mark synthesis). --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Retest]** > **Q(B). With neat diagrams, explain the various unit operations involved in a typical fruit processing plant. Discuss how each operation contributes to value addition and loss reduction.** #### Part (a) — Unit Operations in Fruit Processing (7 marks) A typical fruit processing line consists of the following sequential operations: 1. **Reception & Weighing** (0.5 mark) — Bulk intake of raw fruit, weighing for batch control. 2. **Sorting & Grading** (0.5 mark) — Manual or optical (color, size) removal of damaged/immature fruit. 3. **Washing** (0.5 mark) — Spray or soak to remove dirt, pesticides, microbial load. 4. **Peeling / Deseeding / Pitting** (0.5 mark) — Mechanical (abrasion), chemical (NaOH lye), steam, or enzymatic. 5. **Slicing / Dicing / Pulping** (0.5 mark) — Size reduction for uniform downstream processing. 6. **Blanching** (0.5 mark) — Hot water/steam 80–100 °C × 1–5 min to inactivate enzymes (PPO). 7. **Extraction** (0.5 mark) — Juice via screw-type / basket press / centrifugal extractor. 8. **Concentration** (0.5 mark) — Evaporation to 28–32 °Brix for pulp / concentrate. 9. **Pasteurization / Sterilization** (0.5 mark) — Thermal kill of pathogens; F-value control. 10. **Filling & Sealing** (0.5 mark) — Hot-fill into glass/PET/cans; hermetic sealing. 11. **Packaging & Labeling** (0.5 mark) — FSSAI-compliant labeling, batch coding. 12. **Storage & Distribution** (0.5 mark) — Ambient/cold storage; supply chain to consumer. #### Part (b) — Contribution to Value Addition & Loss Reduction (7 marks) | Unit Operation | Loss Reduction Mechanism | Value Addition Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Sorting | Removes 5–10 % defective | Uniform premium-grade batches | | Washing | Reduces microbial load by 90 % | Meets food-safety standards | | Peeling / Deseeding | Eliminates inedible fractions | Higher pulp recovery | | Blanching | Inactivates PPO → no browning | Brighter color, longer shelf-life | | Extraction | Concentrates juice 8–10 °Brix → 60 °Brix | High-value concentrate export | | Pasteurization | 5-log reduction of pathogens | Commercial sterility, regulatory compliance | | Aseptic Packaging | $a_w$ & O₂ control → 12–24 mo shelf-life | Premium retail price, export market | | By-product recovery | Converts 30 % waste → pectin, oil | Additional revenue 5–10 % of turnover | > **Synthesis (0.5 mark):** Each unit operation adds incremental value. The **integrated processing line** converts ₹ 25/kg raw mango into ₹ 90/kg pulp — a **260 %** end-to-end value uplift, while reducing post-harvest losses from 30 % to under 5 %. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic (and how to avoid them):** > 1. **Generic answers**: Writing *"Processing is important to reduce wastage"* — examiners want **specific numbers** (30–40 % loss, 2–3 % processing level, 152 % VAR etc.). Always quantify. > 2. **Skipping the "Why"**: Just listing methods without explaining the *principle* (e.g., why hot-fill, why low pH < 4.5) costs 2–3 marks per sub-question. > 3. **Ignoring FSSAI**: Not mentioning FSSAI, AGMARK, or Codex standards in a 2024-scheme answer is a 1-mark penalty trigger. > 4. **No diagram**: Part (b) questions on this topic **almost always** expect a flow diagram / block diagram — a missing diagram costs up to 2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting by-products**: Markers reward answers that mention **circular economy / zero-waste** via peel, seed, pomace utilization. > 6. **Mixing up terminology**: Confusing **preservation vs processing** — preservation is the *goal*, processing is the *means*. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition**: Post-harvest processing of F&V = value addition + preservation + packaging + transformation of perishable horticultural produce. - **Five pillars of importance**: (1) Loss reduction (30–40 % to < 5 %), (2) Shelf-life extension (days → months), (3) Value addition (1.5–4×), (4) Nutrition & safety, (5) Diversification & by-product use. - **India's status**: 2nd largest F&V producer globally; only 2–3 % processed — vast opportunity. - **Standard safety thresholds**: **pH < 4.5**, **a_w < 0.6**, **pasteurization 70–90 °C**, **sterilization 121 °C**, **cold chain 0–4 °C**. - **Key formulas**: PHL %, $M_{wb}$, $M_{db}$, MR (drying kinetics), VAR, yield %. - **Processing methods** (must know all): thermal, refrigeration/freezing, dehydration, osmotic, freeze-drying, chemical, fermentation, MAP/CAP, irradiation. - **Typical line**: reception → sorting → washing → peeling → blanching → extraction → concentration → pasteurization → filling → packaging. - **Regulatory bodies**: FSSAI (India), AGMARK, BIS, Codex Alimentarius (international), HACCP, ISO 22000. - **By-products**: peel → pectin, seed → oil/cake, pomace → biogas/compost/feed. - **Always mention Kerala context** in KTU exams: Kerala produces coconut, banana, mango, pineapple, jackfruit, spices, cashew — processing these has high regional relevance. - **Common 14-mark answer skeleton**: *Introduction → Loss reduction → Value addition → Shelf-life extension → Nutrition & safety → Diversification → By-products → Conclusion with future scope.* - **Mnemonic**: **FIVE** — Food security, Income, Variety, Extended shelf-life, (and Loss reduction).

topics.title: Spices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Spices

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spice** (As per FSSAI / Spices Board of India Definition):
> A **spice** is any dried, fragrant, aromatic, or pungent vegetable substance, in whole, broken, ground, or powdered form, obtained from the root, stem, bark, leaf, bud, flower, fruit, or seed of a plant, used to season, flavour, or impart aroma to food, beverages, medicines, cosmetics, or for similar purposes. Spices do **not include** salt, sugar, or any substance used as a food additive of a purely nutritional nature.

**Spice Oleoresin** is the concentrated extract of a spice containing both the volatile (essential oil) and non-volatile (resin/residue) flavour principles, obtained by solvent extraction.

**Essential Oil of a Spice** is the volatile, aromatic fraction obtained by steam distillation or hydro-distillation of the spice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spices Board of India** recognizes **52 scheduled spices** under the Spices Board Act, 1986, including pepper, cardamom, ginger, turmeric, chilli, cumin, coriander, fennel, fenugreek, nutmeg, mace, clove, cinnamon, cassia, etc.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Spices

Spices can be classified based on several criteria. The KTU-relevant classification system is given below.

### A) Botanical / Morphological Classification (Most Important for KTU)

| Sl. No. | Plant Part Used | Examples | KTU-Weighted Importance |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Seed / Fruit** | Cumin, Coriander, Fennel, Fenugreek, Mustard, Black Cumin | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Rhizome / Root** | Turmeric, Ginger | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Dried Fruit / Berry** | Black Pepper, Chilli | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Flower / Flower Bud** | Clove, Saffron, Mace | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Bark** | Cinnamon, Cassia | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Leaf** | Bay leaf, Mint, Curry leaf | ⭐ |
| 7 | **Aril / Seed Coat** | Nutmeg (kernel), Mace (aril) | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | **Stigma / Style** | Saffron | ⭐ |

### B) Classification Based on Botanical Family

| Family | Important Spices |
|:---|:---|
| Zingiberaceae | Ginger, Turmeric, Cardamom |
| Piperaceae | Black Pepper, Long Pepper |
| Solanaceae | Chilli, Paprika |
| Umbelliferae (Apiaceae) | Cumin, Coriander, Fennel, Anise |
| Myrtaceae | Clove, Allspice |
| Leguminosae | Fenugreek |
| Lauraceae | Cinnamon, Cassia |
| Labiatae | Basil, Mint |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍳

> [!TIP]
> **Think of spices as the "Soul of Indian & Global Cuisine"**:
> Imagine cooking a dish (dal, curry, or biryani) — without spices, it is just hot, bland boiled ingredients. The spice is what transforms food from **fuel → feast**. Similarly, in post-harvest engineering, spices are the most **value-added** horticultural commodity: a kilogram of raw turmeric fetches only ₹40-60, but the **oleoresin** extracted from it can fetch ₹1,500-3,000/kg. The post-harvest engineer is therefore the "perfumer and alchemist" who converts a humble farm produce into a global industrial commodity.

**Engineering Significance:**
- Spices are highly **hygroscopic** → prone to mould, aflatoxin, insect infestation.
- Spices contain **volatile oils** → drying must be gentle (below 60°C) to preserve aroma.
- Spices have **high unit value** → even small processing losses mean large revenue losses.

## 1.4 Standard Quality Constants for Spices

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|:---|:---|
| **Safe Moisture Content (storage)** | **8–11 % (w.b.)** depending on spice |
| **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)** for safe storage | **65–70 %** |
| **Aflatoxin Limit (FSSAI / Codex)** | **20 µg/kg (B1+B2+G1+G2)** |
| **Volatile Oil (Black Pepper min.)** | **2.0 % v/w** |
| **Curcumin Content (Turmeric min.)** | **2.0 % (powder)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operations in Post-Harvest Engineering of Spices

The complete post-harvest processing chain for spices consists of the following sequential unit operations. Each operation has specific engineering parameters that KTU examiners frequently test.

### Step 1: **Pre-Cleaning & Primary Cleaning**
Removal of field dirt, stones, chaff, immature seeds, and extraneous matter.

**Equipment:** Air-screen cleaner, gravity separator, magnetic separator, spiral separator.

**Engineering Parameter:** **Cleaning efficiency (η)**
$$
\eta_c = \frac{W_c}{W_{in}} \times 100 \;[\%]
$$
where $W_c$ = weight of impurities removed, $W_{in}$ = total impurities in feed.

### Step 2: **Drying (Most Critical Operation)**
Reduces moisture content from **harvest moisture (50–80 % w.b.)** to **safe storage moisture (8–11 % w.b.)** to prevent microbial spoilage and aflatoxin formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying principle for spices:** Spices lose water by evaporation from the surface, while internal moisture migrates by diffusion. The internal moisture movement is the **rate-limiting step** for high-moisture spices like ginger and turmeric rhizomes.

**Drying methods:**
1. **Sun drying** – traditional, low cost, but unhygienic and slow.
2. **Solar drying** – improved solar dryers (cabinet, tunnel).
3. **Mechanical (hot-air) drying** – tray dryer, fluidized bed dryer, rotary dryer.
4. **Specialized drying** – microwave, vacuum, freeze drying (for high-value spices like saffron).

### Step 3: **Grading & Size Separation**
Spices are graded based on **size (diameter), density, colour, and defect levels.**

**Equipment:** Vibratory screens, indented cylinders, optical sorters.

**Grade specification for Black Pepper (Spices Board):**
- **Special Grade (TGSEB)** – > 4.75 mm, bulk density > 600 g/L
- **Grade 1 (TGSEB)** – > 4.25 mm
- **Grade 2** – 3.75–4.25 mm
- **Grade 3** – 3.25–3.75 mm

### Step 4: **Sterilization / Pasteurization**
Destruction of surface microbes and insect eggs without harming volatile oils.

**Methods:**
- **Steam sterilization** (most common) at 1.05 kg/cm² for 3–6 min.
- **Gamma irradiation** (1–10 kGy, approved in India).
- **Ethylene oxide fumigation** (now banned in EU).

### Step 5: **Grinding / Milling** (For Powder Spices)
Particle size reduction using hammer mill, pin mill, or cryogenic grinder.

> [!WARNING]
> During grinding, temperature rise must be **< 10 °C** above ambient; otherwise volatile oils flash off. **Cryogenic grinding (LN₂ at –196 °C)** is preferred for high-value spices like cardamom, clove, cinnamon.

### Step 6: **Packaging**
Moisture-proof, light-proof, gas-flushed (N₂ / CO₂) packaging to extend shelf life.

**Packaging materials:** Polyethylene pouches, multi-layer laminates, aluminium foil pouches, glass bottles, tin containers.

### Step 7: **Storage & Warehousing**
Storage at **< 65 % RH** and **< 25 °C** in ventilated, fumigated godowns.

### Step 8: **Value Addition / Extraction**
- **Essential oil extraction** → steam distillation (Clevenger apparatus).
- **Oleoresin extraction** → solvent extraction (acetone, ethanol, hexane).
- **Encapsulation** → spray drying of oleoresin with maltodextrin.

---

## 2.2 🌟 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas appear every year in KTU University Exams and Internal Tests. Memorize all of them.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Drying |
| 2 | **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying kinetics |
| 3 | **MC conversion (wb → db)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$ | — | Conversions |
| 4 | **Moisture Ratio (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | dimensionless | Thin-layer drying |
| 5 | **Page Drying Model** | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$ | — | Spice drying |
| 6 | **Henderson-Pabis Model** | $MR = A \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | — | Single exponential decay |
| 7 | **Newton (Lewis) Model** | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | — | Simplest thin-layer model |
| 8 | **Drying Rate** | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -\dfrac{h_m A (H_s - H_a)}{W_d \cdot \rho_p}$ | kg/kg·h | Drying rate |
| 9 | **Oleoresin Yield** | $Y_{ol} = \dfrac{W_{extract}}{W_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Extraction efficiency |
| 10 | **Volatile Oil Yield (Clevenger)** | $\% v/w = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % | Quality test |
| 11 | **Cleaning Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_c}{W_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Cleaning |
| 12 | **Weight Loss During Drying** | $\Delta W = W_0 - W_t$ | kg | Process control |
| 13 | **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** | $M_e = f(RH, T)$ (Oswin / Henderson eqn.) | % | Storage design |
| 14 | **Bulk Density (Spice)** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Packaging design |
| 15 | **Specific Heat Capacity of Spice** | $C_p = 1.4 + 0.036 \cdot M_{db}$ | kJ/kg·K | Thermal design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Henderson-Pabis model** ($MR = A e^{-kt}$) is **the most frequently asked** drying model for spices in KTU exams. Pay extra attention to it.

---

## 2.3 Critical Theory: Moisture Sorption Isotherm of Spices

Spices are **hygroscopic biomaterials** — they adsorb or desorb moisture depending on ambient RH. The plot of $M_e$ versus RH at constant $T$ is called the **moisture sorption isotherm** (typically sigmoid-shaped, Type II).

The **Henderson equation** is widely used for spices:
$$
M_e = \left[\dfrac{-\ln(1-RH)}{A \cdot (T+C)}\right]^{1/B}
$$
where $A$, $B$, $C$ are empirical constants; $T$ in °C; $RH$ as fraction.

**Practical utility:** At $RH > 75\%$, spices gain moisture → mould growth, mycotoxin risk. Hence spices must be stored below **65 % RH**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

- Designing **solar/mechanical dryers** for cardamom and turmeric in Kerala.
- Sizing **packaging material** based on water-vapour transmission rate.
- Specifying **storage humidity** in godowns and spice processing plants.
- Optimizing **oil extraction yield** for export-quality oleoresins.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Problem #1: Moisture Content Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most repeated numerical** in KTU University Exams for Post Harvest Engineering.

**Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern — May 2023):**
A sample of wet turmeric rhizome weighs **250 g**. After complete drying in a hot-air oven at 103 °C for 24 hours, the final dry weight is **78 g**. Calculate:
1. Moisture content on **wet basis**.
2. Moisture content on **dry basis**.
3. The amount of water (in kg) to be removed from **1 tonne** of wet turmeric to dry it down to **10 % (w.b.)** safe moisture content.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Wet weight, $W_w = 250 \; g$
- Dry weight, $W_d = 78 \; g$
- Weight of water = $W_w - W_d = 250 - 78 = 172 \; g$

#### Part (a) Moisture Content (Wet Basis)

$$
MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = \frac{250 - 78}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = \frac{172}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{wb} = 0.688 \times 100 = 68.8 \;\% \;(w.b.)
$$

> Hence the initial moisture content is **68.8 % on wet basis**.

#### Part (b) Moisture Content (Dry Basis)

$$
MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{db} = \frac{172}{78} \times 100
$$

$$
MC_{db} = 2.205 \times 100 = 220.5 \;\% \;(d.b.)
$$

> The initial moisture content is **220.5 % on dry basis** — this is the value to be used in drying-rate equations.

#### Part (c) Water to be removed from 1 tonne (1000 kg)

We have to convert MC from wet basis to dry basis for calculation purposes.

Final desired moisture: $MC_{wb,2} = 10\% \Rightarrow MC_{db,2} = \dfrac{0.10}{1-0.10} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$

**Step 1:** Find the weight of dry matter in 1 tonne of wet turmeric.

Assuming the initial MC is the same as the sample (68.8 % w.b.):
- Dry matter fraction = $1 - MC_{wb} = 1 - 0.688 = 0.312$
- Mass of dry matter, $W_d = 1000 \times 0.312 = 312 \; kg$

**Step 2:** Find the final weight of turmeric at 10 % w.b.

Dry matter fraction at final condition = $1 - 0.10 = 0.90$
$$
W_{final} = \frac{W_d}{1 - MC_{wb,2}} = \frac{312}{0.90}
$$
$$
W_{final} = 346.67 \; kg
$$

**Step 3:** Amount of water removed
$$
W_{water} = W_{initial} - W_{final} = 1000 - 346.67
$$
$$
\boxed{W_{water} = 653.33 \; kg}
$$

> **Interpretation:** To convert 1 tonne of fresh turmeric to safe-storage form, **~653 kg of water must be evaporated**, which represents enormous energy expenditure — justifying the engineering need for efficient drying systems.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem #2: Thin-Layer Drying of Black Pepper

**Problem (KTU Module Test Pattern):**
Black pepper was dried in a tray dryer at **60 °C** and air velocity **1.5 m/s**. The data are:

| Time $t$ (min) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| MC (%, d.b.) | 280 | 195 | 130 | 85 | 55 |

The equilibrium moisture content $M_e = 12\%$ (d.b.). Fit the **Newton (Lewis) model** to estimate the drying constant $k$, and predict the time required to reach **15 % (d.b.)** MC.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Moisture Ratio $MR$ at each time.**

The Newton model is:
$$
MR = \exp(-k t)
$$

where
$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}
$$

Given $M_0 = 280$, $M_e = 12$:

$$
MR_0 = \frac{280-12}{280-12} = 1.000
$$
$$
MR_{30} = \frac{195-12}{280-12} = \frac{183}{268} = 0.6828
$$
$$
MR_{60} = \frac{130-12}{280-12} = \frac{118}{268} = 0.4403
$$
$$
MR_{90} = \frac{85-12}{280-12} = \frac{73}{268} = 0.2724
$$
$$
MR_{120} = \frac{55-12}{280-12} = \frac{43}{268} = 0.1604
$$

**Step 2: Linearize using $\ln(MR) = -k \cdot t$**

| $t$ (min) | $MR$ | $\ln(MR)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 30 | 0.6828 | –0.3814 |
| 60 | 0.4403 | –0.8201 |
| 90 | 0.2724 | –1.3003 |
| 120 | 0.1604 | –1.8294 |

**Step 3: Linear regression $\ln(MR) = a + b \cdot t$.**

Sum of $t = 300$, Sum of $\ln(MR) = -4.3312$, Sum of $t^2 = 22500$, $n = 5$.

Slope (drying constant) $k = -b$:
$$
b = \frac{n \sum t \ln(MR) - \sum t \sum \ln(MR)}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}
$$
$$
b = \frac{5(-1299.36) - (300)(-4.3312)}{5(22500) - 90000}
$$
$$
b = \frac{-6496.8 + 1299.36}{112500 - 90000} = \frac{-5197.44}{22500}
$$
$$
b = -0.2310 \; \text{min}^{-1}
$$
$$
\boxed{k = 0.2310 \; \text{min}^{-1}}
$$

Intercept $a \approx 0.005 \approx 0$ (good fit for Newton model).

**Step 4: Time to reach 15 % (d.b.) MC.**

$$
MR_{target} = \frac{15 - 12}{280 - 12} = \frac{3}{268} = 0.01119
$$

From $MR = \exp(-k t)$:
$$
t = -\frac{\ln(MR)}{k} = -\frac{\ln(0.01119)}{0.2310}
$$
$$
t = \frac{4.4937}{0.2310}
$$
$$
\boxed{t \approx 19.45 \; \text{min}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Pepper requires ~19.5 minutes of additional drying to reach the safe moisture level under the given conditions.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem #3: Oleoresin Yield

**Problem:**
500 g of ground chilli is subjected to solvent extraction using acetone. After evaporation of solvent, **82 g** of oleoresin is recovered. Calculate the **yield (%)**.

### Solution
$$
Y_{ol} = \frac{W_{extract}}{W_{feed}} \times 100 = \frac{82}{500} \times 100
$$
$$
\boxed{Y_{ol} = 16.4 \;\%}
$$

Typical commercial chilli oleoresin yield: **12–18 %**.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define the term **"Spice Oleoresin"**. How is it different from essential oil?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Spice Oleoresin (2 marks):**
An oleoresin is a concentrated extract obtained from a spice by solvent extraction (using acetone, ethanol, or hexane). It contains the **complete flavour profile** of the spice — both the **volatile (essential oil) and non-volatile (resinous, fatty, pigmented) components**. It is standardized, paste-like or semi-solid, and has a far longer shelf life than the whole spice.

**Difference from Essential Oil (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Essential Oil | Oleoresin |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Extraction | Steam/hydro-distillation | Solvent extraction |
| Volatile fraction only | Yes (volatile only) | No (volatile + non-volatile) |
| Aroma | True aroma | Complete flavour (taste + aroma) |
| Colour | Absent | Present (e.g., red in paprika) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
List **four important spices** obtained from the **rhizome/root** and **seed/fruit** categories. What is the safe moisture content for long-term storage of spices?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rhizome/root spices (1 mark):** Ginger (*Zingiber officinale*), Turmeric (*Curcuma longa*), Galangal, Horseradish.

**Seed/fruit spices (1 mark):** Cumin (*Cuminum cyminum*), Coriander (*Coriandrum sativum*), Fennel (*Foeniculum vulgare*), Black Pepper (*Piper nigrum*), Chilli (*Capsicum annuum*).

**Safe moisture content (1 mark):** For most spices, the safe moisture content for long-term storage is **8–11 % (w.b.)**, which corresponds to an equilibrium relative humidity (ERH) of **65–70 %**.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **post-harvest processing of Black Pepper** (King of Spices), with a neat flow diagram. Mention the standard grading parameters and the method of oleoresin extraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Henderson-Pabis thin-layer drying model** and explain its application to spice drying. A sample of cardamom was dried in a thin-layer dryer and the following data were recorded:

| Time (min) | 0 | 60 | 120 | 180 | 240 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| MC (%, d.b.) | 250 | 165 | 105 | 65 | 38 |

The equilibrium moisture content $M_e = 10\%$ (d.b.). Fit a **Newton (Lewis) drying model** and predict the time required to reach **20 % (d.b.)** MC. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A(a): Post-Harvest Processing of Black Pepper (7 marks)**

#### 1. Introduction (1 mark)
Black pepper (*Piper nigrum*) is the world's most traded spice, called the "Black Gold" or "King of Spices." India is the largest producer and exporter. The harvested berries are processed to give whole pepper, pepper powder, and pepper oleoresin.

#### 2. Unit Operations with Parameters (5 marks)

**Step 1: Harvesting (0.5 marks)**  
Berries are harvested at **maturity** (one or two berries on spike turn red/orange). Traditional hand picking.

**Step 2: Threshing (0.5 marks)**  
Berries are separated from spikes by **threshing** (manual beating or mechanical thresher). Foreign matter is removed.

**Step 3: Blanching & De-berrying (0.5 marks)**  
Berries dipped in **boiling water at 80 °C for 1 minute** to soften pericarp, then rubbed to remove stalk and dirt. **Decay prevention** is achieved by this step.

**Step 4: Drying (1.5 marks)**  
- Initial moisture: **65–70 % (w.b.)**
- Final moisture: **10–11 % (w.b.)**  
- Methods: Sun drying (7–12 days on bamboo mats), or mechanical drying at **50 °C for 8–10 h** in a tray dryer.
- Quality retention: Uniform drying → glossy, black, wrinkle-free berries.

**Step 5: Cleaning & Grading (1 mark)**  
Air-screen cleaner → destoner → grader. Grades based on **TGSEB (Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold)** size > 4.75 mm, bulk density > 600 g/L.

**Step 6: Sterilization & Packaging (0.5 marks)**  
Steam sterilization at **1.05 kg/cm² for 3 min**, dried, then packed in **double-lined jute bags** or **polyethylene pouches** for export.

**Step 7: Oleoresin Extraction (0.5 marks)**  
By **solvent extraction** using acetone or ethanol in a Soxhlet extractor → concentrated to yield **oleoresin (8–12 %)**. Then standardized to **piperine 5–6 %**.

#### Flow Diagram (0.5 marks)
Refer to SECTION_4.1 for the complete flow chart (must be drawn in the answer script).

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b): Newton Drying Model & Numerical (7 marks)**

#### 1. Derivation of Henderson-Pabis Model (2 marks)

The thin-layer drying of a spice under constant drying conditions follows Fick's second law of diffusion. Assuming a first-order rate of moisture removal:
$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = -k(M - M_e)
$$

Integrating with boundary condition $M = M_0$ at $t = 0$:
$$
\int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \int_0^t dt
$$
$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}\right) = -k\, t
$$
$$
MR = \exp(-k\,t)
$$

This is the **Newton (Lewis) model**, a special case of the Henderson-Pabis model where $A = 1$. The general Henderson-Pabis model is:
$$
MR = A \cdot \exp(-k\, t)
$$

where $A$ and $k$ are drying constants. (1 mark)

#### 2. Application to spice drying (1 mark)  
For cardamom, pepper, ginger — used to predict drying time and design dryers. The model assumes:
- Uniform initial moisture distribution
- No shrinkage
- Constant $M_e$ and air conditions

#### 3. Numerical Solution (4 marks)

**Step 1: Compute MR**

$M_0 = 250$, $M_e = 10$.

| $t$ (min) | $M$ (d.b.) | $M - M_e$ | $MR = \dfrac{M-M_e}{M_0-M_e}$ | $\ln(MR)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 250 | 240 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 60 | 165 | 155 | 0.6458 | –0.4383 |
| 120 | 105 | 95 | 0.3958 | –0.9275 |
| 180 | 65 | 55 | 0.2292 | –1.4733 |
| 240 | 38 | 28 | 0.1167 | –2.1484 |

**Step 2: Linear regression $\ln(MR) = a - k\,t$**

Using $n = 5$:
- $\sum t = 600$
- $\sum \ln(MR) = -4.9875$
- $\sum t \ln(MR) = -1018.92$
- $\sum t^2 = 108000$

Slope $k$:
$$
k = -\frac{5(-1018.92) - (600)(-4.9875)}{5(108000) - 600^2}
$$
$$
k = -\frac{-5094.6 + 2992.5}{540000 - 360000} = -\frac{-2102.1}{180000}
$$
$$
k = 0.01168 \; \text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Time to reach 20 % (d.b.)**

$$
MR = \frac{20-10}{250-10} = \frac{10}{240} = 0.04167
$$

$$
t = -\frac{\ln(0.04167)}{0.01168} = \frac{3.178}{0.01168}
$$
$$
\boxed{t \approx 272 \; \text{min} \; (\approx 4.5 \; \text{h})}
$$

> **Conclusion:** To dry cardamom from 250 % (d.b.) to 20 % (d.b.) requires approximately **4.5 hours** under the given conditions.
