### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **post-harvest processing of Turmeric** in detail. Describe the conventional and improved methods of drying. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **steam distillation process for the extraction of essential oils from spices**. Mention the principle, equipment, and the engineering design of the Clevenger apparatus. A 100 g sample of cumin seed yielded 3.2 mL of essential oil by hydro-distillation. Calculate the **% v/w volatile oil content**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a): Post-Harvest Processing of Turmeric (7 marks)**

#### 1. Introduction (0.5 marks)
Turmeric (*Curcuma longa*) is a rhizome spice, widely used as a colouring and flavouring agent. India produces ~80% of world's turmeric. Fresh rhizomes contain **70–80 % (w.b.)** moisture.

#### 2. Processing Steps (6 marks)

**Step 1: Harvesting & Curing (1 mark)**  
Rhizomes are harvested at **8–10 months** maturity, then **boiled in water for 30–60 min** to gelatinize starch and deactivate enzymes → uniform yellow colour and reduces drying time.

**Step 2: Drying (2 marks)**

| Method | Process | Moisture | Time | Quality |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Traditional Sun Drying** | On clean floor / mats | 80→10% | 10–15 days | Dust, insect contamination |
| **Improved Solar Dryer** | Cabinet / tunnel | 80→10% | 3–5 days | Better hygiene |
| **Mechanical Tray Dryer** | 55–65 °C, sliced (3 mm thick) | 80→10% | 6–8 h | Best quality |
| **Fluidized Bed** | 55–60 °C | 80→10% | 1.5–2 h | Premium, uniform |

**Step 3: Polishing & Cleaning (0.5 marks)**  
Dried rhizomes are polished in a **drum polisher** to remove outer scales and improve appearance.

**Step 4: Grading (0.5 marks)**  
Graded by size, colour (deep orange-yellow), and curcumin content (≥2% for powder grade).

**Step 5: Grinding (0.5 marks)**  
Hammer mill or pin mill with water-cooled jacket to prevent temperature rise above 40 °C. **Cryogenic grinding** for premium export quality.

**Step 6: Packaging (0.5 marks)**  
Polypropylene or jute bags for whole rhizomes; **aluminium foil laminates** for powder to prevent UV degradation of curcumin.

**Step 7: Value Addition (0.5 marks)**  
- Turmeric powder → curry powders.
- Curcumin extraction → oleoresin (4–6% yield).
- Turmeric oil → steam distillation (1.5–3%).

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b): Steam Distillation & Numerical (7 marks)**

#### 1. Principle (1 mark)
Steam distillation is based on the fact that a mixture of two immiscible liquids boils at a temperature **lower than the boiling point of either component**, when their combined vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure. This allows volatile oils (which may decompose above 100 °C) to be distilled at **below 100 °C**.

The combined vapour pressure:
$$
P_{total} = P_{water} + P_{oil} = P_{atm}
$$

#### 2. Equipment — Clevenger Apparatus (1.5 marks)

**Components:**
1. **Round-bottom flask (2–3 L)** – holds spice + water
2. **Heating mantle** – provides heat
3. **Clevenger trap (graduated)** – separates oil from water by density difference
4. **Condenser (Liebig)** – condenses vapours

**Working:** Spice + water boiled → mixed vapours → condenser → condensate enters Clevenger trap → oil floats on water (or sinks for heavier oils like clove) → measured in graduated tube.

#### 3. Flow Diagram (1.5 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Spice + Water in Flask] --> A2[Heat Source Mantle]
    A2 --> A3[Steam + Oil Vapours Rise]
    A3 --> A4[Liebig Condenser Cooling Water]
    A4 --> A5[Condensate Enters Clevenger Trap]
    A5 --> A6[Oil Separates by Density]
    A6 --> A7[Volatile Oil Collected]
    A6 --> A8[Water Returns to Flask]
```

#### 4. Numerical (3 marks)

**Given:** Sample weight = 100 g, Oil volume = 3.2 mL

For spices, density of essential oil $\approx 0.85$ to $0.95 \; g/mL$.

Assuming $\rho_{oil} = 0.9 \; g/mL$:
$$
W_{oil} = V \times \rho = 3.2 \times 0.9 = 2.88 \; g
$$
$$
\% v/w = \frac{V_{oil}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 = \frac{3.2}{100} \times 100 = 3.2 \;\% \; v/w
$$

For mass basis:
$$
\% w/w = \frac{W_{oil}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 = \frac{2.88}{100} \times 100 = 2.88 \;\% \; w/w
$$

> **Conclusion:** The cumin sample contains **3.2 % v/w volatile oil**, which is within the standard range (2.5–4.5 %) for good quality cumin.

---

## 5.3 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this as your **last-night revision checklist** before the exam.

- ✅ **Definition of Spice (FSSAI):** Dried aromatic vegetable substance from any plant part.
- ✅ **Spices Board of India recognizes 52 scheduled spices.**
- ✅ **Safe storage moisture:** 8–11 % (w.b.) for most spices; ERH < 70 %.
- ✅ **Classification — most exam-relevant:** Plant-part-based (seed, rhizome, flower, bark, leaf, aril).
- ✅ **Key drying parameters:** Temperature **50–65 °C**, slice thickness **3–5 mm** (for rhizomes).
- ✅ **Drying models — must memorize:** Newton (Lewis) $MR = e^{-kt}$, Henderson-Pabis $MR = A e^{-kt}$, Page $MR = e^{-kt^n}$.
- ✅ **Oleoresin extraction:** Solvent (acetone/ethanol), Soxhlet apparatus.
- ✅ **Essential oil extraction:** Steam/hydro-distillation, Clevenger apparatus.
- ✅ **Aflatoxin limit:** **20 µg/kg (FSSAI)** — driven by *Aspergillus flavus* in poorly stored spices.
- ✅ **Black pepper grades:** TGSEB, Grade 1, 2, 3 — based on diameter (4.75, 4.25, 3.75, 3.25 mm).
- ✅ **Turmeric quality:** Curcumin ≥ 2 %, volatile oil ≥ 1.5 %.
- ✅ **Cardamom (Queen of Spices):** Kerala produces ~70%; dried in **cardamom curing houses** at 45–55 °C for 18–24 h.
- ✅ **Chilli:** Capsaicin content determines pungency (measured in **SHU — Scoville Heat Units**).
- ✅ **Nutmeg & Mace:** Same plant (*Myristica fragrans*) — nutmeg = kernel, mace = aril.
- ✅ **Cryogenic grinding (LN₂):** Used for heat-sensitive spices (cardamom, clove, cinnamon).
- ✅ **Packaging:** Aluminium foil laminates for powders, jute + PE liner for whole spices.
- ✅ **Steam sterilization:** 1.05 kg/cm² for 3–6 min (Spices Board export standard).
- ✅ **Conversion formulas (must memorize):**
  - $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$
  - $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$
  - $MR = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$
- ✅ **Henderson sorption equation** for EMC of spices — used in storage design.
- ✅ **Clevenger apparatus** — only for hydro-distillation of essential oils.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condiments and Flowers, Characteristics and Properties of Horticultural Crops Important for Processing, Peeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Condiments and Flowers, Characteristics and Properties of Horticultural Crops Important for Processing, Peeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Condiments — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CONDIMENTS (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> Condiments are plant-derived aromatic substances (whole, ground, or extracted) used in small quantities as food additives to impart **flavor, aroma, pungency, or color** to food systems. They include true spices (pepper, cardamom, clove), herbs (mint, basil), and their derivatives (oleoresins, essential oils, spice powders).

**Classification of Condiments (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**

| Category | Examples | Primary Use |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Pungent Spices** | Pepper, Ginger, Chillies | Heat/pungency |
| **Aromatic Spices** | Cardamom, Clove, Cinnamon | Aroma/fragrance |
| **Colouring Spices** | Turmeric, Saffron, Paprika | Natural colorants |
| **Herbs** | Mint, Basil, Coriander | Garnish/leafy flavor |
| **Blended Condiments** | Garam Masala, Curry Powder | Composite flavoring |

## 1.2 Flowers as Horticultural Crops

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HORTICULTURAL FLOWER CROPS**
> Flowers cultivated for purposes **other than** ornamental display — i.e., for extraction of essential oils, perfume bases, food flavoring, nutraceutical compounds, pharmaceutical ingredients, and value-added products like gulkand, rose water, and floral teas.

**Major Commercial Flower Crops for Processing:**

- **Rose (Rosa damascena / Rosa centifolia):** Rose water, gulkand, rose oil (attar), dried petals
- **Jasmine (Jasminum grandiflorum):** Jasmine concrete, absolute, perfumery base
- **Marigold (Tagetes erecta):** Lutein extraction, natural pigment, poultry feed colorant
- **Chrysanthemum (Chrysanthemum morifolium):** Pyrethrin (biopesticide), herbal tea
- **Saffron Crocus (Crocus sativus):** World's costliest spice — dried stigmas
- **Calendula (Calendula officinalis):** Medicinal ointments, herbal cosmetics
- **Tulsi / Holy Basil (Ocimum sanctum):** Eugenol extraction, ayurvedic preparations

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY — "The Chef's Toolkit"**
> Think of condiments as the **"SALT and PEPPER SHAKERS"** of the food world — they don't fill the plate, but they define the dish. In post-harvest engineering, condiments are **"Tiny but Mighty Crops"**: low moisture, high bioactive density, and extremely sensitive to heat and light. A peppercorn contains ~3% essential oil — losing 1% through poor drying means a 33% loss in aroma value.
>
> Similarly, flowers for processing are **"Nature's Chemical Factories"** — each petal is a microscopic reactor producing fragrance molecules (terpenes, phenylpropanoids) that evaporate within hours of harvest. This is why rose petals are plucked **before dawn** — to capture maximum essential oil content.

## 1.4 Characteristics and Properties of Horticultural Crops Important for Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **CORE PRINCIPLE (KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight)**
> The success of any post-harvest process (drying, extraction, peeling, freezing) depends on understanding the **physical, thermal, mechanical, chemical, and biological properties** of the raw material. These properties determine **equipment selection, process parameters, and final product quality.**

### A. Physical Properties

| Property | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|----------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Size & Shape** | Linear dimensions, sphericity | Determines screen size, sorter capacity |
| **Bulk Density (ρ_b)** | Mass per unit volume of bulk | Silo/bin design, transport |
| **True Density (ρ_t)** | Mass per unit true volume | Separation efficiency (air classification) |
| **Porosity (ε)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$ | Affects drying rate, air flow |
| **Surface Area / Volume Ratio** | Geometric descriptor | Critical for heat & mass transfer |
| **Color (L*, a*, b*)** | CIE Lab color values | Quality grading, sorting |
| **Moisture Content (% wb)** | Mass of water / total mass | Determines storage life, drying load |

### B. Thermal Properties

| Property | Unit | Typical Range (Horticultural Crops) |
|----------|------|-------------------------------------|
| Specific Heat (c_p) | kJ/kg·K | **3.0 – 4.0** |
| Thermal Conductivity (k) | W/m·K | **0.10 – 0.60** |
| Thermal Diffusivity (α) | m²/s | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | kJ/kg | ~**2260** (water at 100°C) |

### C. Mechanical Properties

- **Hardness** — Resistance to deformation (e.g., peppercorn vs. cardamom)
- **Firmness / Texture** — Penetration force (kg-f or N)
- **Fracture Force** — Required to break the seed/skin
- **Coefficient of Friction** — Critical for hopper and conveyor design

### D. Chemical & Biochemical Properties

- **Essential Oil Content** (%) — Determined by **Clevenger apparatus** (hydro-distillation)
- **Oleoresin Content** (%) — Solvent extraction (acetone/hexane)
- **Moisture Content** — Affects microbial stability
- **pH & Titratable Acidity** — Determines blanching/preservation strategy
- **Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C)** — Oxidative browning indicator
- **Pectic Substances** — Protopectin → Pectin → Pectic acid (firmness loss)

### E. Biological / Physiological Properties

- **Respiration Rate (mg CO₂ / kg · h)** — Dictates cooling requirements
- **Ethylene Production Rate (µL/kg·h)** — Climacteric vs. non-climacteric classification
- **Transpiration Rate** — Post-harvest water loss

## 1.5 Peeling — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PEELING (KTU 2024 Module 4 Official Definition)**
> Peeling is the **unit operation of removing the outer skin (epidermis/pericarp) or inedible coating** of fruits, vegetables, or rhizomes using mechanical, thermal, chemical, or enzymatic means, in preparation for further processing (canning, drying, freezing, juice extraction, etc.).

**Why peeling is done:**
1. To **remove inedible/inedible portions** (skin, rind, outer bark)
2. To **improve texture** and eating quality
3. To **reduce microbial load** on the surface
4. To **enhance heat transfer** during subsequent processing
5. To **improve color, flavor, and appearance** of the end product
6. To **enable uniform drying** of slices/pieces

**Examples of crops requiring peeling:**
- **Potato, Sweet Potato** — Lye/abrasion peeling
- **Tomato** — Steam/flame peeling
- **Apple, Pear** — Mechanical/enzymatic peeling
- **Peach, Plum** — Lye peeling
- **Garlic, Onion** — Flame peeling
- **Ginger, Turmeric** — Abrasive peeling (rhizome skin)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Processing Properties of Condiments

### 2.1.1 Why Condiments are "Engineering-Challenging" Crops

Condiments have **very high surface area to volume ratio** and contain **volatile essential oils** that degrade at temperatures as low as **40–50°C**. This dictates the use of:
- **Low-temperature drying** (40–60°C cabinet drying)
- **Shade drying** for color retention (turmeric, chillies)
- **Cryogenic grinding** for spice powders (preserves volatile oils)

### 2.1.2 Critical Properties — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Moisture Content & Water Activity (a_w)**
- Spices must be dried to **<10% moisture** to prevent mold
- Water activity **a_w < 0.6** is required for safe storage
- *Why?* Below a_w = 0.6, no microbial growth occurs

**Step 2: Essential Oil Recovery**
- Clevenger apparatus uses **hydro-distillation** with water as the carrier
- Boiling water releases oil through **co-distillation**
- Oil is collected, separated (oil/water density difference), and measured

**Step 3: Oleoresin Extraction**
- Uses **organic solvents** (acetone, hexane, ethanol)
- After extraction, solvent is **recovered by evaporation**
- Yields a **concentrated, standardized flavoring matrix**

**Step 4: Aroma Retention in Grinding**
- Conventional grinding: **temperature rises to 60–90°C** → volatile oil loss
- **Cryogenic grinding** (using liquid nitrogen at **−196°C**):
  - Material becomes brittle
  - Fine grinding with **minimal heat generation**
  - Aroma retention improves by **30–70%**

## 2.2 Detailed Processing Properties of Flowers

| Property | Rose | Jasmine | Marigold | Saffron |
|----------|------|---------|----------|---------|
| Moisture (% wb) | 70–80 | 60–75 | 80–90 | 75–85 |
| Main Product | Rose oil (attar) | Concrete/absolute | Lutein pigment | Dried stigmas |
| Extraction Method | Steam distillation | Solvent (hexane) | Solvent/drying | Hand-picking + drying |
| Yield (%) | 0.02–0.05 | 0.10–0.30 | 5–10 (lutein) | 0.001 (stigma) |
| Optimal Harvest Time | Early morning | Pre-dawn | Fully open flowers | Sunrise (before wilting) |

### 2.2.1 Why Flower Crops are Highly Perishable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL REASONING — Why flowers are processed at farm-site:**
> 1. Flowers have **extremely high respiration rates** (50–200 mg CO₂/kg·h)
> 2. **Volatile fragrance compounds evaporate within 4–6 hours** of harvest
> 3. **Microbial spoilage begins within 24 hours** at ambient temperature
> 4. **Moisture loss >10%** causes wilting, petal browning, oil loss
> 5. Therefore, processing units must be **located within the farm cluster** (≤ 25 km radius)

## 2.3 PEELING — Comprehensive Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Classification of Peeling Methods

```
PEELING METHODS
│
├── A. MECHANICAL PEELING
│   ├── 1. Manual (Knife) Peeling
│   ├── 2. Abrasive / Roller Peeling
│   └── 3. Cup / Rotor Peeling
│
├── B. THERMAL PEELING
│   ├── 1. Steam Peeling
│   ├── 2. Hot Water / Blanching Peeling
│   ├── 3. Flame / Infrared Peeling
│   └── 4. Hot Air Peeling
│
├── C. CHEMICAL PEELING (Lye Peeling)
│   ├── 1. NaOH (Caustic Soda) — 1–10%
│   ├── 2. KOH
│   └── 3. Ca(OH)₂ (Lime water)
│
├── D. ENZYMATIC PEELING
│   └── (Pectinase / Cellulase cocktail)
│
└── E. EMERGING METHODS
    ├── 1. High Pressure Peeling
    ├── 2. Freeze Peeling
    ├── 3. Ultrasonic Peeling
    └── 4. Vacuum Steam Peeling
```

### 2.3.2 Detailed Mechanism of Each Method

**A. Mechanical Peeling:**

- **Knife Peeling:** Operator removes skin with hand-held blade. Peeling loss: **10–20%**
- **Abrasive Peeling:** Rotating carborundum rollers (roughness **40–80 grit**) scrape the skin. Used for potato, carrot, beetroot, ginger, turmeric.
- **Cup / Rotor Peeling:** Product tumbles against a stationary abrasive liner. Used for irregular shapes (knobby ginger, odd-shaped potatoes).

**B. Steam Peeling (Most common in industry):**
- Product exposed to **high-pressure steam at 100–300 psi (0.7–2.0 MPa)**
- Exposure time: **15–60 seconds**
- The **rapid steam condensation** beneath the skin **shatters the peel-skin interface**
- Skin is then removed by **rubber roller or water spray**
- Peeling loss: **5–15%**

**C. Hot Water / Lye Peeling:**
- Product immersed in **boiling 1–10% NaOH** for **30 s – 6 min**
- Temperature: **80–100°C**
- Lye **saponifies the cuticle** and **hydrolyzes pectin** in the peel
- After lye treatment, **cold water rinse** removes the loosened skin

**D. Flame Peeling:**
- Product passed over **gas flame (1100–1300°C) for 10–30 s**
- Used for **onion, garlic, tomato**
- Surface charring loosens the papery skin
- Loss: **5–10%**

**E. Enzymatic Peeling:**
- Pectinase enzyme cocktail (0.5–2%) applied at **35–50°C** for **15–60 min**
- Enzyme **hydrolyzes pectin** in the middle lamella
- Used for **citrus segments, apple, peach** (premium products)

### 2.3.3 Selection Criteria for Peeling Method

| Crop | Recommended Method | Reason |
|------|--------------------|--------|
| **Potato** | Steam / Lye | High-volume industrial scale |
| **Tomato** | Steam / Flame | Thin skin, soft texture |
| **Apple** | Mechanical / Enzymatic | Premium product, low loss |
| **Peach** | Lye | Fuzzy skin, firm flesh |
| **Carrot** | Abrasive | Smooth, cylindrical |
| **Ginger** | Abrasive (drum type) | Irregular knobby surface |
| **Onion** | Flame | Papery dry skin |
| **Citrus** | Enzymatic | Segments must be intact |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAM FOCUS — Memorize these formulas; 70% of numerical problems come from here**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{peel} = \dfrac{W_{peel}}{W_{peel}+W_{peelable}} \times 100$ | Peeling efficiency (%) | % |
| 2 | $L_{peel} = \dfrac{W_{sample} - W_{peeled}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | Peeling loss (%) | % |
| 3 | $Y_{peeled} = \dfrac{W_{peeled}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | Peeling yield (%) | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{drying} = \dfrac{M_1 - M_2}{M_1 - M_e} \times 100$ | Drying efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{h_{fg}}$ | Steam mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise temperature | kJ |
| 7 | $Q = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat for vaporization | kJ |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{10 \cdot m_{CO_2}}{W \cdot t}$ | Respiration rate | mg CO₂/kg·h |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | Porosity of bulk solid | dimensionless |
| 10 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity | dimensionless |
| 11 | $A_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | Surface area (cylinder) | m² |
| 12 | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot L$ | Volume (cylinder) | m³ |
| 13 | $\phi = \dfrac{d_e}{d_c}$ | Sphericity (diameter equivalent/critical) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Convective heat transfer | W |
| 15 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k}$ | Biot number (heat transfer regime) | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **WHERE YOU SEE THIS IN INDUSTRY (Recruitment Interview Material):**
> - **ITC Spices, MDH, Everest** — Cryogenic spice grinding (preserves 95% volatile oils)
> - **Dabur, Patanjali** — Rose water, gulkand, and herbal flower processing units
> - **Mother Dairy, Safal** — Industrial-scale steam peelers (10 tons/hour potato)
> - **Haldiram's, Bikaji** — Abrasive peeling of ginger, garlic at 500 kg/hour
> - **Kerala State Spices Board** — Cardamom drying using wood-fired cum solar driers
> - **Saffron (Iran/India)** — Pampore valley saffron uses shade drying of stigmas for 2–3 days
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peeling Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Aim:** To derive the universal peeling efficiency formula used in KTU exams and industry QC labs.

### Given Data (Standard Problem):
- Initial weight of sample: $W_s = 100$ g
- Weight of peel removed: $W_p = 18$ g
- Weight of remaining unpeeled material: $W_{up} = 3$ g
- Weight of perfectly peeled usable flesh: $W_f = 79$ g

### Step 1 — Identify the three output streams

After peeling, the sample divides into three distinct mass streams:

$$
W_s = W_p + W_{up} + W_f
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
The total sample splits into (a) peel removed, (b) usable flesh, and (c) **peelable flesh that remained attached** (this is the inefficiency — the machine did not remove all the skin).

### Step 2 — Define "Peelable Material"

$$
W_{peelable} = W_p + W_{up}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
The "peelable material" is everything that *should* ideally have come off. This includes both the peel successfully removed and the peelable portion still stuck on the product.

### Step 3 — Define Peeling Efficiency

$$
\eta_{peel} = \frac{\text{Peel successfully removed}}{\text{Total peelable material}} \times 100
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$
\eta_{peel} = \frac{W_p}{W_{peelable}} \times 100 = \frac{W_p}{W_p + W_{up}} \times 100
$$

### Step 5 — Plug in values

$$
\eta_{peel} = \frac{18}{18 + 3} \times 100 = \frac{18}{21} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{peel} = 85.71\%
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Peeling Loss

Peeling loss is the **usable flesh lost along with the peel**:

$$
L_{peel} = \frac{W_s - (W_{up} + W_f)}{W_s} \times 100
$$

In our case, since the three streams already sum to 100 g, the loss is incorporated implicitly. The *peeled yield*:

$$
Y_{peeled} = \frac{W_f}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{79}{100} \times 100 = 79\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAM TRAP — KTU students often confuse efficiency, loss, and yield. Memorize:**
> - **Efficiency** = How much of the peelable material was *actually* removed
> - **Loss** = How much good product was *wasted* during peeling
> - **Yield** = How much good product you *retained* as percentage of input

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steam Mass Required for Steam Peeling

> [!NOTE]
> **Aim:** To calculate the steam consumption of a continuous steam peeler processing 1 ton/hour of potatoes.

### Given:
- Throughput: $m = 1000$ kg/h = **0.278 kg/s**
- Steam pressure: **200 psi (1.38 MPa)**
- Steam temperature: **194°C**
- Latent heat of steam at 1.38 MPa: $h_{fg} = 2014$ kJ/kg
- Heat required per kg of potato to raise temperature by ΔT and partially gelatinize: $Q_{req} = 320$ kJ/kg (industry benchmark)

### Step 1 — Total heat to be supplied per second

$$
\dot{Q}_{req} = m \cdot Q_{req} = 0.278 \times 320
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{req} = 88.89 \; \text{kJ/s} = 88.89 \; \text{kW}
$$

### Step 2 — Steam mass flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{req}}{h_{fg}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{88.89}{2014} = 0.0441 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kg/h

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.0441 \times 3600 = 158.9 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 4 — Steam-to-product ratio

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{158.9}{1000} = 0.159 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 15.9\%)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INDUSTRY BENCHMARK:** Steam peeler typically uses **15–25% steam by weight** of the product. A 1 TPH line consumes ~160–250 kg/h of steam.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Respiration Rate Calculation

### Given:
- $\text{CO}_2$ produced: $V = 20$ mL
- Time: $t = 4$ h
- Sample mass: $W = 1$ kg

### Standard respiration rate formula:

$$
R = \frac{V_{CO_2} \times 10}{W \times t} \;\; \text{mg CO}_2/\text{kg·h}
$$

### Step 1 — Convert CO₂ volume to mass (at STP, 1 mol CO₂ = 22.4 L = 44 g)

$$
m_{CO_2} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{44}{22.4} = 0.0393 \; \text{g} = 39.3 \; \text{mg}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply formula

$$
R = \frac{39.3}{1 \times 4} = 9.825 \; \text{mg CO}_2/\text{kg·h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference for flower crops:** Rose = 15–30; Jasmine = 25–45; Marigold = 35–55 mg CO₂/kg·h. Therefore, this sample is **low-respiring** (could be a stored, low-temperature flower).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Peeling Method Comparison

| Criterion | Manual | Abrasive | Steam | Lye (NaOH) | Enzymatic | Flame |
|-----------|--------|----------|-------|------------|-----------|-------|
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High | Low |
| **Operating Cost** | High (labor) | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Low |
| **Peeling Loss (%)** | 10–20 | 8–15 | 5–15 | 5–20 | 3–8 | 5–10 |
| **Throughput** | Low | High | Very High | High | Low-Med | Medium |
| **Product Quality** | High (variable) | Good | Good | Good | Premium | Acceptable |
| **Wastewater** | None | Low | Low | **High (alkaline)** | Low | None |
| **Skill Required** | High | Low | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Best for** | Premium fruits | Potato, carrot, ginger | Potato, tomato, peach | Tomato, peach, plum | Citrus, apple | Onion, garlic |

## 3.5 Numerical — Drying Efficiency for Condiment

### Given:
- Initial moisture: $M_1 = 80\%$ (wet basis)
- Final moisture: $M_2 = 8\%$ (wet basis)
- Equilibrium moisture: $M_e = 4\%$ (wet basis)

### Step 1 — Convert to dry basis (DB)

$$
M_{1,db} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.00 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry matter}
$$

$$
M_{2,db} = \frac{0.08}{1 - 0.08} = 0.087 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry matter}
$$

$$
M_{e,db} = \frac{0.04}{1 - 0.04} = 0.0417 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry matter}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply drying efficiency formula

$$
\eta_{drying} = \frac{M_{1,db} - M_{2,db}}{M_{1,db} - M_{e,db}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{drying} = \frac{4.000 - 0.087}{4.000 - 0.0417} \times 100 = \frac{3.913}{3.958} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{drying} = 98.86\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **EXAM TIP:** Always show DB conversion; KTU evaluators explicitly check this step (2 marks allotment).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Condiment Processing Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Harvested Spices at Farm Gate] --> A1[Cleaning and Washing]
    A1 --> A2[Sorting and Grading]
    A2 --> A3[Pre-conditioning Blanching or Curing]
    A3 --> A4[Drying Cabinet or Solar or Fluidized Bed]
    A4 --> A5[Moisture Reduction to less than 10 percent wb]
    A5 --> A6[Cryogenic or Ambient Grinding]
    A6 --> A7[Sieving and Classification]
    A7 --> A8[Packaging under Nitrogen or Vacuum]
    A8 --> A9[Storage at Low Humidity]
    A9 --> A10[Final Product Whole Powder Oleoresin Essential Oil]
```

**Caption (KTU 2024):** Block flow of a typical spice/condiment processing line, showing the sequential unit operations from harvest to finished product.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Flower Processing (Rose as Model Crop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Pre-dawn Harvest of Rose Petals] --> B1[Sorting and Petal Separation]
    B1 --> B2[Washing in Cold Water]
    B2 --> B3[Steam Distillation Unit]
    B3 --> B4[Hydrosol Collection]
    B4 --> B5[Oil-Water Separator Clevenger Type]
    B5 --> B6[Rose Oil Attar Storage]
    B5 --> B7[Rose Water Bottling]
    B1 --> B8[Shade Drying on Wire Mesh]
    B8 --> B9[Dried Petals for Gulkand or Potpourri]
```

**Caption (KTU 2024):** Dual-pathway processing of rose petals — distillation route for oil/water, drying route for gulkand/potpourri.

## 4.3 Peeling Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Start Choose Peeling Method] --> C1{Product Type}
    C1 -->|Root or Tuber| C2{Scale}
    C2 -->|Large Industry| C3[Steam Peeling 100 to 300 psi]
    C2 -->|Small Industry| C4[Abrasive Roller Peeling]
    C1 -->|Fleshy Fruit Apple Pear| C5{Quality Priority}
    C5 -->|Premium Low Loss| C6[Enzymatic Pectinase]
    C5 -->|Mass Production| C7[Mechanical Cup Peeler]
    C1 -->|Soft Fruit Tomato Peach| C8{Texture}
    C8 -->|Soft| C9[Steam or Lye Peeling]
    C8 -->|Firm| C10[Lye NaOH 1 to 3 percent]
    C1 -->|Rhizome Ginger Turmeric| C11[Abrasive Drum Peeler with Water Spray]
    C1 -->|Onion Garlic| C12[Flame Peeling 1100 to 1300 degree C]
```

**Caption (KTU 2024):** Decision tree used by food process engineers to select the appropriate peeling method based on product and processing scale.

## 4.4 Process Flow — Lye (Chemical) Peeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Raw Tomatoes or Peaches] --> D1[Sorting]
    D1 --> D2[Lye Bath NaOH 2 to 10 percent at 90 to 100 degree C]
    D2 --> D3[Immersion Time 30 s to 6 min]
    D3 --> D4[Mechanical Agitation or Steam Jets]
    D4 --> D5[Loosened Skin Removal by Water Spray]
    D5 --> D6[Neutralization in Dilute Citric Acid 0.5 percent]
    D6 --> D7[Final Rinse in Cold Water]
    D7 --> D8[Peeled Product Ready for Canning or Drying]
    D2 --> D9[Alkaline Wastewater Treatment before Discharge]
```

**Caption (KTU 2024):** Detailed block flow of lye peeling, including critical alkaline wastewater treatment step (regulatory compliance).

## 4.5 Process Flow — Cryogenic Spice Grinding (High-Yield Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Dried Spices Moisture less than 8 percent] --> E1[Pre-cooling Chamber minus 50 degree C]
    E1 --> E2[Liquid Nitrogen Injection minus 196 degree C]
    E2 --> E3[Pin Mill or Hammer Mill Cryogenic Grade]
    E3 --> E4[Grinding at Minus 100 degree C Brittle Failure]
    E4 --> E5[Sieving to Desired Mesh 60 to 100]
    E5 --> E6[Volatile Oil Recovery from Mill Gases]
    E6 --> E7[Packaging Vacuum or Nitrogen Flushed]
    E3 --> E8[Cold Nitrogen Gas Recirculation]
```

**Caption (KTU 2024):** Cryogenic grinding flow — preserves heat-sensitive essential oils, which is the key reason condiment industries adopt this technology.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the physical and chemical properties of horticultural crops that are important for processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structure expected):**

*(1 mark) Physical properties:* Size, shape, bulk density, true density, porosity, color (CIE Lab), and surface area-to-volume ratio. These determine grading, sorting, drying, and storage equipment design.

*(1 mark) Chemical properties:* Moisture content, water activity, essential oil content, oleoresin content, pH, titratable acidity, pectin content, and ascorbic acid. These determine preservation strategy, extraction yield, and nutritional retention.

*(1 mark) Significance:* The combination of these properties dictates the **selection of processing method, equipment, process parameters, and final product quality**. For example, high moisture flowers need rapid drying to retain essential oil, while low-moisture spices need cryogenic grinding to retain volatiles.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between essential oil and oleoresin? Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Essential Oil | Oleoresin |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Definition | Volatile, aromatic fraction obtained by steam/water distillation | Complete flavor extract obtained by solvent extraction |
| Composition | Terpenes, terpenoids (volatile) | Essential oil + non-volatile resins, fixed oils, pigments |
| Method | Hydro-distillation / Steam distillation | Solvent extraction (hexane, acetone, ethanol) |
| Example | Clove oil (eugenol) | Pepper oleoresin (piperine + volatile oils) |
| Application | Perfumery, aromatherapy | Standardized food flavoring |

*(1 mark)* Essential oils are the **volatile, steam-distillable aroma fraction** (e.g., clove oil, cardamom oil).

*(1 mark)* Oleoresins are the **complete solvent-extracted flavor matrix** containing both volatile oils and non-volatile resins (e.g., pepper oleoresin, turmeric oleoresin).

*(1 mark)* Industrial significance: Oleoresins offer **standardized flavor strength, longer shelf life, and microbial safety** over raw spice powders.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE question from a Module. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Classify the different methods of peeling used in food processing. Explain **steam peeling** and **lye (NaOH) peeling** with their operating parameters, mechanisms, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit processing industry uses a continuous **steam peeler** to process **2 tons/hour** of tomatoes at **150 psi pressure** (latent heat of steam = 2018 kJ/kg). If the heat required per kg of tomato to peel effectively is **280 kJ/kg**, calculate:
  - (i) The total heat required per hour. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The steam consumption rate in kg/h. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The steam-to-product ratio. **(1 mark)**
  - (iv) Comment on how this ratio would change if you switch to **lye peeling** instead. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Peeling is the removal of inedible outer skin of fruits/vegetables to prepare them for further processing. The major methods are **mechanical, thermal, chemical, enzymatic, and emerging (high-pressure, ultrasonic)**.

**Steam Peeling (3 marks):**
- **Principle:** High-pressure steam (100–300 psi) is released onto the product for 15–60 seconds. The rapid condensation of steam beneath the skin creates a pressure differential that **shears the skin off the underlying flesh**.
- **Operating Parameters:** Pressure 100–300 psi; Exposure time 15–60 s; Product temperature rises to 80–95°C.
- **Mechanism:** Latent heat of steam condenses → water penetrates the peel-flesh interface → peel softens and separates.
- **Equipment:** Continuous rotary steam peeler or batch chamber.
- **Advantages:** Uniform peeling; low labor; handles large volumes (5–10 TPH); peeling loss only 5–15%.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost; requires boiler; over-cooking may occur at long exposures.

**Lye (NaOH) Peeling (2.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Hot alkali solution (1–10% NaOH at 90–100°C) **saponifies the waxy cuticle** and **hydrolyzes pectin** in the middle lamella of the peel, causing it to detach.
- **Operating Parameters:** 1–10% NaOH; 80–100°C; 30 s to 6 min immersion.
- **Mechanism:** Saponification of cutin + alkaline hydrolysis of pectin → skin detaches.
- **Equipment:** Lye bath with conveyor + neutralization tank.
- **Advantages:** Excellent for fuzzy fruits (peach, apricot); high throughput; low peeling loss.
- **Limitations:** Generates **alkaline wastewater** (must be neutralized with acid before discharge); potential Na⁺ residue on product; surface softening of flesh.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Steam peeling is preferred for **tomato and potato**; lye peeling for **peach, apricot, plum**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Throughput: $m = 2000$ kg/h
- Latent heat of steam: $h_{fg} = 2018$ kJ/kg
- Heat required per kg of tomato: $q = 280$ kJ/kg

**(i) Total heat required per hour (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{total} = m \times q = 2000 \times 280
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 560{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h}
$$

**(ii) Steam consumption rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{560{,}000}{2018}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 277.5 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**(iii) Steam-to-product ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{m} = \frac{277.5}{2000} = 0.139
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Steam-to-product ratio} = 0.139 \text{ or } 13.9\%}
$$

**(iv) Lye peeling comparison (2 marks):**

In **lye peeling**, the heat requirement is much lower because the **chemical reaction (saponification) does the peel-loosening work** rather than thermal energy. The lye bath needs only to maintain a temperature of **~95°C** with a residence time of **2–3 min**. Steam consumption in a lye line is typically **0–5% of product weight** (only for maintaining bath temperature). So switching to lye peeling would reduce the steam-to-product ratio to **about 0.03–0.05 (3–5%)** — i.e., **steam consumption drops by 60–75%**. However, the trade-off is that the industry must install a **wastewater neutralization system** to handle the alkaline effluent (compliance with Pollution Control Board norms).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Discuss the **characteristics and properties of horticultural crops important for processing**, with reference to:
  - (i) Physical properties (size, shape, density, porosity, color) **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Thermal properties (specific heat, thermal conductivity) **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Biochemical/chemical properties (moisture, essential oil, oleoresin, respiration) **(3 marks)**

**(b)** A rose processing unit receives **500 kg of fresh rose petals per day** at **moisture content 78% (wb)**. The petals are dried to a **final moisture of 10% (wb)**. Calculate:
  - (i) The mass of dried petals obtained per day. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The mass of water removed per day. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) If the rose oil yield is **0.03% of fresh petal weight**, calculate the daily rose oil production. **(2 marks)**
  - (iv) State **three processing properties** that are critical for flower processing and justify each. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Physical properties (2 marks):**
- **Size & Shape:** Determines screen apertures, sorter capacity, and grader settings. Sphericity $\phi$ affects rolling behavior in dryers.
- **Bulk density $\rho_b$, True density $\rho_t$, Porosity $\varepsilon$:** Critical for designing silos, hoppers, and drying-air flow rates. Porosity affects air-flow resistance in fluidized bed dryers.
- **Color (L*, a*, b*):** Used for quality sorting; browning or yellowing indicates deterioration.

**(ii) Thermal properties (2 marks):**
- **Specific heat $c_p$ (3.0–4.0 kJ/kg·K):** Determines the energy needed to raise the product temperature during blanching, drying, or steam peeling.
- **Thermal conductivity $k$ (0.10–0.60 W/m·K):** Governs the rate of heat penetration during thermal processing; lower $k$ means slower internal heating.
- **Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho \cdot c_p)$:** Controls how quickly temperature changes propagate through the product.

**(iii) Biochemical/chemical properties (3 marks):**
- **Moisture content & water activity ($a_w$):** Most important single property. Flowers with $a_w > 0.9$ spoil rapidly; spices must be dried to $a_w < 0.6$ for safe storage.
- **Essential oil content:** Determines flavoring strength; measured by **Clevenger hydro-distillation** (BT: 30 min at 100°C). Critical for rose, jasmine, cardamom, clove.
- **Oleoresin content:** Complete flavor profile (volatile + non-volatile); used for standardized industrial flavoring.
- **Respiration rate (mg CO₂/kg·h):** Dictates cooling requirements post-harvest. Climacteric flowers (rose, jasmine) need immediate cold storage.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Fresh petal mass: $M_{fresh} = 500$ kg/day
- Initial moisture: $M_1 = 78\%$ wb
- Final moisture: $M_2 = 10\%$ wb
- Oil yield: $0.03\%$ of fresh weight

**(i) Mass of dried petals (2 marks):**

Dry matter in fresh petals:
$$
M_{dry} = M_{fresh} \times \frac{100 - M_1}{100} = 500 \times \frac{100 - 78}{100} = 500 \times 0.22
$$

$$
M_{dry} = 110 \;\text{kg of dry matter}
$$

This dry matter remains in the dried petals. So the dried petal mass is:
$$
M_{dried} = \frac{M_{dry}}{(1 - M_2)} = \frac{110}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{110}{0.90}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{dried} = 122.22 \;\text{kg/day}}
$$

**(ii) Mass of water removed (2 marks):**

Initial water content:
$$
W_{initial} = 500 \times 0.78 = 390 \;\text{kg of water}
$$

Final water content:
$$
W_{final} = 122.22 \times 0.10 = 12.22 \;\text{kg of water}
$$

Water removed:
$$
\boxed{W_{removed} = 390 - 12.22 = 377.78 \;\text{kg/day}}
$$

**(iii) Daily rose oil production (2 marks):**

$$
M_{oil} = M_{fresh} \times \frac{0.03}{100} = 500 \times 0.0003
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{oil} = 0.15 \;\text{kg/day} = 150 \;\text{g/day}}
$$

**(iv) Three critical processing properties (1 mark — 1 mark total for any 3 correct points):**

1. **High respiration rate (40–60 mg CO₂/kg·h)** — Justification: Flowers must be processed within 4–6 hours; otherwise 30–50% aroma compounds are lost.
2. **High moisture content (75–85% wb)** — Justification: Drying is energy-intensive; low-temperature drying (40–50°C) is needed to retain volatile oils.
3. **High fragility of petals** — Justification: Mechanical handling must be minimized; conveyor systems should be cushioned (PVC food-grade belts) to avoid bruising and oil loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls on this Topic**
> 1. **Confusing "Peeling efficiency" with "Peeling yield":** Efficiency = how completely the peel is removed; Yield = how much edible product you retain. Students swap them and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert moisture content from wet-basis to dry-basis** in drying calculations. KTU evaluators specifically allocate **1 mark** for the conversion step.
> 3. **Not specifying the units in steam mass flow calculations.** Always state the result in **kg/h** with the time basis clearly written.
> 4. **Writing generic answers for "properties of horticultural crops."** KTU expects the answer categorized into **Physical, Thermal, Chemical/Biochemical, Biological** with **one example per category**.
> 5. **Omitting wastewater treatment** in lye peeling answers. KTU has a strong question trend on **environmental compliance** — losing 1–2 marks if you don't mention alkaline effluent treatment.
> 6. **In Cryogenic Grinding** — many students write "uses liquid oxygen" by mistake. The correct cryogen is **liquid nitrogen (LN₂, −196°C)**, never LOX (fire hazard).

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Re-read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Condiments** = plant-derived flavoring materials (whole/ground/extracted); 4 categories: pungent, aromatic, colouring, herbs.
- [x] **Flower crops for processing** = rose, jasmine, marigold, chrysanthemum, saffron, calendula, tulsi.
- [x] **5 property groups for processing:** Physical, Thermal, Chemical, Biochemical, Biological.
- [x] **Peeling** = removal of inedible skin via mechanical, thermal, chemical, enzymatic, or emerging methods.
- [x] **5 peeling methods:** Manual, Abrasive, Steam, Lye (NaOH), Enzymatic, Flame (6 actually).
- [x] **Steam peeling:** 100–300 psi, 15–60 s; loss 5–15%.
- [x] **Lye peeling:** 1–10% NaOH, 80–100°C, 30 s – 6 min; loss 5–20%; **alkaline wastewater** is a major issue.
- [x] **Abrasive peeling:** Carborundum rollers 40–80 grit; ideal for potato, carrot, ginger.
- [x] **Enzymatic peeling:** Pectinase cocktail 0.5–2%, 35–50°C; lowest loss 3–8%; premium quality.
- [x] **Flame peeling:** 1100–1300°C, 10–30 s; for onion, garlic.
- [x] **Peeling efficiency formula:** $\eta_{peel} = W_p / (W_p + W_{up}) \times 100$.
- [x] **Peeling loss formula:** $L_{peel} = (W_s - W_{peeled}) / W_s \times 100$.
- [x] **Respiration rate formula:** $R = (10 \times m_{CO_2}) / (W \times t)$ in mg CO₂/kg·h.
- [x] **Porosity:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$.
- [x] **Moisture dry-basis conversion:** $M_{db} = M_{wb} / (1 - M_{wb})$.
- [x] **Drying efficiency formula:** $\eta_{drying} = (M_1 - M_2)/(M_1 - M_e) \times 100$.
- [x] **Steam mass flow:** $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{Q}_{req} / h_{fg}$.
- [x] **Steam-to-product ratio for steam peeler** ≈ 0.13–0.20 (13–20%).
- [x] **Cryogenic grinding** uses **liquid nitrogen** (NOT liquid oxygen) at −196°C.
- [x] **Condiment drying target:** moisture < 10% wb, $a_w$ < 0.6.
- [x] **Flower processing window:** 4–6 hours post-harvest (respiration-driven aroma loss).
- [x] **Saffron:** world's costliest spice; yield 0.001% of stigma; manual picking.
- [x] **Clevenger apparatus:** for essential oil measurement by hydro-distillation.
- [x] **Oleoresin** = essential oil + resins + pigments (solvent-extracted).
- [x] **Six KTU-favoured crops for peeling questions:** Potato, Tomato, Apple, Peach, Carrot, Ginger.
- [x] **Alkaline wastewater** from lye peeling must be **neutralized with citric or sulfuric acid** before discharge.
- [x] **KTU expected answer structure** for 7-mark questions: **Definition (1M) → Mechanism (2M) → Equipment (1.5M) → Parameters (1.5M) → Advantages/Limitations (1M)**.
- [x] **For 14-mark calculation questions** — always show **3-step solution:** Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer with units.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Condiments and Flowers, Characteristics and Properties, Peeling**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Slicing** is a fundamental **size reduction operation** in post-harvest engineering wherein a food material (typically fruits, vegetables, tubers, or roots) is mechanically cut into **thin, uniform sections** of predetermined thickness using a sharp-edged cutting tool. Unlike dicing (which produces cubes) or shredding (which produces strips), slicing yields **planar pieces** with two large surfaces and a controlled uniform thickness, primarily governed by the clearance between a stationary blade and a moving feed mechanism.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, slicing is classified under **mechanical separation / comminution** unit operations and is distinct from **chopping**, **mincing**, or **pureeing** because the cellular structure of the produce is intentionally preserved in sheet-like geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Slicing is a **primary size-reduction technique** that directly influences the **drying kinetics**, **mass transfer rates**, and **final product quality** (color, texture, rehydration ratio) of processed horticultural crops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine slicing a loaf of bread with a bread knife — each downward stroke produces a **uniform thin sheet** that retains the structural integrity of the loaf. Now apply the same principle to a mango, potato, carrot, or banana, but at **industrial scale** with **mechanized precision**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of slicing like using a **guillotine paper cutter** in an office. The thickness of paper cut depends on how far the clamp sits from the blade. Similarly, in industrial slicers, a **reciprocating or rotary blade** cuts the produce as it advances on a **conveyor or rotating disc**, and the **gap between the blade and a feed roller** determines slice thickness.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally applied in slicing operations:

- **Slice Thickness (t):** Typically **1 mm to 25 mm** for most horticultural produce
- **Cutting Velocity (V):** Range: **0.5 m/s to 8 m/s**
- **Compression Ratio (λ):** Ratio of original thickness to slice thickness, **λ ≥ 1**
- **Angle of Slice (θ):** **15° to 45°** relative to the cutting plane
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** **0.3 to 0.6** for most fresh produce against stainless steel

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are critical when calculating the **cutting force**, **energy requirements**, and **throughput** of slicing machinery used in KTU-endorsed processing plants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Slicing Operation

Slicing is governed by the **shear-cutting mechanism**, where a sharp blade exerts a **concentrated compressive force** on a small contact area, generating internal shear stress that exceeds the **shear strength** of the cellular matrix of the produce.

The operational sequence is as follows:

1. **Feeding:** The produce is transported to the cutting zone via a **conveyor belt**, **gravity chute**, or **rotating disc feeder**.
2. **Clamping/Holding:** A pressure roller or feed roller holds the produce against a fixed reference surface to prevent slippage.
3. **Cutting:** The blade (stationary, reciprocating, or rotary) traverses the produce, severing tissue along the cutting plane.
4. **Discharge:** The sliced piece is conveyed away for further processing (e.g., drying, frying, packaging).

### 2.2 Classification of Slicers

Slicing machines are classified based on the **blade motion principle**:

| **Type of Slicer** | **Blade Motion** | **Best Suited For** | **Typical Capacity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotary Slicer** | Rotating disc with knives | Potatoes, carrots, beetroot | 500 – 5000 kg/hr |
| **Reciprocating Slicer** | Back-and-forth stroke | Cabbage, leafy vegetables | 200 – 2000 kg/hr |
| **Centrifugal Slicer** | High-speed impeller with blades | Vegetables, fruits for drying | 1000 – 10000 kg/hr |
| **Gravity-Fed Slicer** | Fixed blade, product falls | Soft fruits, mushrooms | 100 – 500 kg/hr |
| **Urschel Slicer (Comitrol)** | High-speed rotating knives | Uniform precise cubes/slices | 500 – 8000 kg/hr |

### 2.3 Theoretical Basis of Cutting Force

The **cutting force (F)** required to slice a horticultural product is determined by **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion**, adapted for food materials:

$$F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c$$

Where:
- $F_c$ = Cutting force (N)
- $\sigma_s$ = Shear strength of the produce (N/m² or Pa)
- $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area at the cut (m²)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Force | $F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c$ | N | Basic force calculation |
| Energy per Cut | $E = F_c \cdot d$ | J | Energy consumed per slice |
| Power Requirement | $P = F_c \cdot v$ | W | Motor sizing |
| Throughput | $Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | kg/s | Machine capacity |
| Slice Thickness | $t = \dfrac{v_f}{N \cdot f}$ | m | Design geometry |
| Compression Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{T_0}{t}$ | dimensionless | Size reduction ratio |
| Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ | Pa | Material resistance |
| Rittinger's Constant (K) | $E \propto \dfrac{1}{d} - \dfrac{1}{D}$ | — | Size reduction energy |
| Angle of Repose Effect | $\tan(\theta) = \mu_s$ | — | Feed stability |
| Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | J/kg | Efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symbols:** In the formulas above, $v_f$ = feed velocity, $N$ = number of blades, $f$ = frequency of rotation, $T_0$ = original thickness, $D$ = initial size, $d$ = final slice size, $\mu_s$ = static friction coefficient.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Slicing is the **gateway operation** in numerous post-harvest value chains:

- **Dehydration Industry:** Potato chips, banana chips, jackfruit chips, onion flakes
- **Canning Industry:** Pineapple slices, mango slices, carrot discs
- **Frozen Food Sector:** Pre-sliced vegetables for ready-to-cook meals
- **Pickle & Preserve Industry:** Lime slices, ginger slices, garlic slices
- **Essential Oil & Spice Industry:** Turmeric slicing, ginger slicing for oleoresin extraction

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutting Force for a Rotary Slicer

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the **cutting force per blade** in a rotary slicer cutting a cylindrical root vegetable of diameter $D$ at slice thickness $t$.

**Given:**
- Product: Carrot (cylindrical geometry)
- Diameter: $D$ (m)
- Slice thickness: $t$ (m)
- Shear strength of carrot: $\sigma_s$ (Pa)

**Step 1 — Identify the cross-sectional area at the cutting plane:**

For a cylindrical vegetable, the blade intersects the cross-section perpendicular to the axis. The cutting area is therefore:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

This is simply the circular cross-section of the carrot.

**Step 2 — Apply the shear failure criterion:**

The cutting force required to sever the carrot cross-section is:

$$F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c$$

Substituting Step 1:

$$F_c = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Account for multiple blades:**

If the rotary disc carries $N$ blades and rotates at frequency $f$ (rev/s), the **number of cuts per second** is $N \cdot f$. The instantaneous force per blade remains $F_c$, but the **total force transmitted** is distributed.

**Step 4 — Total power requirement:**

Each blade traverses a chord length approximated by $D$, moving at peripheral velocity $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f$:

$$P = F_c \cdot v = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f$$

Simplifying:

$$P = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot \sigma_s \cdot D^3 \cdot N \cdot f}{4}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Requirement Using Rittinger's Law

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the **energy required to slice 1000 kg of potatoes** from an average whole potato diameter of $D = 60$ mm to slice thickness $d = 3$ mm.

**Given:**
- Mass: $m = 1000$ kg
- Initial size: $D = 0.060$ m
- Final size: $d = 0.003$ m
- Rittinger's constant for potato: $K_R = 113.3$ kJ·mm/kg (literature value)

**Step 1 — State Rittinger's Law:**

$$E = K_R \cdot m \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{D} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E = 113.3 \cdot 1000 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{60} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{3} = 0.3333 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{60} = 0.01667 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{D} = 0.3333 - 0.01667 = 0.3166 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute final energy:**

$$E = 113300 \cdot 0.3166 = 35870.78 \text{ kJ}$$

Thus, the **energy required is approximately 35.87 MJ** for slicing 1 tonne of potatoes.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Slicer Types

| **Criterion** | **Rotary** | **Reciprocating** | **Centrifugal** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Slice Uniformity | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| Mechanical Damage | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Maintenance | Moderate | High | High |
| Capital Cost | Medium | Low | High |
| Power per kg | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Best Produce | Tubers, roots | Leafy, bulky | Fruits, small vegetables |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Rotary Slicer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper Loading] --> B[Feed Conveyor]
    B --> C[Feed Roller Clamp]
    C --> D[Rotary Disc with Blades]
    D --> E[Sliced Product Discharge]
    D --> F[Blade Sharpening Unit]
    E --> G[Collection Tray]
    G --> H[Conveyor to Drying or Packaging]
    F --> I[Drive Motor Coupling]
    I --> D
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart maps the **linear process flow** of a rotary slicing machine — raw produce enters the hopper, is transported and clamped, gets sliced by the rotating disc, and the slices are discharged for downstream operations.

### 4.2 Cutting Mechanics Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drive Motor Power Input P_in] --> B[Power Transmission via Belt or Gearbox]
    B --> C[Rotary Disc Rotational Velocity omega]
    C --> D[Blade Engages Produce at Cutting Plane]
    D --> E[Shear Failure of Cellular Matrix]
    E --> F[Slice Detachment and Discharge]
    F --> G[Energy Dissipated as Heat and Sound]
    style A fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#e59866,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#cb4335,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#7d3c98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the **energy transformation chain** during slicing — electrical/mechanical power is converted to kinetic energy of the disc, which is then transferred as shear stress to overcome the produce's shear strength, ultimately resulting in slice formation with associated losses (heat, sound, vibration).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define slicing. Mention any two industrial applications of slicing in post-harvest processing.*

**Model Answer:**
Slicing is a **mechanical size reduction operation** in which food materials are cut into **thin, uniform sections** of predetermined thickness using a sharp blade. The slice thickness is controlled by the **clearance between the cutting blade and the feeding mechanism**.
- **Application 1:** Production of **potato chips** where uniform 1.5–2 mm slices are essential for consistent frying and dehydration.
- **Application 2:** Preparation of **pineapple rings** for canning, where uniform 8–10 mm slices ensure even syrup penetration and heat transfer during sterilization.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the different types of slicing machines based on blade motion.*

**Model Answer:**
Based on blade motion, slicing machines are classified as:
1. **Rotary Slicers** — Rotating disc with blades (used for tubers).
2. **Reciprocating Slicers** — Back-and-forth blade motion (used for cabbage, leafy vegetables).
3. **Centrifugal Slicers** — High-speed impeller with radial blades (used for fruits).
4. **Gravity-Fed Slicers** — Fixed blade, product moves by gravity (used for soft fruits).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **rotary slicer**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A rotary slicer has a disc of **0.5 m diameter** carrying **6 blades**, rotating at **300 rpm**. The shear strength of carrots is **1.2 MPa** and average carrot diameter is **40 mm**. Calculate the **cutting force per blade** and **total power required**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
A rotary slicer consists of:
- A **horizontal rotating disc** mounted on a vertical shaft driven by an electric motor.
- **6–12 adjustable blades** bolted on the upper surface of the disc at fixed radial positions.
- A **feed hopper** with a **feed roller** that clamps the produce against a stationary curved surface above the disc.
- A **discharge chute** placed tangentially to collect sliced pieces.
- An optional **sharpening attachment** for blade maintenance.

**Working:**
The produce is loaded into the hopper and forced by gravity and the feed roller onto the spinning disc. As each blade sweeps past, it slices the produce into uniform thickness. The slice thickness equals the **vertical clearance** between the blade tip and the stationary feed surface. Slices are flung outward by centrifugal force into the discharge chute.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Cutting area of one carrot:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.040)^2}{4}$$

$$A_c = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Cutting force per blade:**

$$F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c = 1.2 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_c = 1507.96 \text{ N} \approx 1.51 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Peripheral velocity of disc:**

$$v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot \frac{300}{60}$$

$$v = \pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 5 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Power per blade:**

$$P_{\text{blade}} = F_c \cdot v = 1507.96 \cdot 7.854 = 11843.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Total power for 6 blades:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 6 \cdot 11843.5 = 71061 \text{ W} \approx 71.06 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that **peripheral velocity uses the disc diameter (0.5 m)**, not the carrot diameter. Many also mistake **rpm** for **rps** — always convert: $N (\text{rps}) = \dfrac{N_{\text{rpm}}}{60}$. Marks are commonly lost in unit conversion errors.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **principle of cutting** in slicing operations. Derive the expression for **specific energy consumption** in slicing. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using **Rittinger's law**, calculate the **energy required to slice 500 kg of mango** from an initial whole size of **80 mm** to slice thickness of **5 mm**. Given $K_R = 95.4$ kJ·mm/kg. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Principle of Cutting:**
Slicing operates on the principle of **shear failure**. A sharp blade concentrates applied force over a small contact area, generating **shear stress** within the produce. When this stress exceeds the **shear strength** of the cellular matrix, the material fractures along the cutting plane.

**Derivation of Specific Energy:**

The energy consumed per unit mass of produce sliced is the **specific energy** $E_s$:

$$E_s = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{F_c \cdot d}{m}$$

Where $d$ is the slice thickness. Substituting $F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c$ and $m = \rho \cdot V$ where $V$ is the volume of produce:

$$E_s = \frac{\sigma_s \cdot A_c \cdot d}{\rho \cdot A_c \cdot T_0} = \frac{\sigma_s \cdot d}{\rho \cdot T_0}$$

For $N$ slices per unit time at mass flow rate $\dot{m}$, the **power-based specific energy** is:

$$E_s = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} \quad \text{(J/kg)}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Rittinger's Law:**

$$E = K_R \cdot m \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{D} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$E = 95.4 \cdot 500 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{80} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{5} = 0.2000, \quad \frac{1}{80} = 0.0125$$

$$0.2000 - 0.0125 = 0.1875 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$E = 47700 \cdot 0.1875 = 8943.75 \text{ kJ}$$

$$E \approx 8.94 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Students frequently write the Rittinger equation with **incorrect sign convention**. Always remember it is $\left(\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{D}\right)$, NOT $\left(\frac{1}{D} - \frac{1}{d}\right)$. Also ensure $K_R$ units (kJ·mm/kg) are consistent with $d$ and $D$ in **mm**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Slicing** is a **planar size reduction** producing uniform thin sections; thickness is governed by **blade-to-feed clearance**.
- Slicers are classified as **rotary**, **reciprocating**, **centrifugal**, and **gravity-fed** based on blade motion.
- Cutting force $F_c = \sigma_s \cdot A_c$ — derived from **Mohr-Coulomb shear failure**.
- Power $P = F_c \cdot v$ where $v$ is **peripheral velocity** of the blade.
- **Rittinger's Law** is preferred over Kick's or Bond's laws for slicing (a fine size-reduction operation).
- Throughput $Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ depends on **density, cross-section, and blade velocity**.
- Compression ratio $\lambda = T_0 / t$ indicates the degree of size reduction.
- Industrial applications: **chips, canning, drying, pickling, freezing, oleoresin extraction**.
- Always convert **rpm → rps** before substituting in formulas.
- The constant $K_R$ is **produce-specific** (potato ≈ 113, mango ≈ 95, carrot ≈ 80 kJ·mm/kg).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blanching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Blanching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Blanching

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blanching** is a **pre-processing thermal treatment** in which fruits, vegetables, or cereals are subjected to a short-duration exposure to hot water, steam, hot air, or dielectric/microwave heating, typically at temperatures between **75 °C and 100 °C**, for a defined residence time (usually **1 to 10 minutes**, depending on product geometry and thermal diffusivity). The primary engineering purpose is the **inactivation of deteriorative native enzymes** (particularly *peroxidase*, *catalase*, *polyphenol oxidase*, and *lipoxygenase*), partial expulsion of intercellular gases, tissue softening, and stabilization of pigments prior to subsequent preservation operations such as freezing, dehydration, canning, or oil extraction.

The process is governed by the principles of **unsteady-state heat transfer** into a porous biological solid, coupled with **first-order enzyme inactivation kinetics**, where the residual enzyme activity $A_t$ after time $t$ is expressed as:

$$A_t = A_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

where $k$ is the **thermal inactivation rate constant** (s⁻¹) and $A_0$ is the initial activity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are about to store freshly plucked spinach leaves in your freezer. If you put them in raw, they will turn brown, mushy, and develop off-flavors after a few weeks — because invisible worker-enzymes inside the leaves are still "alive" and slowly breaking down chlorophyll, proteins, and lipids even at freezer temperatures. **Blanching is like giving these leaves a brief, controlled "hot shower"** that knocks out (denatures) these enzymes without cooking the food. Think of it as a **defensive pre-treatment**: you sacrifice a little color/texture now to save a lot of quality later. For a Kerala student, this is exactly like briefly dipping banana chips in hot water before frying them — to prevent discoloration and improve crispness.

## 1.3 Why Blanching is Critical in Post-Harvest Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Objectives of Blanching (KTU Board Favourite List):**
> 1. **Enzyme inactivation** — primarily *peroxidase* (most heat-stable indicator enzyme).
> 2. **Gas expulsion** — removal of intercellular O₂ and CO₂ reduces oxidative rancidity and shrinkage during drying.
> 3. **Texture fixation** — partial pectin solubilization stabilizes cell walls against later mechanical damage.
> 4. **Microbial load reduction** — surface vegetative microbial count drops by **1–2 log cycles (90–99 %)**.
> 5. **Wax and pesticide removal** — important for fruits like mango, apple, and capsicum.
> 6. **Improved drying/canning efficiency** — moisture equilibration and uniform rehydration behavior.

## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Hot water blanch temperature | 70 – 100 | °C |
| Steam blanch temperature | 100 – 105 | °C |
| Residence time (leafy vegetables) | 30 – 90 | seconds |
| Residence time (root/dense tissues) | 2 – 10 | minutes |
| Peroxidase residual activity target | < 5 (often < 10) | % |
| Catalase residual activity target | < 5 | % |
| Cold water quench (post-blanch) | 10 – 20 | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Peroxidase (POD) is used as the **marker enzyme** for blanching adequacy because it has the **highest thermal resistance** among food enzymes. If POD is inactivated, virtually all other deteriorative enzymes are also inactivated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biochemical Basis of Enzyme Inactivation

Enzymes are globular proteins. When their tertiary structure unfolds under thermal stress, the **active site** is destroyed and catalytic function is lost. This process follows **first-order kinetics** for dilute enzyme systems.

### 2.1.1 First-Order Inactivation Model

The fractional residual activity at any instant is:

$$\frac{A_t}{A_0} = e^{-k_d \cdot t}$$

The temperature dependence of $k_d$ follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k_d = k_{d,\text{ref}} \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $E_a$ = activation energy of inactivation (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.1.2 Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)

The **D-value** is the time required at a given temperature to reduce the enzyme activity by **90 %** (i.e., to 10 % of original).

$$D_T = \frac{\ln(10)}{k_d} = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

The **Z-value** is the temperature rise required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10.

$$\log\left(\frac{D_{T_1}}{D_{T_2}}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Peroxidase in vegetables:** Typical $Z$ ≈ 30 – 45 °C and $D_{90°C}$ ≈ 3 – 7 minutes depending on pH and matrix.

## 2.2 Heat Transfer Perspective

The product (say a carrot cube of side $a$) heats from the surface inward. The **Biot number** decides whether lumped or distributed analysis applies.

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k_s}$$

For typical blanching: $\text{Bi} \approx 0.5$ to $5$, hence **distributed unsteady-state conduction** dominates.

The **Fourier number** quantifies dimensionless time:

$$\text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha \cdot t}{L_c^2}$$

Transient center-temperature response for a slab:

$$\theta^* = \frac{T_c - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = 2 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{n+1} \frac{\sin(\lambda_n)}{\lambda_n} \exp(-\lambda_n^2 \cdot \text{Fo})$$

with eigenvalues $\lambda_n = (2n-1)\pi / 2$ for a slab of half-thickness $L$.

## 2.3 Mass Transfer During Blanching

### 2.3.1 Moisture Loss / Gain
- **Hot water blanch** → moisture **gain** (typically + 2 to + 8 %).
- **Steam blanch** → moisture **loss** (typically – 2 to – 6 %).

Mass transfer flux follows:

$$\dot{m} = k_m \cdot (C_s - C_\infty)$$

### 2.3.2 Leaching of Soluble Solids
Losses of sugars, vitamins (especially **ascorbic acid / Vitamin C**), and minerals occur with first-order kinetics:

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_L \cdot t}$$

For Vitamin C in green peas during water blanch, $k_L$ at 100 °C is about **0.08 – 0.12 min⁻¹**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Boundary | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Residual activity (1st order) | $A_t = A_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Arrhenius for $k_d$ | $k_d = k_{\text{ref}} \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}}-\dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | D-value | $D_T = \dfrac{2.303}{k_d}$ | s or min |
| 4 | Z-value relation | $\log(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2}) = (T_2 - T_1)/Z$ | °C |
| 5 | Bi number | $\text{Bi} = h L_c / k_s$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Fourier number | $\text{Fo} = \alpha t / L_c^2$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Vit-C loss (water blanch) | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_L t}$ | mg/100 g |
| 8 | Energy required | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m_{\text{latent}}$ | kJ |
| 9 | Peroxidase index | residual activity $\leq 5\%$ of original | % |
| 10 | Cooling load | $Q_c = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{blanch}} - T_{\text{quench}})$ | kJ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food industry**: pre-treatment in IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) lines for peas, corn, cauliflower.
- **Oil seeds**: microwave/steam blanching of soybeans before solvent extraction **inactivates lipoxygenase**, preventing off-flavors and improving oil quality.
- **Dehydration industry**: blanching of onion, garlic, and potato slices before drying **reduces drying time by 20 – 35 %**.
- **Canning industry**: ensures vacuum formation and texture integrity in retort-pouched products.
- **Cereal industry**: steam blanching of parboiled rice (paddy) is a form of partial blanching used for milling yield improvement.

## 2.6 Effects on Nutritional Quality

> [!NOTE]
> **Nutrient Loss Ranking (Most → Least Sensitive):**
> Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) > Thiamine (B1) > Folic acid > Carotenoids > Riboflavin > Minerals.
> - Water blanching causes the **highest losses** of water-soluble nutrients.
> - **Steam blanching preserves 80–90 %** of Vitamin C compared to 50–70 % in hot water.
> - **Microwave blanching** preserves the **maximum** nutritional quality (90–95 %) due to short residence time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Enzyme Inactivation Kinetics

**Starting Principle:** Enzyme inactivation occurs by a unimolecular denaturation reaction:

$$E_{\text{native}} \xrightarrow{k_d} E_{\text{inactive}}$$

**Step 1:** Write the rate of disappearance of active enzyme:

$$\frac{d[E]}{dt} = -k_d [E]$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables and integrate from $t=0$ (where $[E]=A_0$) to $t=t$ (where $[E]=A_t$):

$$\int_{A_0}^{A_t} \frac{d[E]}{[E]} = -\int_{0}^{t} k_d \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{A_t}{A_0}\right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{A_t}{A_0} = e^{-k_d t}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for residual activity $A_t$:

$$\boxed{A_t = A_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}}$$

**Step 6:** Define D-value as time for $A_t/A_0 = 0.10$:

$$0.10 = e^{-k_d D_T} \;\Rightarrow\; \ln(10) = k_d D_T \;\Rightarrow\; D_T = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Required to Reach 3 % Residual Peroxidase Activity at 90 °C

**Given:** $k_d$ at 90 °C for peroxidase in carrots ≈ **0.32 min⁻¹**.

**Step 1:** Set residual fraction equal to 0.03:

$$\frac{A_t}{A_0} = 0.03 = e^{-0.32 t}$$

**Step 2:** Take natural log:

$$\ln(0.03) = -0.32 \, t$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerically: $\ln(0.03) = -3.507$.

$$-3.507 = -0.32 \, t$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t$:

$$t = \frac{3.507}{0.32} = 10.96 \text{ min}$$

**Result:** Blanching carrots at 90 °C for **≈ 11 minutes** is needed to bring peroxidase activity down to 3 % — sufficient for safe freezing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Required to Blanch 100 kg of Green Peas from 25 °C to 95 °C (Hot Water Method)

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 100$ kg
- $c_p$ of peas ≈ **3.55 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $T_i = 25$ °C, $T_{\text{blanch}} = 95$ °C

**Step 1:** Sensible heat to raise product temperature:

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 100 \times 3.55 \times (95 - 25)$$

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 100 \times 3.55 \times 70 = 24{,}850 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2:** Heat to maintain bath at 95 °C (losses to surroundings) — assume **10 %** of sensible heat:

$$Q_{\text{losses}} = 0.10 \times 24{,}850 = 2{,}485 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Heat to raise incoming cold water from 25 °C to 95 °C for bath make-up (assume **20 kg** water):

$$Q_{\text{makeup}} = 20 \times 4.18 \times (95 - 25) = 5{,}852 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4:** Total heat required:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 24{,}850 + 2{,}485 + 5{,}852 = 33{,}187 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5:** If steam at 1.5 bar (latent heat ≈ 2,226 kJ/kg) is used:

$$\text{Steam required} = \frac{33{,}187}{2{,}226} \approx 14.9 \text{ kg steam per batch}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This calculation is routinely asked in KTU numerical problems. Always include **sensible + losses + bath makeup** components in the energy balance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Blanching Methods

| Property | Hot Water | Steam | Microwave | Hot Air / IR | Chemical |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (°C) | 75 – 100 | 100 – 105 | Internal 95 – 105 | 100 – 200 | 50 – 70 |
| Time | 1 – 10 min | 0.5 – 5 min | 30 s – 3 min | 1 – 6 min | 5 – 20 min |
| Nutrient retention | Low (50–70 %) | High (80–90 %) | Very high (90–95 %) | Medium (70–85 %) | High |
| Texture loss | Moderate | Low | Very low | Low | Negligible |
| Water pollution | Yes (effluent) | Minimal | None | None | Possible |
| Energy efficiency | Low | Medium | High (volumetric) | High | Low |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Low |
| Peroxidase inactivation | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Variable |
| Typical application | Peas, corn, carrots | Leafy vegetables | Premium frozen foods | Onion, garlic flakes | Mushroom, frozen veg |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Peroxidase Z-Value from Two D-values

**Given:**
- $D_{80} = 30$ min
- $D_{90} = 6$ min

**Step 1:** Apply Z-value formula:

$$Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2})} = \frac{90 - 80}{\log(30/6)}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate log ratio: $\log(5) = 0.699$.

$$Z = \frac{10}{0.699} = 14.3 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 14.3 °C rise in temperature causes a 10-fold reduction in the time needed to inactivate peroxidase. This is why industrial blanching shifts from 70 °C to 95 °C — huge time savings.

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Biot and Fourier Numbers for a Potato Cube

**Given:**
- Cube side $a = 20$ mm = 0.02 m
- Thermal conductivity of potato $k_s = 0.55$ W/(m·K)
- Surface heat transfer coefficient $h = 500$ W/(m²·K)
- Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s
- Blanching time $t = 300$ s
- Characteristic length $L_c = a/6 = 0.00333$ m (for a cube)

**Step 1:** Calculate Bi:

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k_s} = \frac{500 \times 0.00333}{0.55} = 3.03$$

**Step 2:** Since Bi > 0.1, lumped analysis fails — distributed heating needed.

**Step 3:** Calculate Fourier number:

$$\text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{-7} \times 300}{(0.00333)^2} = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.11 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.78$$

**Step 4:** From slab Heisler chart at Bi ≈ 3, Fo ≈ 3.8, center temperature ratio:

$$\theta^* \approx 0.20$$

$$T_c = T_\infty + \theta^*(T_i - T_\infty) = 95 + 0.20(25 - 95) = 95 - 14 = 81 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Conclusion:** The cube center has reached **81 °C** after 5 min — enough for peroxidase inactivation at the slowest-heating point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Architecture)

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Industrial Blanching Line

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the complete process flow of a typical **steam-blanching line** as installed in a frozen vegetable processing plant (e.g., peas or cauliflower in Kerala agro-industries).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Raw Material Receiving Hopper] --> A1[Pre-Wash Rotary Drum]
    A1 --> A2[Sorting and Grading Belt]
    A2 --> A3[Size Reduction Cutter or Slicer]
    A3 --> B1[Steam Blancher Tunnel 100 C]
    B1 --> C1[Spray Cooling Section]
    C1 --> C2[Immersion Cold Water Tank 10 C]
    C2 --> D1[Vibratory Dewatering Screen]
    D1 --> D2[Inspection Conveyor with Peroxidase Spot Test]
    D2 --> E1[Next Stage Freezing or Drying or Canning]
    S1[Steam Boiler 1.5 bar] -. supplies latent heat .-> B1
    S2[Chilled Water Chiller 5 C] -. supplies cooling .-> C2
    Q1[Effluent Treatment for Condensate] -. collects discharge .-> B1
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Blanching Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0{Product Type and End Use}
    P0 -->|Leafy vegetables IQF| R1[Steam Blanch]
    P0 -->|Root vegetables dehydrated| R2[Hot Water Blanch]
```
