T_f) \\ Q_1 &= 0.35 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \times 80 \text{ m}^2 \times (38 - 13) \text{ K} \\ Q_1 &= 0.35 \times 80 \times 25 \\ Q_1 &= 700 \text{ W} = 0.700 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Heat load due to respiration of produce ( $Q_2$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_2 &= m \cdot q_r \\ Q_2 &= 5{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.05 \text{ W/kg} \\ Q_2 &= 250 \text{ W} = 0.250 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Infiltration load ( $Q_3$ ): > Assume infiltration volume $V_{inf} = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$/opening, $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_3 &= V \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot n \\ Q_3 &= 2.5 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times (38 - 13) \times 8 \\ Q_3 &= 2.5 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 25 \times 8 \\ Q_3 &= 603 \text{ W} = 0.603 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Fan motor heat ( $Q_4$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_4 &= \frac{P_{fan}}{\eta} = \frac{600}{0.85} \\ Q_4 &= 705.88 \text{ W} \approx 0.706 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Latent load from transpiration ( $Q_5$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_5 &= m_w \cdot h_{fg} \\ Q_5 &= \frac{0.6 \text{ kg/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \times 2{,}460{,}000 \text{ J/kg} \\ Q_5 &= 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.46 \times 10^6 \\ Q_5 &= 410 \text{ W} = 0.410 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Total Heat Load: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 \\ Q_{total} &= 0.700 + 0.250 + 0.603 + 0.706 + 0.410 \\ Q_{total} &= 2.669 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Required refrigeration capacity in TR (Tons of Refrigeration): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Capacity} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{3.517} \\ &= \frac{2.669}{3.517} \\ &= 0.759 \text{ TR} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineer's Insight:** Apply a **safety factor of 1.5** to account for solar gain, aging compressor, and partial loading → **Required Reefer Capacity ≈ 1.14 TR**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Thin-Layer Drying Model (Page's Equation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Favourite:** "Derive the Page's thin-layer drying model" appears in *almost every* KTU 2024 Scheme exam paper. **Starting Point — Newton's Law of Cooling applied to drying:** The drying rate is proportional to the difference between the moisture content at time $t$ and the equilibrium moisture content: $$ -\frac{dM}{dt} = k(M - M^*) $$ **Step 1 — Separation of variables: $$ \begin{aligned} -\frac{dM}{M - M^*} &= k \cdot dt \\ \int_{M_0}^{M_t} -\frac{dM}{M - M^*} &= \int_0^t k \, dt \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Integration (using $\int dx/x = \ln x$): $$ \begin{aligned} -\left[ \ln(M - M^*) \right]_{M_0}^{M_t} &= k \cdot t \\ -\ln(M_t - M^*) + \ln(M_0 - M^*) &= k \cdot t \\ \ln\left( \frac{M_0 - M^*}{M_t - M^*} \right) &= k \cdot t \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Exponentiation: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{M_0 - M^*}{M_t - M^*} &= e^{k t} \\ \frac{M_t - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} &= e^{-k t} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Define Moisture Ratio (MR): $$ \begin{aligned} MR &= \frac{M_t - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} = e^{-k t} \quad \text{...(Newton's Model)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Empirical Modification by Page (1949): Page observed that Newton's model under-predicted drying for many biological materials. He modified the time term by an exponent **$n$**: $$ \begin{aligned} MR &= e^{-k t^{n}} \quad \text{...(Page's Model)} \end{aligned} $$ > **Why "n" is introduced:** To capture the **internal moisture diffusion resistance** that dominates the falling-rate period. For most fruits & vegetables, **$n$ lies between 0.5 and 1.5**. **Step 6 — Linearization for finding $k$ and $n$ (the KTU trick): $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(MR) &= -k \cdot t^{n} \\ \ln[-\ln(MR)] &= \ln(k) + n \cdot \ln(t) \end{aligned} $$ This is in the form $Y = C + n X$ — a straight line, where plotting $\ln[-\ln(MR)]$ vs $\ln(t)$ gives slope $= n$ and intercept $= \ln(k)$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Drying Rate Calculation **Problem:** A 100 kg batch of sliced mango (initial MC = 85% w.b.) is dried in a tray dryer. After 4 hours, MC drops to 25% w.b. Calculate: 1. Initial & final MC in dry basis 2. Mass of water removed 3. Average drying rate (assume exposed area = 5 m²) **Step 1 — Convert to Dry Basis: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{0,db} &= \frac{85}{100 - 85} = \frac{85}{15} = 5.667 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid} \\ M_{t,db} &= \frac{25}{100 - 25} = \frac{25}{75} = 0.333 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Mass of dry solid in batch: $$ \begin{aligned} W_d &= W_0 \times (1 - M_{wb,i}) \\ W_d &= 100 \times (1 - 0.85) = 15 \text{ kg dry solid} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Initial & Final mass of water: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{w,0} &= 100 - 15 = 85 \text{ kg} \\ W_{w,t} &= 0.333 \times 15 = 4.995 \approx 5.0 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Water removed: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta W_w &= 85 - 5.0 = 80 \text{ kg of water} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Average Drying Rate: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \frac{\Delta W_w}{A \cdot t} = \frac{80}{5 \times 4} \\ N &= 4.0 \text{ kg water/m}^2\text{h} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A drying rate of **4 kg/m²·h** is typical of a **forced-convection tray dryer at 60–70 °C** for mango. A solar dryer would give only **0.5–1.0 kg/m²·h**. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **Define the term "Cold Chain". List any four essential components of a cold chain system used for horticultural produce.** **Model Answer:** > **Cold Chain Definition:** A cold chain is a continuous, temperature-controlled supply chain that maintains a prescribed low temperature regime from the point of harvest to the point of consumption, ensuring quality retention and shelf-life extension of perishable commodities. > > **Four Essential Components:** > 1. **Pre-cooling facility** (for rapid removal of field heat) > 2. **Cold storage warehouse** (for short- to long-term holding) > 3. **Refrigerated transport vehicles** (reefer trucks/containers) > 4. **Distribution cold rooms and retail display cabinets** (for last-mile delivery) > 5. *(Optional 5th)* Temperature monitoring & data logging system (IoT-based) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** **Differentiate between a Cabinet Dryer and a Spray Dryer. Mention two products each that are dried using them.** **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **Cabinet Tray Dryer** | **Spray Dryer** | |---------------|------------------------|-----------------| | **Form of feed** | Solid slices, pieces, granules | Liquid slurry, solution, emulsion | | **Drying mechanism** | Convective hot air through trays | Atomization + hot air contact | | **Drying time** | 4 – 12 hours | 5 – 30 seconds | | **Product form** | Whole, sliced, flaked | Free-flowing powder | | **Products (2 each)** | Banana chips, apple rings, mango leather | Tomato powder, milk powder, fruit juice powder | --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]** > **A. (a)** Explain the constructional features of a refrigerated transport vehicle (reefer). With a neat diagram, describe the working principle of its refrigeration system. **(7 Marks)** > > **A. (b)** A reefer truck carries 5,000 kg of mangoes. The ambient temperature is 38 °C and the desired cargo temperature is 13 °C. Compute the **total heat load** given: $U A = 28 \text{ W/K}$, respiration heat $q_r = 50 \text{ mW/kg}$, door openings = 6/h, infiltration volume = 2 m³ per opening, fan motor = 500 W ($\eta = 0.8$), and latent load = 0.4 kW. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for A(a):** The constructional features of a reefer vehicle are explained step-by-step: 1. **Outer Shell:** Made of corrugated aluminium (1 mm) for high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, and ease of cleaning. 2. **Insulation Core:** Polyurethane Foam (PUF) of density 40–50 kg/m³, thickness 80–120 mm. PUF has the lowest thermal conductivity (~0.022 W/m·K) among commercially available insulators. 3. **Vapour Barrier:** A multi-layer FSK (Foil-Scrim-Kraft) laminate is installed on the *warm side* of the insulation to prevent water vapour from migrating into the PUF, which would otherwise degrade its insulating value. 4. **Inner Shell:** Food-grade stainless steel (SS 304) or aluminium, with smooth welded seams to prevent bacterial harbourage. 5. **T-Bar Floor:** Reinforced aluminium T-section channels run lengthwise on the floor to create a **plenum** for uniform chilled air distribution. 6. **Refrigeration Unit:** Mounted on the front wall of the container, typically a **vapour-compression system** with compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator. 7. **Air-Circulation System:** Internal fans draw warm air from the top of the cargo, blow it through the evaporator coils, and discharge cold air under the T-bar floor. 8. **Instrumentation:** Digital thermostat, data logger, GPS-based temperature telemetry, and door-open sensors. **Working of Vapour Compression Cycle (Step-by-Step):** - **Step 1 — Compression:** Low-pressure refrigerant vapour from the evaporator is drawn into the compressor and compressed to high pressure (and high temperature). - **Step 2 — Condensation:** The hot, high-pressure vapour passes through the condenser (usually air-cooled, mounted on the front of the reefer) where it rejects heat to the atmosphere and condenses to a liquid. - **Step 3 — Expansion:** The high-pressure liquid passes through a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) or capillary tube, where its pressure suddenly drops, causing partial flash evaporation and a sharp drop in temperature. - **Step 4 — Evaporation:** The cold, low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture passes through the evaporator coils inside the cargo area, where it absorbs heat from the return air, evaporates fully, and the cycle repeats. > **Diagram Reference:** (See SECTION 4.2 above for the schematic) **Model Solution for A(b):** > This is identical to the derivation in SECTION 3.1 above. Re-computing with the given data: **Step 1 — Wall load ( $Q_1$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_1 &= U A \cdot \Delta T = 28 \times (38 - 13) \\ Q_1 &= 28 \times 25 = 700 \text{ W} = 0.70 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Respiration load ( $Q_2$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_2 &= m \cdot q_r = 5{,}000 \times 0.050 = 250 \text{ W} = 0.25 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Infiltration ( $Q_3$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_3 &= V \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot n \\ &= 2 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 25 \times 6 \\ &= 361.8 \text{ W} \approx 0.36 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Fan heat ( $Q_4$ ): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_4 &= \frac{P_{fan}}{\eta} = \frac{500}{0.8} = 625 \text{ W} = 0.625 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Latent load ( $Q_5$ ): $$ Q_5 = 0.40 \text{ kW (given)} $$ **Step 6 — Total: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= 0.70 + 0.25 + 0.36 + 0.625 + 0.40 \\ &= 2.335 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ > **Final Answer:** The required refrigeration capacity of the reefer is **2.335 kW** (with safety factor 1.5 → **3.50 kW ≈ 1.0 TR**). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **B. (a)** Classify the different types of dryers used for fruits and vegetables. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **fluidized bed dryer**. Mention its merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)** > > **B. (b)** A thin-layer drying experiment on sliced pineapple gave the following data at 60 °C: initial MC = 4.0 (d.b.), MC at t = 60 min = 1.5 (d.b.), and equilibrium MC $M^* = 0.05$ (d.b.). Fit Newton's model and determine the drying constant $k$. Also find the time required to reach MC = 0.3 (d.b.). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for B(a):** **Classification of Dryers (Refer to Section 4.3 above for the visual tree):** 1. **Solar Dryers:** Open sun, cabinet solar, tunnel solar 2. **Hot-Air Convection Dryers:** Tray, tunnel, belt, fluidized bed, spray, drum, pneumatic 3. **Vacuum Dryers:** Vacuum tray, vacuum belt 4. **Specialty Dryers:** Freeze dryer, osmotic, microwave, infrared, heat pump, foam-mat, Refractance Window **Fluidized Bed Dryer (FBD) — Working Principle:** In a fluidized bed dryer, hot air is passed *upward* through a bed of particulate material (diced vegetables, peas, corn kernels) at a velocity high enough to **suspend the particles** in the air stream. The particles behave like a boiling fluid — hence the name "fluidized". **Constructional Components:** - **Air heater** (electric/gas/steam) - **Blower** (to supply high-volume, low-pressure air) - **Perforated distributor plate** (at the bottom of the drying chamber) - **Drying chamber** (vertical cylindrical or rectangular) - **Cyclone separator** (to recover entrained fines) - **Bag filter / wet scrubber** (exhaust cleaning) **Working Steps:** 1. Wet feed (e.g., diced carrot, peas) is charged onto the perforated distributor plate. 2. Hot air is blown upward through the plate at a velocity equal to or slightly above the **minimum fluidization velocity** ($U_{mf}$). 3. The particles become suspended and the entire mass behaves like a fluid — providing **intense particle-air contact**. 4. Heat is transferred from air to particles, water evaporates, and the particles are simultaneously dried and conveyed upward. 5. Dried particles exit at the top; fines are recovered via cyclone; moist exhaust air is vented after filtration. **Merits:** - Very high heat & mass transfer coefficients → **drying time reduced by 70–80%** vs. tray dryer - Uniform drying — no hot spots - Suitable for **free-flowing, granular materials** (peas, diced veg, corn) - Continuous operation possible **Demerits:** - Not suitable for **sticky, pasty, or large-slice products** - High **power consumption** for the blower - **Attrition / particle breakage** may occur - Capital cost higher than tray dryers **Model Solution for B(b):** **Step 1 — Compute Moisture Ratios: $$ \begin{aligned} MR_0 &= \frac{M_0 - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} = 1.000 \quad \text{(at t = 0)} \\ MR_{60} &= \frac{1.5 - 0.05}{4.0 - 0.05} = \frac{1.45}{3.95} = 0.3671 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Model: $MR = e^{-k t}$ $$ \begin{aligned} 0.3671 &= e^{-k \cdot 60} \\ \ln(0.3671) &= -60 k \\ -1.0011 &= -60 k \\ k &= 0.01669 \text{ min}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Time to reach $M_t = 0.3$ (d.b.): $$ \begin{aligned} MR_{target} &= \frac{0.3 - 0.05}{4.0 - 0.05} = \frac{0.25}{3.95} = 0.06329 \\ 0.06329 &= e^{-0.01669 \cdot t} \\ \ln(0.06329) &= -0.01669 \cdot t \\ -2.7602 &= -0.01669 \cdot t \\ t &= \frac{2.7602}{0.01669} = 165.4 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned} $$ > **Final Answer:** Drying constant **$k \approx 0.0167 \text{ min}^{-1}$** and time to reach MC = 0.3 d.b. is **~165.4 min (~2 hours 45 min)**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $t$ from hours to minutes in Page's equation. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for unit errors. > 2. **Wet vs. Dry Basis Confusion:** Always state the basis (w.b. or d.b.) clearly. Conversion formulas are different. > 3. **Reefer Capacity Units:** Never write the answer in TR without first showing the intermediate kW value. > 4. **Forgetting the Safety Factor (1.2–1.5):** In real design, a safety factor is mandatory. Examiners reward it. > 5. **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($M^*$):** If ignored (set to 0) in Page's/Newton's model, the model becomes inaccurate and loses **2 marks**. > 6. **Dryer Selection Justification:** Always justify *why* a particular dryer is chosen — based on **feed form, heat sensitivity, capital cost, scale of operation, and desired product form**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔹 **Cold Chain** = Pre-cooling → Cold Storage → Reefer → Cold Room → Retail → Consumer - 🔹 **Reefer** = Insulated container + Vapour Compression Refrigeration + Air-circulation - 🔹 **Insulation** = **PUF (40–50 kg/m³, 80–120 mm thick)** + FSK vapour barrier - 🔹 **Vapour Compression Cycle** = Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator - 🔹 **$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5$** (walls + respiration + infiltration + fan + latent) - 🔹 **$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$** — remember this cold chain conversion - 🔹 **Drying** = simultaneous heat + mass transfer to reduce **$a_w < 0.6$** - 🔹 **Drying periods** = (i) Initial transient (ii) Constant rate (iii) **Falling rate (dominates in foods)** - 🔹 **Newton's model:** $MR = e^{-k t}$ ; **Page's model:** $MR = e^{-k t^n}$ (where $0.5 < n < 1.5$) - 🔹 **Moisture content w.b. → d.b.:** $M_{db} = M_{wb} / (100 - M_{wb})$ - 🔹 **Cabinet Dryer** — for solid slices; **Spray Dryer** — for liquid → powder - 🔹 **Fluidized Bed Dryer** — best for free-flowing granular products - 🔹 **Freeze Dryer** — best quality (preserves colour, flavour, nutrients) but highest cost - 🔹 **Osmotic Dehydration** — pre-treatment using sugar/salt solution to reduce energy in subsequent drying - 🔹 **Heat Pump Dryer** — most energy-efficient modern technology - 🔹 **Horticultural temperature zones:** Strawberry (0 °C) → Mango (13 °C) → Banana (13–15 °C) → Coconut (ambient) - 🔹 **For F&V drying**, falling-rate period is the **most critical** (internal diffusion controlled)

topics.title: Osmo-Dehydration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Osmo-Dehydration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Osmo-Dehydration
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Osmotic Dehydration (OD)** is a water removal process in which water-bearing cellular plant tissue (fruits, vegetables, or horticultural produce) is immersed in a **hypertonic (concentrated) aqueous solution** of an osmotic agent — typically sucrose for fruits and sodium chloride (NaCl) for vegetables. The **chemical-potential gradient of water** across the selectively permeable cell membrane drives a counter-current mass transfer: **water migrates out** of the product into the solution, while a controlled amount of **osmotic solute diffuses into** the product.

The process is *partial* — it does not reduce moisture content sufficiently for safe long-term storage by itself; rather, it is used as a **pre-treatment** before drying, freezing, frying, or canning to improve product quality, color retention, texture, and energy efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Osmotic dehydration is treated under "Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF) and Minimal Processing." Examineers frequently ask students to (i) state the principle, (ii) explain the driving force, (iii) list the operating parameters, and (iv) solve problems based on **Water Loss (WL)**, **Solid Gain (SG)**, and **Weight Reduction (WR)** equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Raisin in Honey"

Imagine slicing fresh grapes and dropping them into a small bowl of thick, concentrated sugar syrup (like honey). After a few hours, the grape slices become soft, slightly sweet, and noticeably lighter — they have shrunk. What you have just performed, in essence, is **osmotic dehydration**:

- The grape tissue is **> 80% water**.
- The sugar syrup is **hypertonic** — its water concentration is very low.
- The cell walls act as a **semi-permeable membrane**, allowing water to leak out but restricting large sugar molecules from rushing in.
- Result: a partially dried, partially impregnated product — an **intermediate-moisture food (IMF)**.

The same principle — but in reverse — explains why a salted cucumber (cucumber + NaCl brine) becomes limp and watery: the concentrated salt pulls water out of the vegetable cells.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hypertonic solution** | A solution whose osmotic pressure is *higher* than that of the cell sap. |
| **Hypotonic solution** | A solution of *lower* osmotic pressure (causes water uptake). |
| **Isotonic solution** | Equal osmotic pressure (no net transfer). |
| **Osmotic agent (osmoticum)** | The solute used to build osmotic pressure (sucrose, NaCl, glucose, corn syrup, glycerol). |
| **Water activity (a_w)** | Ratio of partial vapour pressure of water in the food to that of pure water; OD lowers a_w. |
| **Equilibrium moisture content** | Moisture content when the product and solution are at chemical-potential equilibrium. |
| **Solution-to-sample ratio (SSR)** | Mass of osmotic solution divided by mass of fresh product; typically kept **≥ 4 : 1** to keep solution concentration approximately constant. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OD is favoured by industry:** It operates at **ambient or mild temperatures (30–60 °C)**, prevents heat damage to colour and flavour, retains volatile aroma compounds, and consumes **far less thermal energy** than hot-air drying because the bulk of water is removed non-thermally.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Driving Force — Why Water Moves

When a fruit piece is dropped into a concentrated sucrose solution (e.g., **60 °Brix**), two chemical potentials no longer match:

$$\mu_w^{\text{cell}} \; > \; \mu_w^{\text{solution}}$$

Water spontaneously flows from **high → low** chemical potential. Because the cell membrane is preferentially permeable to water, water escapes rapidly. Solute (sugar or salt) simultaneously diffuses inward at a much slower rate, since the membrane restricts large molecules and even small ions like Na⁺.

The driving force is often expressed as the **osmotic pressure difference (Δπ)**:

$$\Delta \pi = \pi_{\text{solution}} - \pi_{\text{product}}$$

For a dilute solute, the **van't Hoff approximation** gives:

$$\pi = i \, c \, R \, T$$

where *i* is the dissociation factor, *c* is molar concentration, *R* is the universal gas constant, and *T* is absolute temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For sucrose (non-dissociating), **i = 1**; for NaCl (fully dissociating), **i ≈ 2**.

### 2.2 Mass Transfer Mechanisms — Three Coupled Phenomena

Osmotic dehydration involves **three simultaneous mass fluxes** through the plant tissue:

1. **Water Loss (WL)** — bulk flow of liquid water (and some soluble solids of the cell sap) outward.
2. **Solid Gain (SG)** — diffusion of the osmotic solute (sugar/salt) into the tissue.
3. **Leaching of native solutes** — small molecules (organic acids, pigments, vitamins) of the original cell sap are also washed out into the solution.

The **net weight reduction (WR)** is therefore:

$$WR = WL - SG$$

WL and SG are expressed on the **initial fresh mass (M₀)** basis, exactly as required by KTU valuation keys.

### 2.3 Step-Wise Operating Logic of the OD Process

- **Step 1 — Preparation:** Wash, peel (optional), and cut the produce into uniform geometries (cubes, cylinders, slabs) to ensure predictable diffusion distances.
- **Step 2 — Pre-treatment (optional):** Blanching or dipping in CaCl₂ / KMS to inactivate enzymes and preserve texture.
- **Step 3 — Osmotic immersion:** Submerge the pieces in the osmotic solution under controlled temperature, concentration, and agitation.
- **Step 4 — Mass transfer:** Water exits; solute enters. Typically the rate is rapid in the first 30–60 minutes and slows as the concentration gradient flattens.
- **Step 5 — Removal & rinsing:** Drain product, rinse quickly with water to remove surface syrup/salt.
- **Step 6 — Post-treatment:** Cabinet/air drying, freeze-drying, or vacuum drying to complete moisture removal.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting the Rate of Osmotic Dehydration

| # | Factor | Effect on WL / SG |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solution concentration ↑** | WL ↑, SG ↑ (more driving force) |
| 2 | **Temperature ↑** (30 – 60 °C) | WL ↑, but very high T cooks tissue |
| 3 | **Immersion time ↑** | WL ↑ until equilibrium |
| 4 | **Agitation / stirring ↑** | WL ↑ (thins boundary layer) |
| 5 | **Geometry — smaller thickness** | WL ↑ (shorter diffusion path) |
| 6 | **Solution-to-sample ratio (SSR) ↑** | WL ↑ (keeps solution concentration stable) |
| 7 | **Vacuum pulse (Vacuum OD)** | WL ↑ sharply at the start |
| 8 | **Type of osmotic agent** | Sucrose: WL moderate, SG high; NaCl: WL high, SG low |
| 9 | **Pre-treatment (blanching, ultrasound, HHP)** | WL ↑ (cell-wall permeability ↑) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** When asked "List the factors affecting osmotic dehydration," always mention *at least* concentration, temperature, time, agitation, sample geometry, and SSR. Examiners allocate 1 mark per correct factor up to 4 marks.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle WL(\%) = \frac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0} \times 100$ | Water Loss at time *t* | % (w.b. on M₀) |
| 2 | $\displaystyle SG(\%) = \frac{S_t - S_0}{M_0} \times 100$ | Solid Gain at time *t* | % |
| 3 | $\displaystyle WR(\%) = WL - SG$ | Net Weight Reduction | % |
| 4 | $\displaystyle MR = \frac{W_t - W_e}{W_0 - W_e}$ | Moisture Ratio (for diffusion models) | — |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \ln(MR) = \ln(k) - n \ln(t)$ | Page's empirical model (log-log linear) | — |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \frac{WL_t}{WL_\infty} = 1 - \exp(-K\,t)$ | First-order kinetics (Azuara-style) | — |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty} = \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[-(2n+1)^2\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}\,t \,/\, 4L^2\right]$ | Crank's solution for infinite slab (Fickian diffusion) | — |
| 8 | $\displaystyle D_{\text{eff}} = \frac{0.049\,L^2}{t_{1/2}}$ | Diffusivity from half-time of moisture change | m²/s |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \pi = i\,c\,R\,T$ | van't Hoff osmotic pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \text{SSR} = \frac{M_{\text{solution}}}{M_{\text{product}}}$ | Solution-to-sample ratio (kept ≥ 4) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the markdown tables above, vertical-bar absolute-value notation has been written as `\vert` so the table syntax never breaks. KTU boards *do not* deduct marks for this presentation choice.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Jams, preserves, candied fruits** (cherries, mango, pineapple): OD concentrates natural sugar and shortens the boiling time, saving energy and improving colour.
- **Prunes, dried apricots**: OD pre-treatment prevents case-hardening during air-drying.
- **Minimally processed fruits** (ready-to-eat papaya, melon): OD with mild sugar syrup preserves firmness and freshness.
- **Crisp vegetables for snack foods**: salt-based OD (e.g., carrot, okra, beetroot) reduces oil uptake during subsequent frying.
- **Aquatic and freeze-dried products**: OD as a pre-freeze treatment reduces drip loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Azuara-Type First-Order Kinetic Model

Many KTU problems give tabulated data of *WL* versus *t* and ask the student to fit a first-order model. The Azuara model is:

$$\frac{WL_t}{WL_\infty} = 1 - \exp(-K\,t)$$

This comes directly from the differential mass-balance:

$$\frac{dWL_t}{dt} = K\,(WL_\infty - WL_t)$$

Re-arrange:

$$\frac{dWL_t}{WL_\infty - WL_t} = K\,dt$$

Integrate from $WL = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $WL = WL_t$ at $t = t$:

$$\left[-\ln(WL_\infty - WL_t)\right]_0^{WL_t} = K\,t$$

That is:

$$-\ln(WL_\infty - WL_t) + \ln(WL_\infty) = K\,t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{WL_\infty}{WL_\infty - WL_t}\right) = K\,t$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{WL_\infty - WL_t}{WL_\infty} = \exp(-K\,t)$$

Finally rearrange to obtain the **KTU working form**:

$$WL_t = WL_\infty \bigl[1 - \exp(-K\,t)\bigr]$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Crank's Diffusion Solution for an Infinite Slab

The classic Fickian model assumes that diffusion is the rate-limiting step. For a slab of thickness $2L$ with initial uniform moisture and constant surface concentration, Fick's second law:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{\text{eff}}\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

with boundary conditions $C(\pm L, t) = C_e$ and $C(x, 0) = C_0$. The standard eigenfunction expansion (Crank, 1975) gives:

$$\frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty} = \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[\,-(2n+1)^2\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}\, t / 4L^2\,\right]$$

For *long immersion times* ($t$ large), only the $n = 0$ term survives, yielding the **linearised working equation** used in KTU numerical problems:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}}{4L^2}\,t$$

A plot of $\ln(MR)$ versus $t$ therefore yields a **straight line of slope $-\dfrac{\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}}{4L^2}$,** from which $D_{\text{eff}}$ is calculated.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Type 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:**
A batch of mango cubes (initial mass $M_0 = 200$ g, initial solid mass $S_0 = 36$ g) is osmotically dehydrated in 60 °Brix sucrose syrup at 40 °C with SSR = 5. After 90 minutes, the drained mass is $M_t = 158$ g and the solid mass in the cubes is $S_t = 56$ g. Determine (a) Water Loss %, (b) Solid Gain %, and (c) Weight Reduction %.

**Solution (Step-by-Step — board-exam format):**

**(a) Water Loss**

$$\text{WL} = \frac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0}\times 100$$

$$= \frac{200 - 158}{200}\times 100 = \frac{42}{200}\times 100 = 21.0\%$$

**(b) Solid Gain**

$$\text{SG} = \frac{S_t - S_0}{M_0}\times 100$$

$$= \frac{56 - 36}{200}\times 100 = \frac{20}{200}\times 100 = 10.0\%$$

**(c) Weight Reduction**

$$\text{WR} = \text{WL} - \text{SG} = 21.0 - 10.0 = 11.0\%$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Table (typical KTU expectation)

| Parameter (low → high) | ΔWL | ΔSG | ΔWR | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration (30 → 70 °Brix) | + + + | + + + | + + | Higher uptake; may over-sweeten |
| Temperature (25 → 60 °C) | + + | + + | + | Faster kinetics; risk of cooking |
| Agitation (none → 200 rpm) | + | + / 0 | + | Better uniformity; cost |
| SSR (2 → 10) | + | + / 0 | + | Keeps solution stable |
| Sample thickness (1 cm → 0.3 cm) | + + | + | + + | Higher surface area |
| Vacuum pulse (0 → 50 kPa, 5 min) | + + + | + | + + + | "Vacuum OD" — very fast initial WL |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The OD Process Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Produce Receipt and Grading] --> B[Cleaning and Washing]
    B --> C[Peeling, Cutting to Uniform Geometry]
    C --> D[Optional Pre-Treatment: Blanching, KMS dip, CaCl2]
    D --> E[Immersion in Hypertonic Solution in SS Tank]
    E --> F[Agitation and Temperature Control 30 to 60 C]
    F --> G[Counter-Current Mass Transfer: Water Out, Solute In]
    G --> H[Draining and Quick Rinse]
    H --> I[Surface Water Removal by Air Blast]
    I --> J[Post-OD Treatment: Cabinet Drying or Freeze Drying]
    J --> K[Packaging and Storage as IMF]

    E --> L[Recirculation Pump and Solution Reconditioning]
    L --> E

    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a83232,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffe2cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff2b2,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000

    style F fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#5f3a87,color:#000

    style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    style L fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mass-Transfer Schematic — Counter-Current Diffusion Across Cell Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cell Sap: High Water Activity, aw High] -->|Water diffuses out| P2[Cell Wall Semi-Permeable Membrane]
    P2 -->|Solute enters cell slowly| P3[Tissue After OD: aw Reduced, Solid Enriched]
    S1[Hypertonic Osmotic Solution: Low aw, High Osmotic Pressure] -->|Receives water from tissue| S2[Diluted Osmotic Solution]
    S2 -->|Recondition by evaporation or sugar addition| S1

    style P1 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9c5400,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#ffd6e0,stroke:#9d2235,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Position of OD in the Drying/Preservation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Fresh Horticultural Produce] --> OD[Osmotic Dehydration]
    OD --> IMF[Intermediate Moisture Food]
    OD --> PreCab[Pre-Treatment for Cabinet Drying]
    OD --> PreFreeze[Pre-Treatment for Freeze Drying]
    OD --> PreFry[Pre-Treatment for Frying]

    IMF --> Pack1[Aseptic Packaging]
    PreCab --> Pack2[Shelf-Stable Dried Pack]
    PreFreeze --> Pack3[Premium Quality Dried Pack]
    PreFry --> Pack4[Low Oil Snack Pack]

    style Raw fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a83232,color:#000
    style OD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style IMF fill:#fff2b2,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    style PreCab fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#557f2d,color:#000
    style PreFreeze fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#5f3a87,color:#000
    style PreFry fill:#ffd9f2,stroke:#a83279,color:#000
    style Pack1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style Pack2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style Pack3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style Pack4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
Define **osmotic dehydration**. State any **two advantages** of osmotic dehydration as a pre-treatment.

**Model Answer:**

> **Osmotic Dehydration (OD)** is the process of partial removal of water from water-bearing cellular plant tissue (fruits/vegetables) by direct contact with a **hypertonic solution** of sugar or salt, where the **water moves out** of the product into the solution under the **chemical-potential gradient**, while a controlled amount of **solute enters** the tissue.
>
> **Two advantages:**
> (i) It operates at **ambient or mild temperatures**, preserving colour, flavour, and heat-sensitive nutrients.
> (ii) It consumes **less thermal energy** than hot-air drying because the bulk of water removal is non-thermal.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
List any **four process parameters** that affect the rate of osmotic dehydration.

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Concentration of osmotic solution.**
> (ii) **Immersion temperature** of the osmotic bath.
> (iii) **Immersion time** of the produce in the solution.
> (iv) **Agitation / stirring** speed (boundary-layer thinning).
> *(Additional valid parameters: solution-to-sample ratio, sample geometry, vacuum pulse, type of osmotic agent.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions, each split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **principle, working, and components** of an osmotic-dehydration system. Highlight the **driving force** and the **direction of mass transfer**.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition** of osmotic dehydration as a partial water-removal process using a hypertonic solution.
2. **Principle / Driving Force:** water chemical-potential gradient, expressed as osmotic-pressure difference $\Delta\pi = \pi_{\text{solution}} - \pi_{\text{product}}$.
3. **Components:** raw-produce preparation tank, osmotic-solution tank with jacket heater, agitator/stirrer, solution recirculation pump, temperature controller, sample-holding mesh baskets, drainer/rinser, surface-water removal fan, post-drying tunnel.
4. **Working:** The produce is immersed in the stirred, thermostated syrup; water leaves the tissue, sugar enters; the partially-dehydrated pieces are drained, rinsed, and air-dried.
5. **Mass transfer directions:** **Water flux → outward**, **solute flux → inward** (counter-current).
6. **Sketch explanation:** flow chart as in Section 4.1 (refer block diagram).
7. **Engineering relevance:** Energy-efficient pre-treatment for downstream drying; intermediate-moisture food preparation.

**(b)** Derive the **first-order Azuara kinetic model** for water loss during osmotic dehydration. Using the model, calculate the **time required** to reach **90 % of the equilibrium water loss** if the rate constant $K = 0.012 \text{ min}^{-1}$.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation (as in Section 3.1):** Start from $\dfrac{dWL_t}{dt} = K(WL_\infty - WL_t)$, integrate, and obtain:

$$WL_t = WL_\infty\bigl[1 - \exp(-K\,t)\bigr]$$

**(ii) Time to reach 90 % of equilibrium:**

$$\frac{WL_t}{WL_\infty} = 0.90 = 1 - \exp(-K\,t)$$

$$\exp(-K\,t) = 0.10$$

$$-K\,t = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

$$t = \frac{2.3026}{K} = \frac{2.3026}{0.012} \approx 191.9 \text{ min} \approx 3.20 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{90\%} \approx 192 \text{ min} \,}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting osmotic dehydration**, with emphasis on **solution concentration, temperature, agitation, and solution-to-sample ratio**.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Solution concentration (Brix):** Higher concentration increases $\Delta\pi$, leading to higher WL and SG until equilibrium. Typical industrial range **50 – 70 °Brix** for fruits.
- **Temperature:** Elevated temperature (up to **~ 60 °C**) increases molecular mobility, hence $D_{\text{eff}}$ and WL, but excessive temperature cooks tissue, leaches pigments, and may invert sucrose.
- **Agitation:** Stirring reduces the **boundary-layer resistance** at the solid–liquid interface; typical **50 – 200 rpm** in lab/industrial units.
- **Solution-to-sample ratio (SSR):** Higher SSR maintains an approximately constant solution concentration. KTU preferred value: **SSR ≥ 4**, ideally **6 – 10**.
- Mention sample geometry, immersion time, vacuum pulse, and pre-treatment for full credit.

**(b)** Explain **Crank's diffusion model** for an infinite slab. Show how the **effective diffusivity $D_{\text{eff}}$** is obtained from the linearised form of the model.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Fick's second law for a slab of thickness $2L$: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{\text{eff}}\dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$.
2. Boundary conditions $C(\pm L,t) = C_e$ and initial condition $C(x,0) = C_0$.
3. Eigenfunction expansion gives (Crank's solution):

$$\frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty} = \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[\,-(2n+1)^2\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}} t / 4L^2\,\right]$$

4. For sufficiently long times, only $n = 0$ survives, yielding the linearised form:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M_t - M_\infty}{M_0 - M_\infty}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}}{4L^2}\,t$$

5. Plot $\ln(MR)$ versus $t$; slope $m = -\dfrac{\pi^2 D_{\text{eff}}}{4L^2}$. Hence:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = -\,\frac{4L^2\,m}{\pi^2}$$

6. The constant term $\ln(8/\pi^2) \approx -0.21$ acts as a consistency check on the experimental data.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in OD problems:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator** — Always divide WL and SG by the **initial fresh mass $M_0$**, *not* by $M_t$. This is the single most common error.
> 2. **Units mismatch** — Report $D_{\text{eff}}$ in **m²/s**; report WL/SG/WR in **%**; report time in **seconds or minutes** consistently.
> 3. **Sign confusion in WR** — Remember WR = WL − SG, **never** SG − WL. A negative WR means the piece has *gained* mass (rare but possible at very short times).
> 4. **Forgetting the SSR condition** — Examiners specifically test whether the student has used SSR ≥ 4.
> 5. **Confusing SG with WL** — Solid Gain measures **how much osmotic solute entered**, **not** how much water was gained.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in Part B (a)** — A flow chart or block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in design/explanation questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Osmotic dehydration = partial water removal by immersion in a **hypertonic** solution of sugar or salt.
- **Driving force:** **Chemical-potential gradient** of water (≡ osmotic-pressure difference $\Delta\pi$).
- **Counter-current mass transfer:** Water moves **out**, osmotic solute moves **in**.
- **Three key metrics:** $WL\% = \dfrac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0}\times 100$, $SG\% = \dfrac{S_t - S_0}{M_0}\times 100$, $WR = WL - SG$.
- **Equilibrium condition:** $\mu_w^{\text{cell}} = \mu_w^{\text{solution}}$; thereafter no net transfer.
- **Osmotic agents:** Sucrose for **fruits** (imparts sweetness), NaCl for **vegetables** (preserves, firms).
- **Operating conditions (typical):** Concentration **40 – 70 °Brix**; temperature **30 – 60 °C**; SSR **≥ 4**; time **30 min – 6 h**.
- **Kinetics:** First-order Azuara model $WL_t = WL_\infty[1-\exp(-Kt)]$ is widely accepted.
- **Diffusion:** Crank's Fickian model $D_{\text{eff}}$ is found from the slope of $\ln(MR)$ vs $t$.
- **Industrial applications:** Pre-treatment for cabinet / freeze / vacuum drying; preparation of IMF; pre-treatment before frying to reduce oil uptake.
- **Advantages over hot-air drying:** Mild-temperature operation, **lower energy cost**, better **colour/flavour** retention, **less nutrient loss**, firmer texture.
- **Limitations:** Slow process, only *partial* dehydration, large volumes of syrup to manage, possible surface over-impregnation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Methods of Preservation of Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Methods of Preservation of Fruits and Vegetables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservation of Fruits and Vegetables** is defined as the systematic application of physical, chemical, thermal, biological, or combined techniques to inhibit or eliminate the agents of spoilage (microorganisms, native enzymes, and chemical reactions), thereby extending the **shelf life** of perishable horticultural produce from the point of harvest until final consumption while retaining its sensory, nutritional, and market value.

In the framework of **Post Harvest Engineering (PCAGT601, Module 4)**, preservation is positioned as the *technological bridge* between harvest physiology and food security. The discipline is governed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes** for Module 4, which emphasize the selection of preservation methods based on the **intrinsic properties of the produce** (pH, water activity, respiration rate) and the **extrinsic processing parameters** (temperature, gas atmosphere, packaging).

## 1.2 Causes of Post-Harvest Losses — The "Why" of Preservation

Spoilage in fresh fruits and vegetables arises from **four overlapping vectors**:

| Spoilage Vector | Mechanism | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial** | Bacteria, yeasts, moulds consume sugars/amino acids | Soft rot of tomato by *Erwinia carotovora* |
| **Enzymatic** | PPO, PME, POD, LOX degrade cellular structure | Enzymatic browning of cut apple |
| **Physiological** | Respiration, transpiration, ethylene evolution | Senescence of climacteric fruits |
| **Chemical** | Lipid oxidation, Maillard browning, vitamin loss | Rancidity in dried coconut |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle (Hall's Law of Spoilage):** The rate of biological deterioration approximately **doubles for every 10 °C rise in temperature** within the biological range (0–40 °C). This is the engineering basis for cold-chain logistics.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Food Stability

Any preservation method must attack at least one of these four pillars:

1. **Lower the Water Activity (a<sub>w</sub>)** — drying, salting, sugaring
2. **Lower the Temperature** — refrigeration, freezing
3. **Raise the Temperature** — pasteurization, sterilization
4. **Modify the Atmosphere / Chemistry** — MAP, CA, chemical preservatives

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Battery" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a freshly picked mango as a *biological battery* — a sealed pouch of stored sugars, organic acids, and enzymes that is slowly discharging through **respiration (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>12</sub>O<sub>6</sub> + 6 O<sub>2</sub> → 6 CO<sub>2</sub> + 6 H<sub>2</sub>O + energy)**. Microorganisms outside the fruit act as opportunists trying to tap the same energy. Preservation is therefore either:
> - **Slowing the discharge** (refrigeration, CA storage) 🚰
> - **Cutting the wires** (heat sterilization) ✂️
> - **Extracting the energy as dry fuel** (drying into raisins) 🍇
> - **Adding chemical inhibitors to the electrolyte** (sorbates, benzoates) 🛡️

## 1.5 Critical Control Metrics (Must Know for KTU)

- **Water activity (a<sub>w</sub>):** Most bacteria need a<sub>w</sub> > 0.91, most yeasts > 0.88, most moulds > 0.70. Dry fruits and jams are preserved precisely because their a<sub>w</sub> falls below these thresholds.
- **pH 4.5 rule:** Foods with pH ≤ 4.5 are *acid foods* (most fruits) — only pasteurization is required. Foods with pH > 4.5 (most vegetables) are *low-acid foods* and require full sterilization (botulinum control).
- **Respiration rate (R):** Measured in mL CO<sub>2</sub>·kg<sup>-1</sup>·h<sup>-1</sup>. Climacteric fruits (mango, banana) show a sharp respiratory peak at ripening.
- **Ethylene (C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>4</sub>) evolution rate:** Apples ≈ 1–10 µL·kg<sup>-1</sup>·h<sup>-1</sup>; bananas ≈ 0.5–2 µL·kg<sup>-1</sup>·h<sup>-1</sup>.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Preservation Methods

Preservation methods are taxonomically grouped under **three super-classes** in the KTU syllabus:

### A. Physical / Thermal Methods
1. **Low-Temperature Preservation** (Chilling & Freezing)
2. **High-Temperature Preservation** (Blanching, Pasteurization, Sterilization, Canning)

### B. Chemical Methods
3. **Preservation by Reduction of Water Content** (Drying / Dehydration)
4. **Preservation by Chemical Agents** (Salt, Sugar, Vinegar, Preservatives)
5. **Preservation by Fermentation** (Pickling, Lactic Fermentation)

### C. Advanced / Combined Methods
6. **Preservation by Irradiation** (Gamma, e-beam)
7. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) / Controlled Atmosphere (CA)**
8. **Hurdle Technology**

---

## 2.2 Method-by-Method Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Low-Temperature Preservation

**Principle:** Lowering temperature reduces both **microbial growth rate** and **metabolic activity** of the produce. It does *not* kill microbes — it merely arrests them.

#### (a) Chilling / Cold Storage
- **Range:** 0 °C to 10 °C (typically 0–4 °C for temperate fruits; 7–13 °C for chilling-sensitive tropical fruits).
- **Refrigerated shelf life extension:** 2–10× over ambient.
- **Chilling Injury (CI):** A critical concept for tropical produce stored below ~13 °C. Symptoms include pitting, surface browning, failure to ripen (banana at <13 °C).
- **Chilling-sensitive:** Mango, banana, papaya, cucumber, eggplant.
- **Chilling-tolerant:** Apple, pear, grapes, carrot, cabbage.

#### (b) Freezing
- **Range:** −18 °C or below (commercial) / −40 °C (quick-freezing / IQF).
- **Mechanism:** Freezes freezable water, immobilizing it as ice. a<sub>w</sub> drops to <0.6.
- **Quick Freezing vs Slow Freezing:**
  - **Quick / IQF:** Small ice crystals, retains cellular structure, superior texture.
  - **Slow freezing:** Large extracellular crystals, ruptures cell walls, causes drip-loss on thawing.
- **Freezing Point Depression (ΔT<sub>f</sub>):** Fruits freeze below 0 °C due to dissolved sugars and acids. Pure water = 0 °C; apple juice ≈ −1.5 °C; orange juice ≈ −1.3 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The *initial freezing point* (T<sub>f</sub>) marks the start of ice formation; the *eutectic point* marks the point at which even the bound water solidifies. Storage must always be **below T<sub>f</sub> but above the eutectic temperature** to prevent solute crystallization damage.

### 2.2.2 High-Temperature Preservation

**Principle:** Heat denatures microbial proteins, enzymes, and nucleic acids irreversibly. Effectiveness is governed by **D-value**, **Z-value**, and **F-value** (see Formula Sheet).

#### (a) Blanching
- **Temperature:** 80–100 °C (steam or hot water) for 1–10 minutes.
- **Purpose:** Inactivate **PPO, POD, PME, LOX** enzymes prior to drying/freezing. Not a sterilization step.
- **Indicator enzyme:** Peroxidase (POD) is the most heat-stable; blanching continues until POD is inactivated.

#### (b) Pasteurization
- **Temperature:** 60–90 °C for short times.
- **Examples:** Fruit juices (72 °C × 15 s — HTST); guava pulp (85 °C × 1 min).
- **Kills:** Pathogenic vegetative cells, yeasts, moulds; **does NOT kill bacterial spores** (Clostridium, Bacillus).

#### (c) Sterilization
- **Temperature:** > 100 °C (typically 121.1 °C for 15 min in autoclave / retort).
- **Commercial Sterility:** Achieves 12-log (12D) reduction of *Clostridium botulinum* spores.
- **F<sub>0</sub> concept:** Equivalent sterilization time at 121.1 °C with Z = 10 °C.

#### (d) Canning (Appertization)
- **Steps:** Sorting → Washing → Peeling → Blanching → Filling → Brine/syrup addition → Exhausting → Sealing → Sterilization → Cooling.
- **Acidified canned foods** (pH ≤ 4.5): Boiling-water bath, 100 °C.
- **Low-acid canned foods** (pH > 4.5): Pressure retort, 121.1 °C.

### 2.2.3 Preservation by Water Removal (Drying / Dehydration)

**Principle:** Reducing moisture content to a *safe moisture level* decreases a<sub>w</sub> below the threshold for microbial growth. Microbial growth ceases below a<sub>w</sub> = 0.60.

#### Methods of Drying
- **Sun / Solar Drying** — traditional, slow, contamination risk.
- **Hot-Air Cabinet / Tunnel Drying** — 50–80 °C, most common.
- **Spray Drying** — for liquid pulps, instant powders.
- **Drum Drying** — for purees, fruit leathers.
- **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** — premium quality, sublimation under vacuum, 30 °C shelf.
- **Osmotic Dehydration** — immersion in 60–70 °Brix sugar syrup; water exits by osmosis.
- **Fluidized-Bed Drying** — for diced vegetables.
- **Microwave / RF Drying** — rapid, volumetric heating.

#### Critical Moisture Content vs Equilibrium Moisture Content
- **Critical Moisture Content (C<sub>mc</sub>):** Moisture at which drying rate starts to fall (end of constant-rate period).
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC):** Moisture content in equilibrium with ambient RH — drying stops here.

### 2.2.4 Preservation by Chemical Agents

| Chemical | Mechanism | Typical Concentration | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NaCl** (common salt) | Osmotic plasmolysis of microbial cells | 8–15 % w/w | Pickles, salted mango |
| **Sugar** | Osmotic pressure, reduces a<sub>w</sub> | > 65 °Brix | Jams, jellies, preserves |
| **Vinegar (acetic acid)** | pH < 4.5, kills vegetative cells | 2–6 % acetic acid | Pickled cucumber, onions |
| **Sodium Benzoate** | Inhibits enzymes of TCA cycle in microbes | 0.1 % max (FSSAI) | Sauces, fruit juices |
| **Potassium Sorbate** | Disrupts cell membrane | 0.1 % max | Cheese, wines |
| **SO<sub>2</sub> / Sulphites** | Inhibits PPO, microbes | 350–2000 ppm | Dried fruits, wines |
| **Ascorbic Acid** | Antioxidant, anti-browning | 0.1–0.5 % | Cut fruits |
| **Citric Acid** | pH reduction, chelates metals | 0.1–2 % | Lemon juice, salads |

### 2.2.5 Preservation by Fermentation

**Principle:** Beneficial microbes (mainly *Lactic Acid Bacteria* — LAB like *Lactobacillus*, *Leuconostoc*) convert sugars to **lactic acid + CO<sub>2</sub>**, dropping pH below 4.0 and producing bacteriocins.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{LAB}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{Energy}$$

**Examples:** Sauerkraut (cabbage), kimchi, gundruk (radish), fermented mango pickle, idli/dosa batter.

### 2.2.6 Preservation by Irradiation

- **Source:** <sup>60</sup>Co γ-rays, X-rays, or electron beam (max 10 MeV per WHO/Codex).
- **Doses:**
  - **Low dose (≤ 1 kGy):** Sprout inhibition in potatoes/onions (0.05–0.15 kGy).
  - **Medium (1–10 kGy):** Insect disinfestation, shelf-life extension (1–3 kGy for fruits).
  - **High (> 10 kGy):** Sterilization.
- **Symbol on label:** "Radura" — international logo for irradiated food.

### 2.2.7 MAP and CA Storage

| Parameter | MAP | CA |
|---|---|---|
| Gas composition | Passive equilibration | Active, precise control |
| O<sub>2</sub> level | 2–5 % (typical) | 1–3 % (e.g., apple 1–2 %) |
| CO<sub>2</sub> level | 3–10 % | 1–5 % |
| Equipment | Polymer films + produce | Gas-tight room with analyzers |

### 2.2.8 Hurdle Technology (Leistner, 1995)

A *synergistic* combination of multiple mild preservation factors ("hurdles") such that the combined effect is greater than the sum of individual effects. For fruit products, common hurdles are: pH + a<sub>w</sub> + mild heat + preservatives.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Preserve This Table for Last-Day Revision**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Water activity | dimensionless | Microbial growth prediction |
| 2 | $\text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}\times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % | Drying calculations |
| 3 | $\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}\times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % | Drying calculations |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0/N)}$ | Decimal reduction time | minutes | Microbial inactivation |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log_{10}(D_1/D_2)}$ | Z-value (temp change for 10× D) | °C | Thermal death kinetics |
| 6 | $F_0 = D_{121.1^\circ\text{C}} \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Sterilization value | minutes | Canning process design |
| 7 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\!\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient | dimensionless | Respiration rate scaling |
| 8 | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius equation | variable | Spoilage kinetics |
| 9 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression | °C | Freezing point of juices |
| 10 | $Q = m\,C_p\,\Delta T$ | Sensible heat required | kJ | Blanching/pasteurization energy |
| 11 | $t = \dfrac{m_s\,\lambda}{h\,A}$ | Drying time (constant rate) | s | Hot-air drying |
| 12 | $F = f_h \cdot 10^{\,(T-T_{\text{ref}})/Z} \cdot \Delta t$ | Lethality rate integration | minutes | Process lethality |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Tip for KTU Numericals:** For thermal death kinetics, always convert time to **minutes**, temperature to **°C**, and remember the *reference temperature* is **121.1 °C** for F<sub>0</sub> and **70 °C** for D-ref in milk pasteurization. For fruit juices, the reference is often 90 °C.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Mangoes from Kerala reach Europe in 25 days at 13 °C with controlled humidity (85–90 % RH), preserving 90 % of vitamin C.
- **Aseptic Canning:** Pineapple chunks in 1 kg cans, 12-D F<sub>0</sub> = 6 min process at 121.1 °C — Kerala's largest export item.
- **Solar Tunnel Drying:** Banana figs produced in 4–6 hours of sunlight in Thrissur, India, reducing losses from 30 % to 4 %.
- **Hurdle Technology in Pickles:** Kerala's mango pickle uses 15 % salt + 5 % acetic acid + 0.1 % sodium benzoate — each alone is insufficient, together they achieve a 24-month shelf life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Solutions & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 problems are typically of two types — (a) thermal-death-kinetics (D, Z, F<sub>0</sub>) and (b) moisture-content / drying / water-activity calculations. The derivations below are the **most frequently tested** and must be mastered.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Activity from Equilibrium Relative Humidity

### Statement
For any food in a closed headspace at equilibrium, the water activity (a<sub>w</sub>) is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water above the food (P) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water (P<sub>0</sub>) at the same temperature.

### Derivation

Consider a closed desiccator containing a hygroscopic food and a headspace. At equilibrium, no net mass transfer occurs.

$$\text{Net evaporation rate} = \text{Net condensation rate}$$

By the kinetic theory of gases, evaporation flux is proportional to the difference between the saturation vapour pressure (P<sub>0</sub>) and the actual partial pressure (P). At equilibrium, the rate of condensation (∝ P) equals the rate of evaporation (∝ P<sub>0</sub> − P):

$$k_c \cdot P = k_e \cdot (P_0 - P)$$

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of water in the food equals that of water vapour:

$$\mu_{\text{food}} = \mu_{\text{vapour}}$$

This leads to the standard thermodynamic identity:

$$a_w = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_{\text{food}} - \mu_{\text{pure water}}}{RT}\right) = \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

Since the **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)** of the headspace, expressed as a percentage, equals (P/P<sub>0</sub>) × 100:

$$\boxed{\,a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}\,}$$

### Units & Range
a<sub>w</sub> is **dimensionless**, ranging from 0 (bone-dry) to 1 (pure water).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)

### Statement
The D-value is the time (at a given temperature) required to reduce the microbial population by **one order of magnitude (90 %)**.

### Derivation

Microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

where k is the specific death rate constant. Separating variables:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \int_0^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = k\,t$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm (where $\log_{10} x = \ln x / 2.303$):

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{k\,t}{2.303}$$

By definition, when t = D, the ratio N<sub>0</sub>/N = 10, so $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = 1$. Substituting:

$$1 = \dfrac{k\,D}{2.303} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{2.303}{D}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\,t = D \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)\,}$$

### Numerical Example
A spore suspension has N<sub>0</sub> = 10<sup>5</sup> CFU/mL. The D-value at 121 °C is 2.5 min. What time is required to achieve 12-D (commercial sterility)?

**Solution:** For 12-D, N<sub>0</sub>/N = 10<sup>12</sup>:

$$t = 2.5 \times \log_{10}(10^{12}) = 2.5 \times 12 = 30 \text{ min}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Z-value (Thermal Death Time Curve)

### Statement
Z is the temperature increase (°C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10.

### Derivation
From two D-value observations D<sub>1</sub> at T<sub>1</sub> and D<sub>2</sub> at T<sub>2</sub>:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,Z = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log_{10}(D_1/D_2)}\,}$$

### Numerical Example
At 110 °C, D = 25 min. At 121 °C, D = 2.5 min. Find Z.

**Solution:**

$$Z = \dfrac{110 - 121}{\log_{10}(25/2.5)} = \dfrac{-11}{\log_{10}(10)} = \dfrac{-11}{1} = -11\,^\circ\text{C}$$

Taking magnitude: Z = **11 °C** (sign convention gives negative; KTU convention uses |Z| = 11 °C).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — F<sub>0</sub> (Sterilization Value)

### Statement
F<sub>0</sub> is the equivalent sterilization time at the **reference temperature 121.1 °C (250 °F)** that produces the same lethality as the actual time-temperature profile of a thermal process.

### Derivation
At any instant, the lethal rate L(t) is defined as the ratio of D at the reference temperature to D at the actual temperature:

$$L(t) = \dfrac{D_{\text{ref}}}{D(t)} = 10^{\,(T(t) - T_{\text{ref}})/Z}$$

The total lethality is the integral of L(t) over the process time:

$$F_0 = \int_0^{t_{\text{end}}} 10^{\,(T(t) - 121.1)/Z}\,dt$$

For a constant-temperature hold at T:

$$\boxed{\,F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\,(T - 121.1)/Z}\,}$$

### Numerical Example
A retort holds product at 116 °C for 45 min, with Z = 10 °C. Calculate F<sub>0</sub>.

**Solution:**

$$F_0 = 45 \times 10^{\,(116 - 121.1)/10} = 45 \times 10^{-0.51}$$

$$10^{-0.51} \approx 0.309$$

$$F_0 = 45 \times 0.309 \approx 13.9 \text{ min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A target F<sub>0</sub> ≥ 3 min is generally accepted as **commercial sterility** for low-acid canned vegetables (Codex Alimentarius). For *Clostridium botulinum* specifically, **F<sub>0</sub> ≥ 2.78 min** (= 12-D reduction).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Q<sub>10</sub> (Temperature Coefficient of Spoilage)

### Statement
Q<sub>10</sub> is the factor by which the rate of a biological/chemical process increases for a 10 °C rise in temperature.

### Derivation
Start from the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Take the ratio of two rates:

$$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right] = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right]$$

For Q<sub>10</sub>: T<sub>2</sub> = T<sub>1</sub> + 10. Define $\Delta T = 10$ K. Using algebra and assuming T<sub>1</sub>T<sub>2</sub> ≈ T<sub>1</sub><sup>2</sup> + 10T<sub>1</sub>, the standard form is:

$$\boxed{\,Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\!\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}\,}$$

### Numerical Example
The respiration rate of an apple at 5 °C is 4 mg CO<sub>2</sub>·kg<sup>-1</sup>·h<sup>-1</sup>. The rate at 20 °C is 24 mg·kg<sup>-1</sup>·h<sup>-1</sup>. Find Q<sub>10</sub>.

**Solution:**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{24}{4}\right)^{\!\dfrac{10}{20 - 5}} = 6^{\,2/3}$$

$$6^{\,2/3} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\ln 6\right) = \exp(0.597) \approx 1.82$$

So Q<sub>10</sub> ≈ **1.82** for apple respiration.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Freezing Point Depression for Fruit Juices

### Statement
The freezing point of a solution (fruit juice) is depressed below 0 °C in proportion to its molal solute concentration (Raoult's Law + colligative property).

### Derivation
Raoult's Law gives the vapour pressure lowering:

$$P = x_{\text{water}} \cdot P_0$$

At the freezing point, the chemical potential of liquid water equals that of ice:

$$\mu^{\text{liq}}_{\text{water}}(T_f) = \mu^{\text{ice}}_{\text{water}}(T_f)$$

Using the Clausius–Clapeyron relation and integrating from 0 °C to T<sub>f</sub>:

$$\ln(x_{\text{water}}) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_f} - \dfrac{1}{273.15}\right)$$

For dilute solutions, $\ln(x_{\text{water}}) \approx -x_{\text{solute}} \approx -m \cdot M_w$ (molality approximation). This gives the standard cryoscopic formula:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta T_f = T_0 - T_f = K_f \cdot m\,}$$

where:
- $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg·mol<sup>-1</sup>**
- $m$ = molality of solutes (mol·kg<sup>-1</sup>)

### Numerical Example
A grape juice contains 18 % sucrose (MW = 342 g·mol<sup>-1</sup>) by mass. Find its freezing point.

**Solution:**

$$m = \dfrac{180 \text{ g/kg}}{342 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.5263 \text{ mol/kg}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 0.5263 = 0.979\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_f = 0 - 0.979 = -0.98\,^\circ\text{C}$$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis)

### Derivation
Let W = total mass; W<sub>w</sub> = water mass; W<sub>d</sub> = dry matter mass.

$$\text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W} \times 100 = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W}\times 100$$

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}\times 100 = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W_d}\times 100$$

Dividing numerator and denominator of the db formula by W:

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{wb}/100}{1 - \text{MC}_{wb}/100}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{wb}}{1 - \text{MC}_{wb}}\quad\text{or}\quad \text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{db}}{1 + \text{MC}_{db}}\,}$$

### Numerical Example
A fresh tomato has 94 % moisture (wb). What is its MC on dry basis? (i) W<sub>w</sub> = 94 g, W<sub>d</sub> = 6 g, W = 100 g.

**Solution:**

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{94}{6} \times 100 = 1566.67\,\%$$

(High db value is normal for fresh produce; for engineering calculations, dry basis is preferred during drying.)

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Exam Favourite)

| Feature | Pasteurization | Sterilization | Blanching |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 60–90 °C | > 100 °C | 80–100 °C |
| **Kills spores?** | No | Yes (commercial sterility) | No |
| **Kills vegetative cells?** | Yes | Yes | Partial |
| **Inactivates enzymes?** | Partial | Yes (most) | Yes (PPO, POD, PME) |
| **Shelf life gained** | Days to months | Years (canned) | Days to weeks (with storage) |
| **pH range** | Acid foods (≤ 4.5) | All foods | Pre-treatment for both |
| **Process examples** | Juices, milk, wine | Vegetables, meat, soups | Vegetables pre-freezing/drying |

| Feature | Cold Storage | Freezing | MAP/CA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temp** | 0–13 °C | ≤ −18 °C | 0–13 °C |
| **Microbial effect** | Inhibits | Inhibits (does not kill) | Inhibits (esp. aerobic) |
| **Quality** | Fresh-like | Slight texture loss | Fresh-like |
| **Shelf life** | Days–months | 6–24 months | 2× to 5× of cold |
| **Energy intensity** | Moderate | Very high | Low |

| Feature | Sun Drying | Hot-Air Drying | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|---|
| **a<sub>w</sub> achieved** | 0.6–0.7 | 0.3–0.6 | 0.05–0.1 |
| **Quality** | Low | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Very low | Moderate | Very high |
| **Time** | 3–7 days | 4–24 h | 12–30 h |
| **Vitamin retention** | 30–60 % | 60–80 % | > 90 % |
