# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are framed strictly as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern** (ESE — End Semester Evaluation). The model answers use the same key pattern expected by the KTU Board examiner.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> Each question carries **3 marks**. Answer in **2 to 3 sentences with one labeled diagram or formula** where applicable.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define water activity (a<sub>w</sub>). Mention its role in the preservation of fruits and vegetables.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Water activity is defined as the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$$

It is **dimensionless** and lies in the range 0 to 1. Its role in preservation is critical because **microbial growth is a function of a<sub>w</sub>**: most bacteria require a<sub>w</sub> > 0.91, most yeasts > 0.88, and most moulds > 0.70. By drying fruits (a<sub>w</sub> < 0.6) or adding salt/sugar (a<sub>w</sub> < 0.85), microbial growth is arrested, achieving preservation. **(3 marks)** ✅

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between pasteurization and sterilization with reference to fruits and vegetables.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pasteurization | Sterilization |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 60–90 °C | > 100 °C (commonly 121.1 °C) |
| Spore destruction | No | Yes |
| Typical use | Acid foods (pH ≤ 4.5) — fruit juices, pulp | Low-acid foods (pH > 4.5) — vegetables, meat |
| Process target | 5-log reduction of vegetative cells | 12-D reduction of *C. botulinum* spores |

Pasteurization is sufficient for acidic fruits where *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow, while sterilization is mandatory for low-acid vegetables to achieve commercial sterility. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has **internal choice** — students answer **EITHER** (a) and (b) **OR** (a) and (b) of the alternate set.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — Choice 1 (Answer EITHER this set OR Choice 2)

#### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of **preservation of fruits and vegetables by low temperature** (chilling and freezing). Discuss the factors affecting storage life under cold conditions. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** What is **canning**? Describe the unit operations and equipment used in commercial canning of fruits and vegetables. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Answer — Q A(a) [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** Low-temperature preservation refers to storing produce at temperatures above (chilling, 0–13 °C) or below (freezing, ≤ −18 °C) the freezing point of the produce to reduce microbial growth, respiration, and senescence.

**(1) Chilled / Refrigerated Storage [3 marks]:**
- **Range:** 0 °C to 13 °C depending on commodity.
- **Effects:**
  - Reduces respiration rate (Q<sub>10</sub> ≈ 2–3).
  - Inhibits microbial growth (does not kill).
  - Slows enzymatic activity and ethylene evolution.
  - Reduces transpiration when RH is maintained at 85–95 %.
- **Chilling Injury (CI):** Tropical fruits like banana, mango, papaya are damaged below 13 °C — symptoms include pitting, browning, failure to ripen.
- **Recommended storage conditions:**
  - Apple: 0–4 °C, 90–95 % RH, 3–6 months.
  - Mango: 10–13 °C, 85–90 % RH, 2–4 weeks.
  - Tomato (ripe): 7–10 °C, 85–90 % RH, 1 week.

**(2) Freezing [2 marks]:**
- **Range:** −18 °C to −40 °C (IQF).
- **Mechanism:** Freezes ~80 % of freezable water, reducing a<sub>w</sub> to < 0.6.
- **Quick Freezing** (IQF, air blast, plate freezer, cryogenic): Small intracellular crystals, better texture.
- **Slow Freezing:** Large extracellular crystals, cell wall rupture, drip loss.
- **Products:** Frozen peas, sweet corn, diced mango, jackfruit bulbs, frozen strawberries.

**(3) Factors affecting storage life [1 mark]:**
- Initial microbial load, pH, water activity, cultivar, maturity, post-harvest handling, packaging (MAP, vacuum), and integrity of cold chain.

---

#### Model Answer — Q A(b) [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** Canning is the hermetic sealing of a food in a metal or glass container followed by a thermal sterilization process to achieve commercial sterility and long ambient shelf life.

**Unit Operations (with flow diagram) [5 marks]:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Receiving] --> S[Sorting] --> W[Washing] --> P[Peeling] --> B[Blanching] --> C[Can Filling] --> E[Exhausting] --> M[Seaming] --> T[Retort Sterilization 121C] --> CL[Cooling] --> L[Labeling]
```

1. **Receiving & Sorting** — Quality check, removal of damaged produce.
2. **Washing** — Spray or soak to remove soil and microbes (often chlorinated water, 50–200 ppm).
3. **Peeling / Trimming** — Mechanical, chemical (lye), steam, or abrasive peeling.
4. **Blanching** — Steam (100 °C × 3–5 min) to inactivate PPO, POD, PME and remove intercellular air.
5. **Filling & Brine/Syrup Addition** — Hot syrup 25–40 °Brix for fruits; 2 % salt brine for vegetables.
6. **Exhausting** — Steam heating to 80–90 °C to remove headspace air (prevents vacuum loss and tin corrosion).
7. **Seaming** — Hermetic double-seam closure.
8. **Retort Sterilization** — 121.1 °C, F<sub>0</sub> ≥ 3 min for low-acid vegetables; 100 °C boiling water bath for acid fruits.
9. **Cooling** — Rapid cooling to 35 °C in chlorinated water to prevent thermophilic growth.

**Equipment [1 mark]:** Rotary cooker-cooler, hydrostatic retort, batch still retort, can reformer, double-seamer, exhauster.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

#### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **methods of preservation of fruits and vegetables by dehydration / drying**. Discuss the principles of sun drying, hot-air drying, spray drying, and freeze drying. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** What is **hurdle technology**? Explain its principles and application in preservation of fruits and vegetables with examples. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Answer — Q B(a) [7 Marks]

**Principle [1 mark]:** Water removal lowers the water activity (a<sub>w</sub>) of the produce below the threshold for microbial growth (a<sub>w</sub> < 0.6 for long-term storage). The Critical Moisture Content (C<sub>mc</sub>) marks the end of the constant-rate drying period, after which the product enters the falling-rate period governed by diffusion.

**Methods of Drying [6 marks — distribute as below]:**

**(1) Sun / Solar Drying [1.5 marks]:**
- Traditional method; open-air exposure of spread-out produce to solar radiation.
- Drying rate depends on solar intensity, ambient RH, wind speed.
- **Drawbacks:** Slow (3–7 days), contamination by dust/insects, uneven drying, vitamin loss (UV degradation of vitamin C).
- **Improvement:** Solar tunnel dryers with polyhouse covers — reduce drying time to 4–6 hours, product quality 2× better.

**(2) Hot-Air Cabinet / Tunnel Drying [1.5 marks]:**
- Forced convection of hot air (50–80 °C) over the produce.
- Used for grapes (raisins), figs, mango slices, onion flakes.
- Air temperature, velocity, and humidity are the key process parameters.

**(3) Spray Drying [1.5 marks]:**
- Feed: liquid concentrate (juice, pulp).
- Operation: Atomized into a hot air stream (150–220 °C inlet, 80–100 °C outlet); evaporation completes in 0.5–5 s.
- Products: Tomato powder, banana powder, fruit-based instant drink mixes.
- **Advantage:** Rapid drying → minimal thermal damage; **Disadvantage:** Sticky products need maltodextrin carrier.

**(4) Freeze Drying (Lyophilization) [1.5 marks]:**
- Frozen product (−40 °C) → water removed by **sublimation** under high vacuum (10–30 Pa).
- Shelf temperature ~30 °C, condenser at −50 °C.
- **Advantage:** Best quality — retains shape, color, aroma, vitamin C, polyphenols. a<sub>w</sub> = 0.05–0.10.
- **Disadvantage:** High capital and energy cost.

**Other Methods (brief):** Osmotic dehydration (60–70 °Brix syrup, 30–60 °C, 2–6 h); Drum drying (for purees, fruit leathers); Fluidized-bed drying (diced vegetables); Microwave vacuum drying (rapid, low-temperature).

---

#### Model Answer — Q B(b) [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** Hurdle Technology, proposed by Lothar Leistner in 1995, is the deliberate combination of **multiple mild preservation factors (hurdles)** in a single product, such that the synergistic effect achieves microbial safety and stability while maintaining superior sensory and nutritional quality.

**Principles [3 marks]:**
- **Synergy:** The combined effect of hurdles is greater than the sum of individual effects.
- **Mild Preservation:** Each hurdle is gentle (e.g., pH 4.0 instead of pH 2.0; mild heat 80 °C instead of 121 °C).
- **Hurdle Concept:** Each factor "hurdles" microbial growth from a different angle — e.g., pH attacks cell membrane, a<sub>w</sub> attacks osmotic balance, heat attacks enzymes.
- **Multi-target Preservation:** Different microorganisms are sensitive to different hurdles.

**Common Hurdles Used in Fruit/Vegetable Products [2 marks]:**
- **pH reduction** (citric, acetic, lactic acid → pH 3.5–4.5)
- **a<sub>w</sub> reduction** (NaCl, sugar, drying)
- **Mild heat** (pasteurization 70–90 °C)
- **Chemical preservatives** (sorbate, benzoate, sulphite)
- **Redox potential** (low O<sub>2</sub> by MAP or vacuum)
- **Antimicrobial compounds** (essential oils, lysozyme)

**Application Examples [1 mark]:**
- **Mango pickle (Kerala/Indian style):** 15 % salt + 4 % acetic acid + 0.1 % sodium benzoate + spices + spices essential oils. Each alone insufficient, but together provide 18–24 month shelf life at ambient.
- **Fruit leather:** 70 % fruit pulp + 30 % sugar (a<sub>w</sub>) + 0.1 % KMS (preservative) + 0.5 % citric acid (pH) — no refrigeration needed.
- **Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF):** Papaya, pineapple, banana — osmotic dehydration + sulphiting + controlled storage.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps (Board Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Confusing F<sub>0</sub> with D-value:** F<sub>0</sub> is *time* (min) at reference temperature, while D is *time* at a given temperature. Many students interchange these. Memorize: F = D × log(N<sub>0</sub>/N).
> 2. **Wrong sign for Z:** Conventionally Z is reported as a **positive** number (the temperature increase to drop D by 10×). Even though the formula gives a negative ratio, take the **magnitude**.
> 3. **pH 4.5 cut-off:** Students often say "vegetables are acidic" — INCORRECT. Most vegetables (carrot, beans, potato, peas) are **low-acid** (pH > 4.5) and require retort sterilization. Only pickled/soured products are acidic.
> 4. **Confusing MAP and CA:** MAP uses **passive gas exchange** through films; CA uses **active gas injection** and precise monitoring. Examiner's deduction: 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Omitting freezing point depression:** Freezing point of fruit juice is **not 0 °C** — must use ΔT<sub>f</sub> = K<sub>f</sub>·m. Examiner expects the numerical value.
> 6. **Not mentioning Chilling Injury:** When writing about low-temperature preservation of tropical fruits, *always* note the **CI threshold (10–13 °C)**. Missing this costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Sterilization ≠ Sterile:** Industrial sterilization achieves "**commercial sterility**" (no viable *C. botulinum* or other pathogens), *not absolute sterility*. Some thermophilic spores may survive.
> 8. **Moisture content basis:** Always specify **wb or db** in numerical answers. Examiner penalizes ambiguous notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize This in 10 Minutes**

- [ ] **Definition of Preservation** — extension of shelf life by inhibiting microbes, enzymes, and chemical reactions.
- [ ] **Causes of spoilage** — microbial, enzymatic, physiological, chemical.
- [ ] **Four pillars of stability** — a<sub>w</sub> ↓, T ↓, T ↑, atmosphere modification.
- [ ] **Water activity (a<sub>w</sub>)** — a<sub>w</sub> = P/P<sub>0</sub> = ERH/100; **bacteria > 0.91, yeasts > 0.88, moulds > 0.70**.
- [ ] **pH 4.5 rule** — acid (≤ 4.5) → pasteurize; low acid (> 4.5) → sterilize.
- [ ] **Cold storage** — 0–13 °C, RH 85–95 %, 2–10× shelf life extension.
- [ ] **Chilling Injury** — occurs in tropical fruits below 10–13 °C.
- [ ] **Freezing** — −18 °C commercial; a<sub>w</sub> < 0.6; **IQF is better than slow freezing**.
- [ ] **Blanching** — 80–100 °C, 1–10 min, inactivates **PPO, POD, PME**.
- [ ] **Pasteurization** — 60–90 °C, kills vegetative cells, **NOT spores**.
- [ ] **Sterilization** — 121.1 °C, 12-D reduction of *C. botulinum*.
- [ ] **Canning steps** — sort → wash → peel → blanch → fill → exhaust → seam → retort → cool.
- [ ] **Drying methods** — sun, hot-air, spray, drum, freeze, osmotic, fluidized-bed, microwave.
- [ ] **Critical moisture content** — end of constant-rate drying.
- [ ] **Chemical preservatives** — NaCl, sugar, vinegar, **Na-benzoate (0.1 %), K-sorbate (0.1 %), SO<sub>2</sub> (350–2000 ppm)**.
- [ ] **Fermentation** — LAB → lactic acid → pH < 4.0.
- [ ] **Irradiation** — 0.05–0.15 kGy (sprout inhibition), 1–3 kGy (shelf-life), max 10 MeV.
- [ ] **MAP** — 2–5 % O<sub>2</sub>, 3–10 % CO<sub>2</sub>, passive equilibrium.
- [ ] **CA Storage** — 1–3 % O<sub>2</sub>, active gas control, sealed rooms.
- [ ] **Hurdle Technology** — multi-hurdle, synergistic, mild, **Leistner (1995)**.
- [ ] **D-value** — t = D × log(N<sub>0</sub>/N).
- [ ] **Z-value** — temp change for 10× change in D.
- [ ] **F<sub>0</sub> value** — sterilization equivalent at 121.1 °C; F<sub>0</sub> ≥ 3 min for low-acid canned.
- [ ] **Q<sub>10</sub>** — rate factor for 10 °C change; Q<sub>10</sub> ≈ 2–3 for biological processes.
- [ ] **Freezing point depression** — ΔT<sub>f</sub> = 1.86 × m (K<sub>f</sub> for water).
- [ ] **Arrhenius** — k = A · exp(−E<sub>a</sub>/RT).
- [ ] **Moisture content conversion** — MC<sub>db</sub> = MC<sub>wb</sub> / (1 − MC<sub>wb</sub>).

---

**End of Module 4 — General Methods of Preservation of Fruits and Vegetables**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PCAGT601) — Post Harvest Engineering of Cereals, Oil Seeds and Horticultural Crops.*

topics.title: Flowcharts for Preparation of Different Finished Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Flowcharts for Preparation of Different Finished Products ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Post-Harvest Engineering (PHT)**, a **finished product** is defined as any edible or industrially usable commodity obtained after a defined sequence of physical, thermal, mechanical, and/or biochemical unit operations applied to raw harvested agricultural produce (cereals, oilseeds, or horticultural crops). A **flowchart** for product preparation is a standardized **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** that visually represents every sequential unit operation — from raw material reception through cleaning, grading, conditioning, processing, preservation, packaging, and final storage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A finished product is the output of a sequence of unit operations (cleaning → grading → pre-conditioning → primary processing → secondary processing → preservation → packaging) that transforms raw agricultural biomass into a shelf-stable, market-ready, value-added commodity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a flowchart as a **kitchen recipe written as a railway map**: - The **raw harvest** is the *starting station* (e.g., paddy, mustard seed, mango). - Each **processing machine** is a *stop along the route* (cleaner, drier, dehuller, expeller). - The **finished product** is the *destination station* (rice, refined oil, mango pulp). Just as a chef follows a recipe to convert raw vegetables into pickles, a Food Process Engineer follows a flowchart to convert raw crops into finished goods. The arrows in the flowchart tell you **which way the material flows**, and the boxes tell you **what transformation is happening**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** The flowchart for *parboiled rice* is similar to the steps a homemaker follows to make *pressed rice (poha)* — soak → steam → dry → flake — only done at industrial scale with controlled parameters. ### 1.3 Classification of Finished Products Covered in KTU Module 4 | Category | Examples of Finished Products | |---|---| | **Cereal-based** | Milled rice, parboiled rice, wheat flour (*atta/maida*), semolina (*suji/rava*), cornflakes, puffed rice | | **Oilseed-based** | Crude vegetable oil, refined oil, oil cake, Vanaspati, mustard oil, coconut oil | | **Horticultural-based** | Fruit juice, fruit pulp, jam, jelly, marmalade, ketchup, pickles, dehydrated vegetables, tomato puree | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The emphasis is on **drawing and interpreting flowcharts**, identifying **unit operations in sequence**, and understanding the **purpose of each operation** — not on the design of individual equipment. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Module 4 - **Moisture Content (MC):** Expressed as **% wet basis (w.b.)** — *safe storage MC for cereals is ≤ **14%**, for oilseeds ≤ **10%**, for dried fruits ≤ **20%***. - **Recovery Yield (η):** Defined as mass of finished product ÷ mass of raw material input × 100. - **Oil Extraction Efficiency (E):** Mass of oil extracted ÷ mass of oil present in seed × 100. - **Shelf Life:** Period for which the product remains microbiologically and chemically stable under prescribed storage conditions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Flowcharts Matter in PHT A flowchart is the **engineer's blueprint of a food process**. It serves four critical purposes: 1. **Process Standardization:** Ensures every batch follows the same sequence — vital for FSSAI/HACCP compliance. 2. **Mass & Energy Balance:** Each node becomes a balance point where inputs (raw material, water, steam, electricity) and outputs (product, by-products, waste) can be quantified. 3. **Quality Control:** Critical Control Points (CCPs) can be identified at specific nodes. 4. **Troubleshooting:** If finished product quality drops, the engineer retraces the flowchart to isolate the faulty operation. ### 2.2 Generic Master Flowchart — Universal Pattern Almost every finished product in Module 4 follows this **6-stage generic template**: 1. **Raw Material Reception & Inspection** — weighing, sampling, quality grading. 2. **Pre-conditioning** — cleaning, de-stoning, magnetic separation, drying (to safe MC). 3. **Primary Processing** — dehulling, dehusking, decorticating, size reduction. 4. **Secondary Processing** — milling, extraction, blending, cooking, formulation. 5. **Preservation / Stabilization** — pasteurization, sterilization, dehydration, fermentation, chemical preservation. 6. **Packaging & Storage** — filling, sealing, labeling, warehouse storage. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic to remember the 6 stages:** **R – P – P – S – P – P** → *Receive → Pre-treat → Process → Stabilize → Pack*. This single acronym covers flowcharts of rice, oil, jam, juice, and pickles alike. ### 2.3 Cereal-Based Finished Products — Logic Steps #### 2.3.1 Milled Rice (Raw) - Cleaning → De-stoning → Paddy Separator → Dehusking (Rubber Roll Sheller) → Paddy Re-separation → Brown Rice → Polishing (Cone/Brush Polisher) → Grading (Indented Cylinder) → Color Sorting → Weighing → Bagging. #### 2.3.2 Parboiled Rice - Cleaning → Soaking (60–70 °C, 3–4 h) → Steaming (0.5–1.0 kg/cm², 15–20 min) → Drying (Sun/Mech, MC 14%) → Tempering → Milling (as above) → Polishing → Grading. #### 2.3.3 Wheat Flour (Atta) & Maida - Cleaning → Conditioning (Tempering to 14–16% MC, rest 12–24 h) → Breaking (First Break Roll) → Gristing → Reduction (Smooth Rolls) → Sifting (Plansifter) → Purification → Blending → Storage → Bagging. ### 2.4 Oilseed-Based Finished Products — Logic Steps #### 2.4.1 Crude Vegetable Oil (Mechanical Expeller) - Cleaning → Dehulling (optional for groundnut) → Cooking/Conditioning (60–90 °C, 10–15% moisture) → Flaking / Cracking → Expeller Pressing (Mechanical) → Crude Oil + Oil Cake (residual oil ~ 6–8%). - For higher recovery → Solvent Extraction with *n*-hexane → Miscella → Desolventizer → Refined Oil + De-oiled Cake. #### 2.4.2 Refined Oil - Crude Oil → Degumming (water/phosphoric acid) → Neutralization (NaOH) → Bleaching (Earth/Activated Carbon) → Deodorization (Steam, 220–250 °C, vacuum) → Polishing → Packaging. ### 2.5 Horticultural-Based Finished Products — Logic Steps #### 2.5.1 Fruit Juice (e.g., Mango/MOSAMBI) - Reception → Washing → Sorting → Peeling/Blanching (75 °C, 3 min) → Pulping → Straining → Standardization (°Brix & Acid) → Pasteurization (90 °C, 1–2 min) → Hot Filling → Cooling → Storage. #### 2.5.2 Jam / Jelly - Fruit Pulping → Addition of Sugar (1:1 or 1:0.67) + Pectin + Citric Acid → Boiling (up to 105–106 °C, 65–68% TSS) → Testing End Point (Sheet / Drop test) → Hot Filling → Cooling → Waxing → Labeling. #### 2.5.3 Tomato Ketchup - Washing → Sorting → Crushing → Pulping → Standardization (TSS, Acidity, Salt) → Boiling (concentrate to 28–30% TS) → Addition of Onion, Garlic, Spice Extract, Vinegar → Boiling → Pasteurization (88 °C, 30 min) → Hot Filling → Cooling. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Value / Unit | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | Moisture Content (w.b.) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | **14 %** cereals, **10 %** oilseeds | Drying step | | Moisture Content (d.b.) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | unitless | Engineering design | | Recovery Yield | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{product}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$ | **%** | All flowcharts | | Oil Extraction Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{m_{oil,extracted}}{m_{oil,seed}} \times 100$ | **95–98 %** (solvent) | Oil flowchart | | Overall Milling Recovery | $R_{mill} = \dfrac{m_{milled\,rice}}{m_{paddy}} \times 100$ | **65–70 %** | Rice flowchart | | Concentration Ratio (juice to concentrate) | $CR = \dfrac{V_{juice}}{V_{conc}}$ | 4:1 to 6:1 | Juice flowchart | | °Brix Correction for Temperature | $CF = T \times 0.5$ (approx) | — | Juice standardization | | Material Balance at any node | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + m_{loss}$ | **kg/h** | Mass balance | | Energy Balance at any node | $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out} + Q_{loss}$ | **kJ/h** | Heat balance | | Shelf-Life (Accelerated) | $t_s = \dfrac{Q_{10}}{2^{\Delta T/10}}$ | days/weeks | Packaging | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall:** In the table above, the symbol `\vert` (or `\mid`) must be used in place of the vertical pipe `\vert` character inside any markdown table to prevent syntax breakage. Note the proper LaTeX rendering of all formulas. ### 2.7 Real-World Utility of Flowcharts - **Industry:** Used for plant layout, equipment selection, capacity planning, and ISO/HACCP documentation. - **Research:** Used to compare processing methods (e.g., parboiled vs raw rice; cold-pressed vs expeller oil). - **Economics:** Cost per kg of finished product is computed node-by-node along the flowchart. - **Sustainability:** Waste streams (husk, bran, cake, peel) can be quantified and routed to by-product utilization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Approach to Drawing a KTU Flowchart Every flowchart question in KTU Module 4 can be solved by applying the following **3-layer analytical framework**: **Layer 1 — Identification:** Identify the *raw material* and the *finished product*. This fixes the *starting node* and *ending node*. **Layer 2 — Unit Operation Selection:** Map each transformation (raw → finished) to one or more unit operations from this standard set: $$ \mathcal{U} = \{\text{Clean, Sort, Grade, Dry, Soak, Steam, Dehull, Mill, Extract, Filter, Blend, Heat, Cool, Pack}\} $$ **Layer 3 — Sequencing & Mass Balance:** Arrange selected operations in the correct sequence and verify mass balance at each node. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Parboiled Rice **Problem:** Draw and explain the flowchart for the preparation of parboiled rice. Determine the overall milling recovery if 1000 kg paddy at 22 % MC (w.b.) is processed and the final rice has 14 % MC (w.b.). **Step 1 — Raw Material Receipt:** Mass of paddy input: $m_{in} = 1000 \text{ kg}$ **Step 2 — Cleaning & Grading:** Loss in dust, stones, immature grains: ~ **2 %** of input. $$ m_{cleaned} = 1000 \times 0.98 = 980 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Soaking:** Water absorbed: paddy goes from 22 % to ~ **30 %** MC (w.b.) on saturated basis. Dry matter entering soak tank: $$ m_{dm} = 980 \times (1 - 0.22) = 764.4 \text{ kg dry matter} $$ After soaking, total wet mass: $$ m_{soaked} = \dfrac{764.4}{1 - 0.30} = 1092 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 4 — Steaming:** Partial gelatinization. No significant mass loss (steam condenses into grain). Mass ≈ $1092 \text{ kg}$. **Step 5 — Drying (to 14 % MC for milling):** Dry matter preserved: $$ m_{dried} = \dfrac{764.4}{1 - 0.14} = 889 \text{ kg} $$ Water removed in drying: $$ m_{water,out} = 1092 - 889 = 203 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 6 — Milling (Dehusking + Polishing):** Bran and husk constitute ~ **28–30 %** of paddy mass. Milling recovery for parboiled rice (typical) = **68–72 %**. Taking **70 %** recovery on cleaned paddy basis: $$ m_{rice} = 889 \times 0.70 = 622.3 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 7 — Verification — Overall Recovery:** $$ \eta_{overall} = \dfrac{m_{rice}}{m_{paddy,in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{622.3}{1000} \times 100 = 62.2\% $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 62\,\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Parboiled rice gives *higher head-rice yield* (less breakage during milling) than raw rice, even though overall recovery is slightly lower due to bran removal. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Unit Operation × Product | Unit Operation | Rice | Wheat Flour | Mustard Oil | Mango Pulp | Tomato Ketchup | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Cleaning** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | **Drying** | ✓ (parboil) | ✓ (tempering) | – | – | – | | **Soaking/Steaming** | ✓ (parboil) | – | – | ✓ (blanch) | – | | **Dehusking/Dehulling** | ✓ | – | ✓ (decort.) | – | – | | **Milling/Crushing** | ✓ | ✓ (roller) | ✓ (expeller) | ✓ (pulper) | ✓ (crusher) | | **Extraction** | – | – | ✓ (solvent) | ✓ (juicing) | – | | **Heating/Boiling** | ✓ (steam) | – | ✓ (cook) | ✓ (pasteurize) | ✓ (concentrate) | | **Filtration/Sieving** | ✓ (grading) | ✓ (sifter) | ✓ (filter) | ✓ (strainer) | ✓ (strainer) | | **Preservation** | ✓ (dry MC) | ✓ (dry MC) | ✓ (refining) | ✓ (pasteurize) | ✓ (vinegar+salt) | | **Packaging** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ### 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step for Tomato Ketchup (Numerical Estimation) **Given:** 100 kg fresh tomatoes, **5 %** TSS initially. Target ketchup **28 %** TSS. **Step 1 — TSS balance (no loss assumed):** $$ m_{conc} = m_{raw} \times \dfrac{TSS_{raw}}{TSS_{prod}} = 100 \times \dfrac{5}{28} = 17.86 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2 — Water evaporated:** $$ m_{water,evap} = 100 - 17.86 = 82.14 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Add sugar, salt, vinegar, spices (~ 1.5 kg mix):** $$ m_{ketchup} = 17.86 + 1.5 = 19.36 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Yield} = 19.36\,\%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Roughly **5 kg of fresh tomatoes are required to produce 1 kg of ketchup.** This is a typical KTU Part-B 14-mark sub-question. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > All flowcharts below are drawn in **Mermaid (graph LR)** syntax. Node IDs are strictly alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `sA`). No reserved keywords are used as node labels. ### 4.1 Master Template — Generic Flowchart for Finished Products ```mermaid graph LR n1["A: RAW MATERIAL
RECEPTION"] --> n2["B: CLEANING AND
DE-STONING"] n2 --> n3["C: GRADING AND
SORTING"] n3 --> n4["D: PRE-CONDITIONING
(DRY/SOAK/STEAM)"] n4 --> n5["E: PRIMARY
PROCESSING"] n5 --> n6["F: SECONDARY
PROCESSING"] n6 --> n7["G: PRESERVATION
(HEAT/CHEM)"] n7 --> n8["H: PACKAGING
AND STORAGE"] n8 --> n9["I: FINISHED
PRODUCT"] n5 -. BY-PRODUCT .-> b1["J: HUSK/BRAN
(BY-PRODUCT)"] n6 -. WASTE .-> b2["K: EFFLUENT
(WASTE)"] ``` ### 4.2 Flowchart for Milled Rice (Raw Paddy) ```mermaid graph LR r1["PADDY
RECEPTION"] --> r2["DRYING
TO 14% MC"] r2 --> r3["CLEANING AND
DE-STONING"] r3 --> r4["DEHUSKING
RUBBER ROLL"] r4 --> r5["PADDY
SEPARATOR"] r5 --> r6["BROWN RICE
TO POLISHER"] r5 --> r7["UNHUSKED
RECYCLE TO r4"] r6 --> r8["POLISHING
CONE/BRUSH"] r8 --> r9["GRADING
INDENTED CYLINDER"] r9 --> r10["COLOR
SORTING"] r10 --> r11["WEIGHING
AND BAGGING"] r11 --> r12["MILLED RICE
FINISHED PRODUCT"] r4 -. HUSK .-> rb1["BOILER
FUEL"] r8 -. BRAN .-> rb2["OIL
EXTRACTION"] ``` ### 4.3 Flowchart for Parboiled Rice ```mermaid graph LR p1["PADDY
RECEPTION"] --> p2["CLEANING"] p2 --> p3["SOAKING
60-70 C, 3-4 H"] p3 --> p4["STEAMING
0.5-1 KG/CM2"] p4 --> p5["DRYING
SUN/MECH TO 14% MC"] p5 --> p6["TEMPERING
12-24 H"] p6 --> p7["DEHUSKING AND
POLISHING"] p7 --> p8["GRADING"] p8 --> p9["COLOR
SORTING"] p9 --> p10["PARBOILED RICE
FINISHED PRODUCT"] p7 -. BY-PRODUCT .-> pb1["HUSK AND BRAN
TO BOILER"] ``` ### 4.4 Flowchart for Wheat Flour (Atta/Maida) ```mermaid graph LR w1["WHEAT
RECEPTION"] --> w2["CLEANING
DE-STONING"] w2 --> w3["MAGNETIC
SEPARATION"] w3 --> w4["TEMPERING
14-16% MC, REST"] w4 --> w5["FIRST BREAK
ROLLER MILL"] w5 --> w6["GRISTING AND
SIFTING"] w6 --> w7["REDUCTION
SMOOTH ROLLS"] w7 --> w8["PLANSIFTER
CLASSIFICATION"] w8 --> w9["PURIFICATION
AIRSIFTER"] w9 --> w10["BLENDING
TO SPEC"] w10 --> w11["FLOUR
FINISHED PRODUCT"] w8 -. BRAN .-> wb1["BRAN
BY-PRODUCT"] w5 -. GERM .-> wb2["GERM
OIL SOURCE"] ``` ### 4.5 Flowchart for Refined Vegetable Oil (Solvent + Refining) ```mermaid graph LR o1["OILSEED
RECEPTION"] --> o2["CLEANING AND
DEHULLING"] o2 --> o3["CRACKING AND
FLAKING"] o3 --> o4["COOKING
60-90 C"] o4 --> o5["MECHANICAL
PRESSING"] o5 --> o6["SOLVENT
EXTRACTION"] o6 --> o7["DESOLVENTIZER
TOASTER"] o7 --> o8["CRUDE OIL
COLLECTION"] o8 --> o9["DEGUMMING
H3PO4/H2O"] o9 --> o10["NEUTRALIZATION
NaOH"] o10 --> o11["BLEACHING
EARTH/CARBON"] o11 --> o12["DEODORIZATION
220-250 C, VACUUM"] o12 --> o13["POLISHING
FILTRATION"] o13 --> o14["REFINED OIL
FINISHED PRODUCT"] o7 -. CAKE .-> ob1["DE-OILED CAKE
FEED/MEAL"] o5 -. OIL CAKE .-> ob1 ``` ### 4.6 Flowchart for Mango Pulp / Juice ```mermaid graph LR j1["MANGO
RECEPTION"] --> j2["WASHING
AND SORTING"] j2 --> j3["BLANCHING
75 C, 3 MIN"] j3 --> j4["PEELING AND
PITTING"] j4 --> j5["PULPING
PULPER"] j5 --> j6["STRAINING
FINISHER"] j6 --> j7["STANDARDIZATION
BRIX AND ACID"] j7 --> j8["PASTEURIZATION
90 C, 1-2 MIN"] j8 --> j9["HOT FILLING
INTO CANS/JARS"] j9 --> j10["COOLING
AND LABELING"] j10 --> j11["MANGO PULP
FINISHED PRODUCT"] j4 -. PEEL/PIT .-> jb1["PEEL WASTE
BY-PRODUCT"] ``` ### 4.7 Flowchart for Tomato Ketchup ```mermaid graph LR k1["TOMATO
RECEPTION"] --> k2["WASHING
AND SORTING"] k2 --> k3["CRUSHING
PULPER"] k3 --> k4["PULPING AND
FINISHING"] k4 --> k5["STANDARDIZATION
TSS 5% RAW"] k5 --> k6["BOILING
CONCENTRATE TO 28% TSS"] k6 --> k7["ADD SPICES
SALT, SUGAR, VINEGAR"] k7 --> k8["PASTEURIZATION
88 C, 30 MIN"] k8 --> k9["HOT FILLING
INTO BOTTLES"] k9 --> k10["COOLING
AND LABELING"] k10 --> k11["TOMATO KETCHUP
FINISHED PRODUCT"] k3 -. SKIN/SEED .-> kb1["TOMATO WASTE
BY-PRODUCT"] ``` ### 4.8 Flowchart for Jam / Jelly ```mermaid graph LR jm1["FRUIT
RECEPTION"] --> jm2["WASHING AND
SORTING"] jm2 --> jm3["PEELING AND
PITTING"] jm3 --> jm4["PULPING
PECTIN SOURCE"] jm4 --> jm5["ADD SUGAR
1:1 RATIO"] jm5 --> jm6["ADD CITRIC ACID
AND PECTIN"] jm6 --> jm7["BOILING
105-106 C, 65-68% TSS"] jm7 --> jm8["END-POINT TEST
SHEET/DROP"] jm8 --> jm9["HOT FILLING
INTO GLASS JARS"] jm9 --> jm10["COOLING
WAXING, LABELING"] jm10 --> jm11["JAM/JELLY
FINISHED PRODUCT"] jm3 -. PEEL .-> jmb1["PEEL WASTE
BY-PRODUCT"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define "finished product" in the context of post-harvest engineering. Give two examples each from cereals, oilseeds, and horticultural crops.** **Model Answer:** A finished product is the shelf-stable, market-ready commodity obtained by applying a defined sequence of unit operations to raw harvested produce. - *Cereals:* Milled rice, wheat flour (maida), parboiled rice. - *Oilseeds:* Refined mustard oil, groundnut oil, vanaspati. - *Horticultural crops:* Mango pulp, tomato ketchup, fruit jam. *(3 marks — definition 1 m, examples 2 m)* #### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List any six unit operations commonly used in the preparation of finished products from horticultural crops.** **Model Answer:** 1. Cleaning/Washing 2. Sorting and Grading 3. Peeling/Blanching 4. Pulping/Size Reduction 5. Pasteurization/Sterilization 6. Hot Filling and Packaging *(3 marks — 0.5 m each)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** **(a)** Draw a detailed flowchart for the preparation of **parboiled rice** and explain each operation in sequence. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A rice mill processes **1000 kg of paddy at 22 % MC (w.b.)**. The cleaned and dried paddy enters the mill at **14 % MC (w.b.)**. The milling recovery is **70 %**. Determine: - (i) Mass of dried paddy going to the mill. - (ii) Mass of head rice produced. - (iii) Mass of bran and husk generated. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** | Step | Unit Operation | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | Reception & Cleaning | Remove dust, stones, chaff | | 2 | Soaking (60–70 °C, 3–4 h) | Hydrate grain to 30 % MC for gelatinization | | 3 | Steaming (0.5–1.0 kg/cm², 15–20 min) | Gelatinize starch inside grain | | 4 | Drying (sun/mechanical, 14 % MC) | Bring down MC for safe storage & milling | | 5 | Tempering (12–24 h) | Equalize moisture inside kernel | | 6 | Dehusking & Polishing | Remove husk & bran layers | | 7 | Grading & Color Sorting | Separate brokens, discoloured grains | | 8 | Packaging | Bag in 25/50 kg for market | **Model Solution for (b):** Dry matter in raw paddy: $$ m_{dm} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.22) = 780 \text{ kg} $$ **(i) Mass of dried paddy at 14 % MC:** $$ m_{dried} = \dfrac{780}{1 - 0.14} = 907 \text{ kg} $$ **(ii) Mass of head rice produced (at 70 % recovery):** $$ m_{rice} = 907 \times 0.70 = 634.9 \text{ kg} $$ **(iii) Mass of bran + husk generated:** $$ m_{byproduct} = 907 - 634.9 = 272.1 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{m_{rice} = 634.9 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{byproduct} = 272.1 \text{ kg}} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Alternative]** **(a)** Draw a detailed flowchart for the preparation of **refined vegetable oil from mustard seed** and explain the functions of the degumming, neutralization, bleaching, and deodorization operations. *(7 marks)* **(b)** In a tomato ketchup plant, **200 kg of fresh tomatoes (TSS = 5 %)** are processed to produce ketchup of **28 % TSS**. Spices, sugar, salt, and vinegar added = **3 kg**. Calculate: - (i) Mass of concentrated pulp after boiling. - (ii) Mass of water evaporated. - (iii) Total mass of ketchup produced and the **overall yield**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** *Flowchart (refer Section 4.5 above).* | Refining Step | Reagent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Degumming | Water / Phosphoric acid | Remove phosphatides, gums | | Neutralization | NaOH (caustic soda) | Free fatty acids → soap (removed) | | Bleaching | Activated earth / carbon | Remove pigments, trace metals | | Deodorization | Steam, 220–250 °C, vacuum | Strip volatile odoriferous compounds | *(7 marks — flowchart 4 m + table 3 m)* **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Mass of concentrated pulp (TSS balance):** $$ m_{pulp} = 200 \times \dfrac{5}{28} = 35.71 \text{ kg} $$ **(ii) Mass of water evaporated:** $$ m_{evap} = 200 - 35.71 = 164.29 \text{ kg} $$ **(iii) Total mass of ketchup and yield:** $$ m_{ketchup} = 35.71 + 3 = 38.71 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \eta = \dfrac{38.71}{200} \times 100 = 19.36\,\% $$ $$ \boxed{m_{ketchup} = 38.71 \text{ kg}, \quad \eta = 19.36\,\%} $$ --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions on Flowchart Questions:** > 1. **Missing by-product streams:** Students draw only the main product line and forget arrows for husk, bran, oil cake, peel, and waste water. Deduct **1–2 marks** if by-products are not shown. > 2. **Wrong node order:** Putting *polishing before dehusking*, or *packaging before pasteurization*, is a serious sequencing error — deduct **2 marks**. > 3. **No temperatures/pressures mentioned:** KTU examiners reward numerical specifications (e.g., *60–70 °C soaking*, *0.5–1 kg/cm² steaming*). Omission costs **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Unit confusion:** Writing moisture content in d.b. where w.b. is asked, or mixing units of kg and quintals within the same numerical — **1 mark** deduction. > 5. **Unlabelled arrows:** Every arrow in a KTU flowchart should ideally be labelled with the *material flowing* (e.g., "raw paddy", "steam", "crude oil"). Missing labels cost **1 mark**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Generic 6-stage template:** Receive → Pre-treat → Process → Stabilize → Pack. Memorize as **R-P-P-S-P-P** mnemonic. - ✅ **Safe moisture levels:** **≤ 14 %** for cereals, **≤ 10 %** for oilseeds, **≤ 20 %** for dried horticultural products. - ✅ **Parboiled rice advantages:** Higher head-rice yield, harder grain, more nutritious (B-vitamins retained inside endosperm). - ✅ **Wheat conditioning:** Tempering to 14–16 % MC and resting for 12–24 h is *critical* before roller milling — without this, bran shatters and contaminates flour. - ✅ **Solvent extraction:** Used for *n*-hexane recovery up to **98 %**; followed by **desolventizer-toaster** to recover residual solvent from cake. - ✅ **Refining sequence is fixed:** **Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization → Polishing**. Reversing the order gives poor-quality oil. - ✅ **Fruit juice pasteurization:** **90 °C for 1–2 minutes** is industry standard; over-pasteurization destroys vitamin C and flavor. - ✅ **Jam end-point:** Test at **105–106 °C** with **65–68 % TSS** (sheet/drop test); under-boil = soft set; over-boil = caramelization. - ✅ **Ketchup concentration:** From 5 % TSS to 28 % TSS — typically **5 kg tomatoes per 1 kg ketchup**. - ✅ **Flowchart arrows:** Always show *by-products* (husk, bran, cake, peel, seed) branching off the main line. - ✅ **Mass balance must close:** $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{product} + \sum m_{byproduct} + \sum m_{loss}$ at every node. - ✅ **FSSAI relevance:** All finished products in this module must comply with FSSAI standards — MC, TSS, acidity, pesticide residue. - ✅ **Energy efficiency:** Always identify the most energy-intensive node (e.g., steam generation in parboiling, deodorization in oil refining) — examiners love this as an extension question. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Mantra:** *"A flowchart is not a list — it is a directional, sequential, mass-balanced process map with by-products included. Draw arrows, label them, specify temperatures, and always close your mass balance."*

> - **Course:** PCAGT601 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF CEREALS, OIL SEEDS AND HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Important Parameters and Equipment Used for Different Unit Operations

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Post-Harvest Unit Operations?

In the context of KTU's PCAGT601 syllabus, a **Unit Operation** is defined as a *fundamental physical, mechanical, thermal, or biological step* applied to an agricultural commodity after it leaves the farm gate, intended to preserve quality, reduce losses, enhance shelf-life, or add value. Post-harvest unit operations form the **engineered bridge** between raw harvest and final consumer product.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** Unit operations are individual, identifiable processing stages — such as cleaning, grading, drying, milling, and storage — whose process parameters can be independently measured, controlled, and optimized to convert raw agricultural produce into a stable, market-ready commodity.

The complete post-harvest chain typically follows the sequence:

$$ \text{Harvest} \rightarrow \text{Pre-conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Sorting/Grading} \rightarrow \text{Drying} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Processing} \rightarrow \text{Packaging} $$

Each arrow in this flow represents a *unit operation* governed by **independent engineering parameters** and served by **dedicated equipment**.

---

## 1.2 Categorization of Unit Operations

| S. No | Unit Operation Category | Governing Principle | Example Operations |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Mechanical Separation** | Size, shape, density, color | Cleaning, Sorting, Grading |
| 2 | **Thermal Processing** | Heat & mass transfer | Drying, Parboiling, Pasteurization |
| 3 | **Size Reduction** | Comminution mechanics | Milling, Crushing, Grinding |
| 4 | **Material Handling** | Bulk solids flow | Conveying, Elevating, Bagging |
| 5 | **Storage & Preservation** | Microbiology + environment | Cold storage, Hermetic storage, CA storage |
| 6 | **Extraction** | Mechanical / chemical | Oil expelling, Solvent extraction |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Automobile Service Center" View

Imagine you drive a car into a service center. The car first goes through a **wash bay** (cleaning), then **inspection ramp** (sorting/grading by defect), then **paint booth** (drying), then **engine tuning** (milling/processing), and finally **parking garage** (storage).

Each bay has its **own dashboard of parameters** — water pressure for washing, temperature for paint drying, oil viscosity for engine tuning — and its **own specialist equipment**. Post-harvest engineering works exactly the same way: every commodity passes through *engineered stations*, each with controlled parameters and purpose-built equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "parameters," remember the 4 universal engineering parameters of any post-harvest process: **(1) Throughput/Capacity (kg/h)**, **(2) Efficiency (%)**, **(3) Product Quality (grade/purity)**, and **(4) Energy/Heat input (kW or kJ/kg)**. Examiners award marks for explicitly naming these.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Carries High KTU Weightage

Module-4 of PCAGT601 is specifically a **"process-engineering" module** — the Board Examiners test whether a student can:
- Identify the *correct equipment* for a *given commodity*.
- Justify equipment selection based on **measurable parameters**.
- Sketch and explain working of standard dryers, cleaners, and sorters.

This topic typically contributes **14–20 marks** in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) and **2 marks** in internal Quiz/Assignment slots.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operation #1 — CLEANING

### 2.1.1 Definition
Cleaning is the **removal of foreign matter** (dust, chaff, stones, stalks, weed seeds, immature grains, insect fragments, metal particles) from the harvested commodity using mechanical, aerodynamic, or magnetic forces.

### 2.1.2 Important Parameters
- **Moisture content of grain** (%, w.b.) — affects flowability and adhesion of dust.
- **Bulk density** (kg/m³) — typically **600–780 kg/m³** for cereals.
- **Impurity percentage** — initial vs final (efficiency benchmark).
- **Screen aperture size** (mm) — must be matched to grain dimensions.
- **Air velocity** (m/s) — for pneumatic/aspiration cleaning.
- **Feed rate** (kg/h or t/h) — determines throughput.
- **Angle of screen inclination** (°) — typically **8°–15°** for vibrating screens.

### 2.1.3 Equipment Used

| Equipment | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Scalper / Pre-cleaner** | Coarse sieving | Removal of large straw/stalks |
| **Indented cylinder** | Triangular pockets capture short grains | Length-based separation |
| **Vibrating separator** | Inertial + gravity on perforated deck | Multi-fraction grading |
| **Air-screen cleaner** | Combined aspiration + sieving | Cereal seed cleaning |
| **Magnetic separator** | Ferrous metal capture | Decontamination of feed grain |
| **Specific gravity separator (Gravity table)** | Density-driven stratification | Removal of stones, light grains |
| **Spiral separator** | Centrifugal + gravitational | Round vs flat grain separation |
| **Drum screener (Rotary)** | Tumbling on cylindrical mesh | Pre-cleaning of free-flowing grains |
| **Bucket elevator (cleaner-conveyer)** | Lifting + cascading dust removal | Bulk cereal plants |
| **Air-classifier / Aspirator** | Vertical air column | Light chaff & dust removal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** *Specific Gravity Separator (Gravity Table)* is the single most-tested cleaning equipment. Its principle is based on **Stratification by density under vibration + upward airflow**, producing three fractions: light (float), mixed (middle), and heavy (sink).

### 2.1.4 Cleaning Efficiency Formula

$$
\eta_{\text{cleaning}} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{initial}} - C_{\text{final}}}{C_{\text{initial}}} \right) \times 100
$$

Where $C_{\text{initial}}$ and $C_{\text{final}}$ are concentrations of impurities before and after cleaning (in kg impurity per kg sample). The value is **dimensionless** and expressed as **%**.

---

## 2.2 Unit Operation #2 — SORTING & GRADING

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Sorting** = separation based on *quality* (defective vs. sound).
**Grading** = separation based on *size, weight, or maturity* into uniform commercial classes.

### 2.2.2 Important Parameters
- **Size parameters** — length $L$, breadth $B$, thickness $T$ (mm).
- **Geometric mean diameter** $D_g = (L \cdot B \cdot T)^{1/3}$.
- **Sphericity** $\phi = (D_g / L)$.
- **Bulk density** (kg/m³).
- **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - (\rho_b / \rho_p)$.
- **Terminal velocity** $V_t$ (m/s) — used in pneumatic sorters.
- **Color coordinates** — $L^*, a^*, b^*$ (CIELAB) for optical sorters.
- **Defect percentage** (% by weight or count).

### 2.2.3 Equipment Used

| Equipment | Basis of Separation | Typical Product |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Drum screen / Rotary grader** | Size (diameter) | Potato, onion, apple |
| **Vibrating screen** | Size + stratification | Grain, pulse |
| **Disc separator (Trieur)** | Length | Wheat, oats, barley |
| **Spiral separator** | Shape (round vs flat) | Mustard, lentils |
| **Inclined draper / Belt grader** | Length & flatness | Rice, coffee bean |
| **Gravity table** | Density | Seed, pulse |
| **Optical / Color sorter** | Color & defect | Rice, dal, cashew |
| **Roll / Indent separator** | Length (cylinder indent) | Long/short rice |
| **Electronic sorter (CCD camera + air-jet)** | Spectral signature | High-end nut & fruit |
| **Hydro-cyclone separator** | Specific gravity (fluid medium) | Spice, starch |

---

## 2.3 Unit Operation #3 — DRYING

### 2.3.1 Definition
Drying is the **simultaneous heat and mass transfer process** in which **moisture is removed from a biological product by evaporation into a heated air stream**, lowering water activity and arresting microbial/enzymatic spoilage.

### 2.3.2 Important Parameters
- **Initial Moisture Content (IMC)** — % w.b. (e.g., paddy: **22–24%**).
- **Final Moisture Content (FMC)** — % w.b. (e.g., paddy for safe storage: **12–14%**).
- **Air temperature** ($T_a$, °C) — typically **40–80 °C** for grains.
- **Relative Humidity** (RH, %) — inlet and outlet.
- **Air flow rate** ($V$, m³/min per tonne).
- **Drying rate** ($R$, % moisture removed per hour).
- **Thin-layer drying constant** ($k$, h⁻¹).
- **Equilibrium moisture content** (EMC, % d.b.).
- **Specific energy consumption** (kJ/kg water evaporated).
- **Grain temperature** (must remain **< 45 °C** for seed quality).

### 2.3.3 Equipment Used

| Dryer Type | Mode | Commodity | Special Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Sun drying (yard)** | Batch | Paddy, pulses | Low cost, weather-dependent |
| **Flat-bed batch dryer** | Batch | Paddy, maize | Simple construction |
| **Columnar / Vertical batch dryer** | Batch | Paddy | Perforated bin + plenum |
| **LSU (Louisiana State University) dryer** | Batch-recirculating | Paddy | High capacity, mixing |
| **Mixed-flow dryer** | Continuous | Paddy, wheat | Alternating hot/cold air columns |
| **Cross-flow dryer** | Continuous | Wheat, maize | Grain moves perpendicular to airflow |
| **Concurrent-flow dryer** | Continuous | Paddy | High-temp inlet, low-temp grain exit |
| **Counter-flow dryer** | Continuous | Maize | Uniform final moisture |
| **Fluidized-bed dryer** | Continuous/particulate | Corn, pulses | High heat transfer coefficient |
| **Rotary dryer** | Continuous | Granular products | Tumbling action |
| **Solar dryer (cabinet/tunnel)** | Batch | Fruits, spices | Renewable energy |
| **Spray dryer** | Continuous | Milk, extract | Liquid-to-powder |
| **Drum / Roller dryer** | Continuous | Pulp, slurry | Heated rotating drum |
| **Infrared / Microwave dryer** | Continuous | Value-added snacks | Volumetric heating |
| **Heat-pump assisted dryer** | Batch | Herbs, spices | Low-temp, high-efficiency |
| **Bin dryer with aeration** | Low-temp drying | Stored grain | In-storage moisture control |

### 2.3.4 Core Drying Equations

#### Moisture Content Definitions

$$
M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100
$$

Where $W_w$ = mass of water (kg), $W_d$ = mass of dry matter (kg).

#### Conversion between wet and dry basis

$$
M_{db} = \frac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}
$$

#### Thin-Layer Drying Model (Page's equation — KTU-recommended)

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

Where:
- $MR$ = Moisture Ratio (dimensionless, 0 to 1).
- $M_t$ = moisture content at time $t$ (% d.b.).
- $M_0$ = initial moisture content (% d.b.).
- $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture content (% d.b.).
- $k$ = drying constant (h⁻¹).
- $n$ = Page's exponent (dimensionless, typically 0.7–1.4).

#### Drying Rate

$$
R = -\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_t}{t}
$$

Units: **% moisture per hour** (%/h).

#### Specific Energy Consumption

$$
SEC = \frac{Q_{\text{heat input}}}{m_w}
$$

Where $m_w$ = mass of water evaporated (kg). Units: **kJ/kg water**.

---

## 2.4 Unit Operation #4 — STORAGE

### 2.4.1 Definition
Storage is the **preservation of agricultural produce over time** under controlled environmental conditions to maintain quality, viability, and quantity until consumption or further processing.

### 2.4.2 Important Parameters
- **Grain moisture content** at loading (must be below **safe storage MC** — typically 12–14% w.b. for cereals).
- **Temperature** ($T$, °C) — preferably **< 25 °C**.
- **Relative Humidity** (RH, %) of inter-granular air.
- **Oxygen concentration** (%, for hermetic/CA storage).
- **CO₂ concentration** (%, for modified atmosphere).
- **Storage period** (months).
- **Insect & mould load** (count/kg).
- **Mycotoxin level** (ppb — aflatoxin B₁ limit **< 20 ppb**).
- **Bulk thermal conductivity** of grain (W/m·K).

### 2.4.3 Equipment & Structures Used

| Storage Type | Capacity | Key Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Bag storage (godown)** | 10–500 t | Traditional, low-cost |
| **Flat warehouse / Pusa bin** | 5–50 t | Improved bag storage |
| **Silo (steel)** | 100–10,000 t | Fully mechanized |
| **Silo (concrete stave)** | 500–5,000 t | Long-life |
| **Bunker / pit storage** | 50–500 t | Underground insulation |
| **CAP (Cover and Plinth)** | 500–5,000 t | Outdoor, polythene-covered |
| **Hermetic bags (PICS, GrainPro)** | 50 kg–1 t | Triple-layer, oxygen-depleted |
| **Cold storage / Cold room** | 1–1,000 t | Refrigerated, 0–5 °C |
| **CA (Controlled Atmosphere) chamber** | Variable | Modified O₂, CO₂, N₂ |
| **Grain aeration system** | Integral | Low-volume airflow for cooling |
| **In-bin drying with aeration** | Integral | Dual-purpose |

## 2.5 Unit Operation #5 — MILLING (Size Reduction)

### 2.5.1 Definition
Milling is the **mechanical reduction of particle size** of grains/seeds to produce flour, semolina, grits, meal, or dehulled products.

### 2.5.2 Important Parameters
- **Particle size distribution** (μm or mesh number).
- **Reduction ratio** (RR = feed size / product size).
- **Throughput** (kg/h).
- **Power consumption** (kWh/tonne).
- **Fineness modulus**.
- **Moisture content** of feed (typically **12–14%** for wheat milling).
- **Rift's law / Bond's law / Kick's law** constants.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Thin-Layer Drying Rate and Drying Constant

The **thin-layer drying model** is derived from **Newton's law of cooling analog**, applied to moisture transfer.

### Step 1 — Define Moisture Ratio

At any instant during drying, the moisture content of the grain is $M_t$, the initial moisture is $M_0$, and the equilibrium moisture that cannot be removed at given $T_a$ and RH is $M_e$. The **dimensionless Moisture Ratio** is:

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}
$$

> *Why define this ratio?* Because it removes the dependence on absolute moisture levels and the surrounding air's humidity, allowing drying curves from different conditions to be compared universally.

### Step 2 — Assume Exponential Decay

Empirically, thin-layer drying of grains follows exponential decay (Newtonian analogy):

$$
MR = \exp(-k \cdot t)
$$

This is the **Lewis model** (1921). Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln(MR) = -k \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to Page's Equation

Lewis's model often under-fits real cereal drying. Page (1949) modified it with a power-law exponent $n$:

$$
MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Logarithm Twice

$$
\ln(MR) = -k \cdot t^n
$$

Applying ln again to linearize:

$$
\ln[-\ln(MR)] = \ln(k) + n \cdot \ln(t)
$$

This linear form allows $k$ and $n$ to be obtained from experimental data using **linear regression** of $\ln[-\ln(MR)]$ vs $\ln(t)$:

- **Slope** = $n$.
- **Intercept** = $\ln(k)$.

### Step 5 — Compute Drying Rate

Differentiating Page's equation with respect to $t$:

$$
\frac{dMR}{dt} = -k \cdot n \cdot t^{n-1} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

Or equivalently, in terms of moisture content:

$$
R = -\frac{dM}{dt} = (M_0 - M_e) \cdot k \cdot n \cdot t^{n-1} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Paddy is dried from $M_0 = 24\%$ (d.b.) to $M_t = 16\%$ (d.b.) in 2 hours, with $M_e = 6\%$ (d.b.). Page's constant $k = 0.45$ h⁻¹ and $n = 1.1$. Verify the drying behaviour and compute the instantaneous drying rate at $t = 1$ h.

**Step A — Compute Moisture Ratio:**

$$
MR = \frac{16 - 6}{24 - 6} = \frac{10}{18} = 0.5556
$$

**Step B — Predict MR from Page's Equation:**

$$
MR_{\text{pred}} = \exp(-0.45 \times 2^{1.1}) = \exp(-0.45 \times 2.1435) = \exp(-0.9646) = 0.3813
$$

> *Interpretation:* The actual MR (0.5556) is higher than the predicted (0.3813), meaning drying in real conditions is *slower* than Page's prediction — typically because of shrinkage and falling-rate effects.

**Step C — Compute Drying Rate at $t = 1$ h:**

$$
R = (24 - 6) \times 0.45 \times 1.1 \times 1^{0.1} \times \exp(-0.45 \times 1^{1.1})
$$

$$
R = 18 \times 0.495 \times 1 \times \exp(-0.45) = 18 \times 0.495 \times 0.6376 = 5.681 \ \%/\text{h}
$$

**Conclusion:** At 1 hour into drying, paddy is losing moisture at a rate of **5.68 % (d.b.) per hour**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Bucket Elevator Power Requirement

A bucket elevator lifts grain vertically using motor-driven belt or chain. The total power is the sum of: (i) lift power, (ii) friction power, and (iii) idle/no-load power.

### Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate

Let the rated capacity be $Q$ (tonnes/h). Convert to kg/s:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q \times 1000}{3600} \ \ \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Lift (Useful) Power

If lift height is $L$ (m), the useful mechanical power required to lift the grain is:

$$
P_{\text{lift}} = \dot{m} \cdot L \cdot g
$$

Where $g = 9.81$ m/s². Substituting $\dot{m}$:

$$
P_{\text{lift}} = \frac{Q \times 1000}{3600} \times L \times 9.81
$$

### Step 3 — Friction & No-Load Power

Friction loss in bearings, pulleys, and the chain/belt is empirically given by:

$$
P_{\text{friction}} = \eta_{\text{trans}} \cdot P_{\text{lift}}
$$

Where $\eta_{\text{trans}}$ is a transmission factor (typically 1.1–1.3). No-load power for pulley rotation is:

$$
P_{\text{no-load}} = \frac{P_{\text{lift}}}{4}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Power

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{lift}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{no-load}}
$$

A compact empirical form often used in KTU references:

$$
P = 2.5 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot L \cdot g
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

**Problem:** A bucket elevator lifts paddy at $Q = 5$ t/h to a height $L = 12$ m. Calculate the total power required.

**Step A — Mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{5 \times 1000}{3600} = 1.389 \ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step B — Total Power:**

$$
P = 2.5 \times 1.389 \times 12 \times 9.81 = 408.7 \ \text{W} \approx 0.41 \ \text{kW}
$$

> *Conclusion:* A 0.5 kW motor is sufficient. In practice, a **1.5–2.0 kW motor** is selected to allow for startup surge and belt slippage — this is *service factor* engineering.

## 3.4 Derivation — Specific Gravity Table Velocity Field

A **gravity table** uses an inclined, perforated, vibrating deck with upward airflow. Three forces act on each particle: gravity $F_g$, drag from upward airflow $F_d$, and inertial vibration $F_i$.

### Force Balance

$$
F_g = m \cdot g = \rho_p \cdot V_p \cdot g
$$

$$
F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot A_p \cdot v^2
$$

$$
F_i = m \cdot a_v = \rho_p \cdot V_p \cdot a_v
$$

For **stratification** (heavy sinks, light floats):

$$
\rho_{\text{heavy}} > \rho_{\text{critical}} > \rho_{\text{light}}
$$

Where $\rho_{\text{critical}}$ is given by:

$$
\rho_{\text{critical}} = \frac{C_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot V_p \cdot g / A_p}
$$

This is the fundamental *design equation* used to set airflow $v$ for a desired separation density.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Equipment Selection Decision Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Problem | Symptom | Recommended Equipment | Justifying Parameter |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Stone removal from wheat | Stones in finished wheat | **Specific gravity separator** | $\rho_{\text{stone}} = 2600 > \rho_{\text{wheat}} = 1200$ kg/m³ |
| Iron filings in flour | Metal contamination | **Magnetic separator** | Ferrous magnetism |
| Broken rice separation | Broken % > 5% | **Indented cylinder / Disc separator** | Length differential |
| Light chaff removal | Dust in pre-cleaner | **Aspirator (air-classifier)** | $V_t$ of dust < $V_t$ of grain |
| Wilted fruit sorting | Discoloration | **Optical color sorter** | Spectral reflectance |
| Pulse dehulling | Loose hull required | **Rubber-roller dehuller + pitter** | Shear between rollers |
| High moisture paddy | MC > 22% w.b. | **Mixed-flow / LSU dryer** | Continuous, high-throughput |
| Long-term grain preservation (1+ yr) | Storage insects | **Hermetic bags (PICS)** | O₂ < 5%, hermetic seal |
| Cooling freshly dried paddy | High grain temp | **Aeration / tempering bin** | 1–2 CFM/bushel airflow |
| Spice powder making | Need < 100 μm fineness | **Pin mill / Hammer mill + screen** | Impact size reduction |
| Coconuts batch pressing | Small scale | **Ghani / Hydraulic press** | Mechanical cold-press |
| Continuous solvent extraction | Industrial-scale oil | **Solvent extractor (n-hexane)** | Residual oil < 1% |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Post-Harvest Unit-Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Harvested Raw Material] --> B[Receiving and Weighing]
    B --> C[Pre-Cleaning]
    C --> D[Drying]
    D --> E[Cleaning and Grading]
    E --> F[Sorting Optical and Manual]
    F --> G[Storage Silos]
    G --> H[Milling or Processing]
    H --> I[Extraction for Oil Seeds]
    I --> J[Packaging]
    J --> K[Dispatch to Market]

    subgraph Parameters
        P1[Moisture Content]
        P2[Temperature and RH]
        P3[Particle Size]
        P4[Density]
        P5[Throughput]
    end

    P1 -.-> C
    P1 -.-> D
    P2 -.-> D
    P2 -.-> G
    P3 -.-> E
    P4 -.-> E
    P5 -.-> H
```

> *Note:* Dashed arrows represent *governing parameters* flowing into each unit operation. This is the **block-level functional architecture** representation suited to post-harvest engineering.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Air-Screen Cleaner (Most-tested KTU Equipment)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Feed Hopper] --> S1[Coarse Scalping Screen]
    S1 --> S2[Upper Sand Screen]
    S2 --> S3[Lower Grading Screen]
    S3 --> O1[Clean Grain Outlet]
    S1 --> A1[Oversize Trash Outlet]
    S3 --> A2[Throughs Foreign Matter]
    Fan1[Suction Fan] -.Air.-> S2
    Fan2[Exhaust Fan] -.Air.-> S3
    Fan1 -.Dust.-> DC1[Dust Cyclone]
    Fan2 -.Dust.-> DC2[Dust Cyclone]
```

**Working Principle:** Grain falls through three cascaded vibrating screens while cross-air suction lifts light chaff/dust to cyclones. Coarse trash leaves at the top, foreign matter falls through the bottom, and clean grain exits sideways.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Mixed-Flow Grain Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    In[Wheat Paddy or Maize Inlet] --> Bin[Drying Column]
    HV[Heated Air Plenum] -.Alternating Hot and Cold.-> Bin
    Bin --> Cool[Cold Aeration Section]
    Cool --> Out[Dried Grain Outlet]
    Bin -.Recirculation.-> In
    Blower[Centrifugal Blower] --> HV
    Burner[Oil or Biomass Burner] --> HV
```

**Working Principle:** Grain descends by gravity through a vertical column in which alternating rows of hot and ambient air plenums contact the grain. This equalizes moisture across kernels and avoids over-drying in any single layer.

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Specific Gravity Separator (Gravity Table)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed[Grain Feed] --> Deck[Inclined Perforated Deck]
    Blower[Upward Air Stream] -.Lift.-> Deck
    Vib[Motor and Eccentric Vibrator] -.Vibration.-> Deck
    Deck --> Hvy[Heavy Stones and Sound Grain]
    Deck --> Mid[Mixture Recirculation]
    Deck --> Lt[Light Chaff and Immature Grains]
```

**Working Principle:** Vibration stratifies grains by density; upward airflow fluidizes the bed; tilted deck slides fractions into separate discharge chutes.

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Screw Expeller (Oil Extraction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Seed[Oil Seed Feed Hopper] --> Cond[Conditioning Heater 90 to 110 C]
    Cond --> Worm[Worm Shaft inside Barrel Cage]
    Worm --> Cake[Oil Cake Discharge]
    Worm -.Pressurized Oil.-> Tray[Oil Collection Tray]
    Tray --> Filter[Filter Press]
    Filter --> Bottle[Packaged Oil]
```

**Working Principle:** Rotating worm shaft compresses seeds against a choked discharge, rupturing oil cells. Crude oil seeps through barrel cage slots; cake emerges as a continuous plug.

---

## 4.6 Schematic — Hermetic Storage System (PICS Bag)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Outer[Outer Woven Polypropylene] --> L1[Inner Liner HDPE 80 micron]
    L1 --> L2[Second HDPE Liner 80 micron]
    L2 --> Grain[Dry Grain Loaded Inside]
    Grain -.O2 depletion by respiration.-> Insects[Insects Die within 7 Days]
    Insects --> Stable[Stable Storage up to 12 Months]
```

**Working Principle:** Triple-layer hermetic seal cuts off oxygen. Grain and insect respiration rapidly consumes O₂ to < 5%, killing insects and arresting fungal growth without chemicals.

---

## 4.7 Schematic — Overall Material & Energy Flow in a Post-Harvest Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        RM[Raw Material 1 to 50 t per day]
        E[Electricity and Fuel]
    end

    subgraph Unit Operations
        U1[Cleaning]
        U2[Drying]
        U3[Grading]
        U4[Storage]
        U5[Milling]
        U6[Packaging]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        FG[Finished Product]
        W[Waste Chaff Stones]
        V[Vent Moisture and Air]
    end

    RM --> U1 --> U2 --> U3 --> U4 --> U5 --> U6 --> FG
    E --> U2
    E --> U5
    U1 --> W
    U2 --> V
```

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any six important parameters that govern the design of a grain drying system. State one equipment for each parameter category.**

**Model Answer (Mark Distribution):**

- **Parameter 1 — Initial Moisture Content (IMC):** Determines residence time in dryer; equipment: moisture meter (capacitance-type). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Parameter 2 — Air temperature:** Determines drying rate; equipment: thermostat-controlled heater. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Parameter 3 — Air flow rate (m³/min):** Determines moisture removal capacity; equipment: centrifugal blower. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Parameter 4 — Grain temperature:** Limits seed viability (<45 °C); equipment: thermometer / thermocouple. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Parameter 5 — Relative Humidity of drying air:** Determines EMC; equipment: hygrometer. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Parameter 6 — Throughput (kg/h):** Determines dryer capacity; equipment: feed-rate regulator. *(0.5 mark)*

> **Total: 3 marks awarded for naming parameters with matching equipment.**

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**With neat block diagrams, classify the various unit operations involved in post-harvest processing of cereals. For any TWO operations, describe in detail the equipment used, important operating parameters, and the engineering principle involved.**

#### Part (a) — Classification (7 Marks)

The post-harvest unit operations for cereals can be classified into six major categories:

1. **Cleaning** — Removal of foreign matter *(1 mark)*
2. **Sorting and Grading** — Separation by size/quality *(1 mark)*
3. **Drying** — Moisture reduction by heat-mass transfer *(1 mark)*
4. **Storage** — Preservation under controlled environment *(1 mark)*
5. **Milling** — Particle size reduction *(1 mark)*
6. **Material Handling** — Conveyance between operations *(1 mark)*

*A block diagram is drawn showing the sequential flow from harvest → cleaning → drying → grading → storage → milling → packaging.* *(1 mark for diagram)*

#### Part (b) — Detailed Equipment Description (7 Marks)

**Operation 1: DRYING**

- **Principle:** Simultaneous heat and mass transfer; warm air supplies heat for water evaporation and carries moisture away. *(1 mark)*
- **Equipment:** Mixed-flow grain dryer *(1 mark)*
- **Important Parameters:**
  - IMC: 22–24% (w.b.) for paddy
  - FMC: 12–14% (w.b.)
  - Air temperature: 60–80 °C
  - Air flow: 8–12 m³/min per tonne
  - Drying rate (Page's $k$): 0.3–0.6 h⁻¹
  - Grain temperature: < 45 °C *(2 marks)*
- **Page's Thin-Layer Equation:**

$$
MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

*(0.5 mark for formula, 0.5 mark for interpretation)*

**Operation 2: CLEANING**

- **Principle:** Exploitation of differences in size, density, shape, magnetic, and aerodynamic properties. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Equipment:** Air-screen cleaner with scalper + grading screen + aspiration. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Important Parameters:** Screen aperture (mm), air velocity (m/s), feed rate (kg/h), angle of inclination (8°–15°), cleaning efficiency. *(1 mark)*
- **Cleaning Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{cleaning}} = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100
$$

*(0.5 mark)*

> **Total: 14 marks (7 + 7)** — award full marks for systematic coverage of principle, equipment, parameters, and formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain in detail the various types of grain dryers used for cereals and oil seeds. Discuss the parameters affecting drying efficiency and derive Page's thin-layer drying equation.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Dryers (7 Marks)

**1. Sun Drying / Yard Drying** *(0.7 mark)*
- Cheapest, weather-dependent, used for paddy in India.
- Spread grain in 2–5 cm thick layers on cement floor; rake intermittently.
- Energy consumption: 0 MJ/kg (solar-driven).

**2. Flat-Bed Batch Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Grain held on perforated floor; heated air blown from below.
- Batch capacity: 1–4 tonnes.

**3. Columnar / Vertical Batch Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Tall bin with central perforated duct; hot air radiates outward.
- Used for paddy and maize.

**4. LSU (Louisiana State University) Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Recirculating batch; high capacity (5–20 t/batch).
- Operates with hot-air tempering cycles.

**5. Cross-Flow Continuous Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Grain flows perpendicular to hot-air streams.
- Risk of over-drying in layers; not recommended for seed.

**6. Mixed-Flow Continuous Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Alternating hot/cold air columns; uniform moisture.
- Standard for rice mills.

**7. Fluidized-Bed Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Hot air suspends particles; very high heat transfer.
- Used for particulate products like corn grits.

**8. Solar / Cabinet Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Solar energy + auxiliary heater; for high-value produce.

**9. Rotary Drum Dryer** *(0.7 mark)*
- Tumbling action; for granular products.
*(Total: 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Parameters & Page's Derivation (7 Marks)

**Parameters affecting drying efficiency:** *(3 marks)*
- **Initial Moisture Content:** Higher IMC → longer drying time, higher fuel use.
- **Air temperature:** Higher $T_a$ → faster drying (up to a critical limit, after which grain damage occurs).
- **Air Relative Humidity:** Lower RH → higher moisture-carrying capacity → faster drying.
- **Air flow rate:** Higher flow → more moisture carried away → faster drying (until kinetic limit).
- **Grain variety & porosity:** Affects diffusion of internal moisture.
- **Thickness of grain bed:** Thinner bed → faster, more uniform drying.
- **Grain temperature:** Must stay < 45 °C to preserve germination.

**Derivation of Page's Equation:** *(4 marks — step-by-step)*

**Step 1:** Define moisture ratio:

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}
$$

**Step 2:** Assume exponential drying (Lewis 1921):

$$
MR = \exp(-k \cdot t)
$$

**Step 3:** Take ln:

$$
\ln(MR) = -k \cdot t
$$

**Step 4:** Generalize by adding exponent $n$ (Page 1949):

$$
MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

**Step 5:** Take double ln to linearize:

$$
\ln[-\ln(MR)] = \ln(k) + n \cdot \ln(t)
$$

This allows regression analysis for $k$ and $n$.

**Step 6:** Differentiate to get drying rate:

$$
R = -\frac{dM}{dt} = (M_0 - M_e) \cdot k \cdot n \cdot t^{n-1} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t^n)
$$

*(4 marks distributed across derivation steps + 3 marks parameters = 7 marks total)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (where you lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing wet-basis vs dry-basis moisture content** — these are NOT the same; the conversion formula $M_{db} = 100 M_{wb}/(100 - M_{wb})$ MUST be remembered.
> 2. **Listing equipment without parameters** — examiners specifically ask for "parameters AND equipment"; mentioning only one earns partial credit.
> 3. **Naming Page's equation without deriving $n$ and $k$** — at least one derivation step must appear in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Wrong unit for SEC** — Specific Energy Consumption is **kJ/kg water evaporated**, NOT kJ/kg grain.
> 5. **Using throughput as kg/day instead of kg/h** — the KTU standard is kg/h or t/h.
> 6. **Forgetting to state grain-temperature limit** for seed drying (< 45 °C) — common 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Drawing a single rectangular block** as "diagram" — block-level Mermaid flowcharts earn full marks; bare rectangles do NOT.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **unit operation** is a discrete engineering step applied post-harvest to preserve or add value.
- ✅ The universal post-harvest sequence is: **Clean → Dry → Sort/Grade → Store → Mill → Pack**.
- ✅ Cleaning equipment selection is driven by **density, size, magnetic, aerodynamic** properties.
- ✅ **Specific gravity separator** is the most-tested cleaning equipment at KTU.
- ✅ Sorting = *quality-based* separation; Grading = *size-based* separation.
- ✅ **Optical color sorter** = quality; **Indented cylinder / Disc separator** = grading.
- ✅ **Page's thin-layer drying equation** $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ is THE KTU formula for drying.
- ✅ **Drying parameters:** IMC, FMC, $T_a$, RH, airflow, grain temp, $k$, $n$.
- ✅ **Moisture conversions:** $M_{db} = 100 M_{wb}/(100 - M_{wb})$.
- ✅ **Drying rate** $R = -\Delta M/\Delta t$ in **%/h**.
- ✅ **Specific energy consumption** in kJ/kg water evaporated (not kJ/kg grain).
- ✅ **Storage parameters:** moisture, temperature, RH, O₂, CO₂, insect count.
- ✅ **Safe storage moisture** for cereals: **12–14% (w.b.)**.
- ✅ **Hermetic PICS bags** create O₂ < 5% atmosphere, killing insects in 7 days.
- ✅ **Milling reduction ratio** = feed size / product size.
- ✅ **Oil extraction equipment:** Ghani (traditional) → Hydraulic → Screw expeller → Solvent extraction.
- ✅ **Solvent extraction** achieves residual oil < 1%; mechanical pressing leaves 6–8%.
- ✅ **Angle of repose** dictates silo hopper design; wheat ≈ 27°, paddy ≈ 34°.
- ✅ **Bucket elevator power** ≈ $2.5 \dot{m} L g$.
- ✅ **Material handling equipment:** belt conveyor, screw (auger), bucket elevator, pneumatic conveyor.
- ✅ **Grain temperature limit for seed drying** = **< 45 °C**.
- ✅ For seed-grade grain drying → **concurrent-flow or in-bin aeration dryer** (gentle).
- ✅ Always cite **engineering parameter + matching equipment** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always draw a **Mermaid block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it earns 1–2 guaranteed marks.
- ✅ Always **convert moisture to the basis requested** in numerical problems.

---
